posts.artist: Lil Keed, Young Stoner Life, Lil Jairmy
posts.writeup: The Lil Jairmy feature gives this one a different energy than the bigger-name collaborations on the album. Both artists play off each other loosely, no one trying to dominate, the beat carrying them on a wave of 808s and warped melody. It's a deep cut that rewards the listen.

posts.title: Can't Fall Victim (feat. KayCyy)
posts.artist: Lil Keed, Young Stoner Life, KayCyy
posts.writeup: KayCyy has been putting in work in the YSL orbit and Can't Fall Victim makes a case for why. The two vocalists match in register and delivery, trading verses over a beat that keeps the pressure on without ever breaking into chaos. Keed was in a focused headspace on this project and it shows.

posts.title: Think About It
posts.writeup: A more reflective moment from Keed, the production slowing down and opening up into something closer to a slow burn. His melodic approach to rap has always been his strongest suit and Think About It leans on it without apology. Worth revisiting on its own outside of the album context.

posts.title: Go See
posts.writeup: Go See keeps the energy in the low-to-mid range, a groove-based trap record with Keed sounding comfortable in a pocket he carved out for himself. The production is patient, nothing jumps out for the sake of it. Short and clean.

posts.title: All I Wanna Know
posts.artist: Lil Keed, Young Stoner Life, Young Thug
posts.writeup: Young Thug and Keed close this batch with a melodic one that doesn't overstay its welcome. Both artists at their most relaxed, trading lines over a shuffling trap beat that keeps the temperature down. It's an album track that earns its placement.

posts.title: Pit stop
posts.writeup: Bakar trades his usual gritty guitar-driven energy for something more restrained here, floating over a lo-fi beat that barely holds itself together on purpose. The vocal delivery is half-whispered, half-confessional, sitting right in the pocket between indie rock and UK rap the way only he can. It's a pit stop in the truest sense. Brief, deliberate, and just enough fuel to keep you locked in.

posts.title: Forever
posts.writeup: German singer Lotte delivers a lush, synth-washed ballad that leans into Nordic pop sensibilities. The production is spacious, letting her voice carry the emotional weight without drowning it in layers. We've heard a lot of "forever" songs, but the restraint in the arrangement here makes this one stick. Clean songwriting, no filler.

posts.title: Love For You
posts.writeup: Cero Ismael and Elijah Waters link up for a sun-soaked R&B cut built on soft guitar plucks and a rhythm section that grooves without trying too hard. The vocal interplay between the two is effortless, trading lines over a production that feels like a late-night drive with the windows down. If you're into Daniel Caesar or Emotional Oranges, this is right up your alley.

posts.title: Fatal Attraction
posts.writeup: 6LACK opens his Since I Have A Lover era with this moody, minimal R&B track that strips everything back to voice and atmosphere. The production barely breathes, and that's the point. He sounds more settled than we've heard him in years, leaning into vulnerability without the usual walls up. Quietly devastating.

posts.title: always be a fan
posts.writeup: Eva Grace has a voice that sits somewhere between bedroom pop intimacy and full-blown pop polish. "always be a fan" is built on airy synths and a gentle pulse that never rushes, letting the lyrics about devotion and admiration land with weight. There's a Clairo-adjacent softness here, but the songwriting has its own identity. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Love Yourself
posts.writeup: Nino SLG brings a smooth, piano-led R&B track with a vocal performance that's all warmth. The production is minimal but intentional, giving the melody room to breathe over soft percussion and ambient textures. It's the kind of song that sounds better at 1 AM with the lights low. Self-love as a late-night mood.

posts.title: Bulletproof
posts.writeup: BERWYN continues to blur the lines between spoken word, rap, and raw emotion over production that feels like it was built in a church basement. "Bulletproof" is heavy in the best way, with layered vocals and a beat that pulses underneath his stream-of-consciousness delivery. The Trinidad-born, London-raised artist doesn't make easy music, and that's exactly why it hits. We've been watching this one for a while.

posts.title: Talkback
posts.writeup: More from 6LACK's pre-album rollout, and "Talkback" finds him in a slightly more animated pocket than "Fatal Attraction." The beat knocks a little harder, with warped synths and a bounce that gives him room to flex his melodic range. Still introspective, but there's an edge here. He's working through something and letting us listen.

posts.title: Supercharged
posts.writeup: Daniel Allan and Reo Cragun team up for a high-energy electronic pop cut that pushes both artists out of their usual lanes. Allan's production is bright and propulsive, built on stacked synths and a drop that actually earns its payoff. Cragun rides the beat with a delivery that's part rap, part sung hook. Web3 music scene producing real records.

posts.title: Ogogoro (with Bas & Ayra Starr)
posts.writeup: Bas and Ayra Starr connect over a bouncy Afrobeats-leaning production on this Dreamville compilation cut. The beat is percussive and warm, with Ayra Starr's vocals adding a brightness that contrasts Bas's laid-back flow perfectly. It's a cultural bridge track done right, no gimmicks. The kind of song that moves from a Lagos playlist to a Brooklyn rooftop without missing a beat.

posts.title: Tormenta (feat. Bad Bunny)
posts.writeup: Damon Albarn and Bad Bunny is a combination nobody predicted but somehow makes total sense. The Gorillaz production is characteristically off-kilter, built on swirling synths and a rhythm that pulls from reggaeton without ever fully committing. Bad Bunny floats through it with ease, and the result feels like a tropical storm filtered through a cartoon universe. Genuinely one of the more interesting genre collisions we've heard this year.

posts.title: Since I Have A Lover
posts.writeup: The title track from 6LACK's third album is his most tender, fully realized moment to date. Warm pads, a soft drum pattern, and a vocal that sounds genuinely content rather than searching. After years of navigating heartbreak and isolation in his music, hearing him arrive somewhere peaceful is striking. It's not flashy. It doesn't need to be.

posts.title: Falling
posts.writeup: slowthai pulls back from the chaos for "Falling," delivering a surprisingly stripped-down, emotional cut. The production is sparse, letting his voice carry the weight of the confession. It's a side of him that's always been there underneath the punk energy, just rarely this exposed. Raw and honest.

posts.title: HAPPY
posts.writeup: "HAPPY" finds slowthai leaning into an almost Britpop energy, with bright guitars and a beat that bounces with genuine optimism. It's a sharp turn from his more aggressive output, and the sincerity in the delivery sells it. The Northampton rapper sounds like he's actually found something worth holding onto. Good for him.

posts.title: Never Again
posts.writeup: slowthai gets introspective over a murky, bass-heavy production that feels like late-night London. The delivery is measured, almost conversational, working through regret without melodrama. There's a grime-adjacent edge to the beat, but the emotional weight keeps it grounded. He's at his best when he's this honest.

posts.title: Feel Good
posts.writeup: The title says it all. "Feel Good" is slowthai at his most accessible, riding a warm, funk-inflected beat with a vocal that's equal parts celebratory and reflective. The production borrows from '90s house and UK garage without feeling derivative. It's the kind of track that works at a festival or in your headphones walking home. Versatile record.

posts.title: Encouragement
posts.writeup: Don Toliver's Love Sick era is all atmosphere, and "Encouragement" is the album's emotional center. Soft keys, a muted 808 pattern, and his signature falsetto create something that feels weightless. The production is pristine without being sterile. It's Houston psychedelia at its most refined.

posts.title: Private Landing (feat. Justin Bieber & Future)
posts.writeup: Three very different artists collide on "Private Landing" and the result is smoother than it has any right to be. Don Toliver sets the vibe, Bieber matches the energy with one of his better recent verses, and Future slides in like he's been making this kind of music his whole career. The beat is all atmosphere, synth pads layered over a slow-rolling 808. Premium late-night material.

posts.title: Honeymoon
posts.writeup: "Honeymoon" is Don Toliver doing what he does best: turning a simple melody into an entire world. The production is lush and hazy, built on reverb-soaked guitars and a beat that barely touches the ground. His voice weaves through it like smoke. Three minutes, no wasted space.

posts.title: Let Her Go (feat. James Blake)
posts.writeup: Don Toliver and James Blake is a pairing that makes almost too much sense. Blake's glitchy, deconstructed production meets Toliver's airy vocals, and the two create something that lives in the space between R&B and electronic art pop. It's haunting without being cold, emotional without being heavy-handed. One of the standout collaborations on Love Sick.

posts.title: LoveSickness
posts.writeup: The title track from Don Toliver's debut era still holds weight. "LoveSickness" is built on warped synths and a beat that stutters in all the right places, with his vocal floating above it all like he's narrating a dream. The production has a Cudi-esque quality to it, spacey and introspective. It's the blueprint for everything that came after.

posts.title: Embarrassed (feat. Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: Don Toliver and Travis Scott are a proven combination at this point, and "Embarrassed" doesn't disappoint. The beat is dark and swirling, with Scott's ad-libs adding texture to Toliver's melodic framework. It's Cactus Jack family music at its most cohesive, both artists feeding off each other's energy. The production knocks without ever feeling forced.

posts.title: COCKPIT (feat. spill tab, binki & Harve)
posts.artist: Billy Lemos, binki, spill tab, Harve
posts.writeup: Billy Lemos assembles a murderers' row of indie-adjacent talent for "COCKPIT" and lets them all eat. spill tab, binki, and Harve each bring a distinct flavor over Lemos's glitchy, synth-forward production. The track shifts shape every 30 seconds but never loses its groove. It's organized chaos in the best way, the kind of collaborative energy that makes you want to see all four of them in a room together.

posts.title: Are You Okay?
posts.writeup: BEL asks a simple question and builds an entire sonic world around it. "Are You Okay?" is tender, synth-driven pop with a vocal that trembles just enough to feel lived-in. The production is spacious and warm, never overcrowding the emotion. We don't know much about BEL yet, but this is a strong introduction.

posts.title: Kant Nobody (feat. DMX)
posts.writeup: Wayne and DMX on the same track is a legacy event, full stop. The production is gritty and boom-bap inspired, giving both legends room to do what they do. Wayne's wordplay is as sharp as ever, and hearing DMX's voice posthumously still carries undeniable weight. It's a reminder of the era these two dominated, delivered with the respect it deserves.

posts.title: I GUESS IT'S LOVE?
posts.writeup: LAROI leans into something more tender here, a looping guitar figure carrying most of the emotional weight while his vocal sits right in the pocket between pop and confessional. The production is sparse in a deliberate way, giving the uncertainty in the title room to breathe. We've watched him chase hooks for years; this one feels like he actually meant it.

posts.title: One Of Those Days (feat. Lil Yachty & with 347aidan)
posts.writeup: Zack Bia keeps his curatorial instincts intact on this one, threading Yachty and 347aidan through a hazy late-night groove that doesn't try too hard. The tempo stays comfortable, the textures stay warm. It's the kind of track that plays well at 2 AM when nothing needs to be proven.

posts.title: HIATUS
posts.writeup: Birmingham's Wesley Joseph continues to build one of the more interesting catalogs in UK alternative music, and HIATUS slots right into that lineage. The track moves slow and deliberate, production that pulls from soul and neo-psych without fully committing to either. His voice carries a quiet authority that makes the restraint feel earned.

posts.title: MONSOON
posts.writeup: Layered and textured in ways that reward headphone listening. MONSOON has a cinematic quality to it, Wesley Joseph constructing atmosphere before melody, pulling you into the mood before the song fully arrives. Keep an ear on where he goes next.

posts.title: SUGAR DIVE
posts.writeup: SUGAR DIVE brings DEAN into the fold and the pairing clicks. The track floats between genres on purpose, R&B bones under production that has more in common with indie bedroom pop. Both artists sound comfortable being uncomfortable with easy categorization.

posts.title: Smoke
posts.writeup: Smoke is classic Polachek in its architecture: a simple emotional core expanded outward through production choices that shouldn't work together but absolutely do. The vocal control here is something else, moving from conversational to operatic in the space of a single line. Desire, I Want to Turn Into You was already a landmark; this confirmed what we already suspected.

posts.title: Afford
posts.writeup: Portland's The Last Artful, Dodgr has a conversational flow that sidesteps hip-hop convention without losing any of its punch. Afford sits in that space between spoken word and rap, where the rhythm is the argument and every word has somewhere to be. Worth your full attention.

posts.title: free flow
posts.writeup: Exactly what the title suggests. Kamal. rides a liquid jazz-inflected beat with the kind of ease that sounds effortless and isn't. The production keeps moving, keeps shifting, and the vocal matches it without breaking a sweat. A name to track.

posts.title: the ride-
posts.writeup: From Let's Start Here, Yachty's full psych-rock pivot, and the ride- captures everything that album got right. Woozy guitars, a vocal that sounds genuinely unbothered, production built on texture over structure. It was a real left turn for him, and it worked.

posts.title: the BLACK seminole.
posts.writeup: Diana Gordon is the anchor on this one, her voice cutting through Yachty's haze with something more grounded and soulful. The contrast is the whole point. One of the stronger collaborations on Let's Start Here, and that's saying something given the company the album keeps.

posts.title: Race
posts.writeup: Oddisee has always operated at the intersection of jazz-rap and social commentary without letting either pull too hard. Race moves efficiently, the production giving the words room to land. He's one of the most consistently sharp voices in independent hip-hop, and this is why.

posts.title: Already Knew
posts.writeup: The groove on Already Knew is the kind of thing that sounds simple and isn't. Oddisee builds the beat around a warmth that most producers don't reach for anymore, keeping the focus on the lyric without ever pulling attention away from how good the track feels.

posts.title: How Far
posts.writeup: Reflective without being heavy. How Far is Oddisee at his most precise, the words landing cleanly over production that has a late-afternoon quality to it. DC's influence on his sound shows up in the rhythm section, that mid-Atlantic hip-hop pocket that goes deeper than most.

posts.title: The Start of Something
posts.writeup: A good entry point if you're new to him. The Start of Something has an openness to it, production that moves forward without rushing, and a lyric that sits with possibility instead of resolution. Exactly what the title promises.

posts.title: You Laughed at Me
posts.writeup: SAINt JHN writes from a place of earned frustration, and You Laughed at Me is that energy compressed into a tight, punchy track. The production has an edge that matches the subject matter, and his delivery doesn't oversell it. When it clicks, it clicks.

posts.title: Haven
posts.writeup: RIZ LA VIE builds space into everything. Haven is a slow exhale of a track, production that prioritizes room and warmth over anything aggressive or immediate. His voice sits low in the mix in a way that feels intentional, like the song is something you're meant to lean into rather than be hit by.

posts.title: Long Way to Go
posts.writeup: A reminder of what Lil Keed could do when the production matched his energy. Long Way to Go has a melodic confidence that felt like it was just getting started. We lost him too soon.

posts.title: Honeydew (02)
posts.writeup: Isaia Huron writes R&B with a lightness that doesn't compromise its depth. Honeydew (02) moves on a gentle pulse, his voice carrying warmth without overselling anything. The production stays out of the way and lets the song breathe. Right up your alley if you've been sleeping on him.

posts.title: Simp
posts.writeup: Col3trane is one of the more interesting figures coming out of the UK R&B scene, and Simp shows why. The track has a confident ease to it, production that sits between neo-soul and something more contemporary, and a vocal that never pushes harder than the song needs.

posts.title: Good News
posts.writeup: Bakar keeps it warm and direct. Good News has an alt-soul quality, guitar-forward without going full indie, the production giving his voice exactly the kind of frame it needs. He's been building toward something, and tracks like this make the wait worthwhile.

posts.title: Ma Boy
posts.writeup: JID and Lute are both artists who make hard work sound natural, and Ma Boy is a showcase for exactly that. The Dreamville tape energy is all over this one, two sharp lyricists playing off a beat that has enough room for both of them without feeling crowded.

posts.title: LOVE AGAIN
posts.writeup: LOVE AGAIN leans into LAROI's pop instincts without losing the emotional directness that made him worth watching in the first place. Propulsive production, a hook built for volume, and a vocal that knows exactly where it needs to go. He's gotten sharper.

posts.title: The Alchemist.
posts.writeup: From the same psych-pop territory as the rest of Let's Start Here, but The Alchemist. has a particular looseness to it. The title is apt: Yachty is mixing elements that shouldn't combine, and somehow the formula holds. Production from his collaborators on the album that sounds like nothing else in his catalog.

posts.title: sHouLd i B?
posts.writeup: The existential weight in the title doesn't fully arrive in the music, and that's the point. sHouLd i B? floats, uncertain in a pleasant way, the production cushioning any sharp edges. Yachty in full psych mode, questioning everything while the guitars hum underneath.

posts.title: sAy sOMETHINg
posts.writeup: A standout from Let's Start Here that captures the album's central trick: making psychedelic pop feel intimate instead of overwhelming. sAy sOMETHINg has a softness to it, the production pulling back just enough that the emotional content comes through clean. We've been watching Yachty build toward this for years.

posts.title: Never older
posts.artist: Jonah Zed, Yukon Kash, luggish, poobon
posts.writeup: Jonah Zed links up with Yukon Kash, luggish, and poobon for something quietly heavy. The production sits in that hazy lo-fi pocket where nostalgia and melancholy share the same room, and the vocal layers keep pulling you back in. It's the kind of track that doesn't announce itself, just settles in.

posts.title: Only One
posts.writeup: Daniel OG keeps it stripped and direct on this one. The production is minimal, warm, and gives the vocal plenty of room to breathe. We'll be keeping an ear on where this goes.

posts.title: Selfish
posts.writeup: slowthai slows the pulse here, trading the chaos of his early work for something more measured and exposed. The production has a haunted quality, layered synths that feel uncertain on purpose. He's working through something real, and it shows.

posts.title: Diamonds
posts.writeup: Bas has always been the thoughtful one in Dreamville's orbit, and 'Diamonds' stays in that lane. The beat is spacious and patient, letting his cadence dictate the tempo. Reflective, careful, never in a rush.

posts.title: Lifeline
posts.writeup: Lapsley built her name on voice-as-instrument records, and 'Lifeline' fits that lineage. The production swells around her vocal without drowning it, keeping that delicate tension she does better than most. Right up your alley if you're into that post-FKA Twigs emotional space.

posts.title: ShoulderBlades
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim brings a quietly physical energy to this one, the track built around rhythm and texture more than melody. It crawls and locks in, and the title makes sense by the end. One to sit with.

posts.title: Qtip
posts.writeup: Namou's 'Qtip' is blunted in the best way, a slow-rolling beat with a hook that sticks without trying. The production has character and the delivery matches it. Underrated and easy to loop.

posts.title: true
posts.writeup: JAWNY's been doing this guitar-pop-meets-bedroom-soul thing since his early SoundCloud days, and 'true' is another clean entry. The chord progression has a classic ache to it and the production stays light on its feet. Catchy without being cheap.

posts.title: King Snipe
posts.writeup: Gucci and Kodak together is exactly what it sounds like, two Atlanta heavyweights trading bars over a beat that hits hard and stays cold. No surprises here, just two guys very good at what they do.

posts.title: Moment
posts.writeup: Glasgow's Joesef writes heartbreak like he's lived in it long enough to redecorate. 'Moment' from his debut 'Permanent Damage' is slow-burning and piano-anchored, the kind of track that creeps up on you mid-verse. The vocal control here is understated and completely devastating.

posts.title: H e r O l d F r i e n d s
posts.writeup: PND does the most by doing the least. The production on this one is midnight-quiet, just enough to hold space for lyrics about the kind of closeness that doesn't have a clean name. His delivery hasn't lost a step.

posts.title: essential
posts.writeup: Kamal. is working in a space between ambient R&B and something harder to categorize, and 'essential' leans into that ambiguity. The production shimmers without rushing, and the vocal sits just far enough back to feel submerged. We've got our ear on this one.

posts.title: Good For Now (feat. Lucky Daye)
posts.writeup: Chiiild and Lucky Daye are two of the most texturally interesting voices in neo-soul right now, and putting them together was an obvious but still satisfying move. The production has that late-70s AM radio warmth filtered through a more contemporary lens. Both of them know exactly when to step back and let the song breathe.

posts.title: Traumatised
posts.writeup: UK drill with some emotional heft behind it. Clavish uses the template without being trapped by it, the production crisp and cold, the content more introspective than the genre usually allows. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: We Caa Done (feat. Drake)
posts.writeup: Popcaan has always had Drake's ear for good reason, and this collab proves the chemistry hasn't faded. The dancehall bounce is built for movement, and Drake takes the feature seriously rather than phoning it in. A summer track that found us in winter.

posts.title: In2you
posts.writeup: Downtown Kayoto keeps the energy high and the stakes low on 'In2you,' a bright electro-pop track that knows exactly what it is. The production is glossy and propulsive, and the hook earns its repetition. Pure momentum.

posts.title: What's Your Poison?
posts.writeup: London's Finn Foxell is one of those artists who can shift registers without losing the thread. 'What's Your Poison?' sits somewhere between indie pop and piano-driven singer-songwriter, the production subtle enough to let the lyrical wit land clean. Sharp and worth your time.

BERWYN has been one of the most raw and unguarded voices to come out of the UK in years, and '3450' strips things down even further. The production is skeletal and intentional, giving his vocal nowhere to hide. That's the point.

posts.title: 2daside
posts.writeup: redveil is still in his early 20s and already writing with a maturity that most artists spend a decade chasing. The production is hazy and layered, the kind of beat that sounds like a city at 3 AM. His pen stays sharp.

posts.title: ‎vacation
posts.writeup: maxime. keeps things small and intimate on this one, the production barely above a whisper and the vocal sitting right on top. There's a delicate sadness to it that doesn't ask for too much of your attention, just enough.

posts.title: Tell Me It's Over (feat. Summer Walker & 6LACK)
posts.writeup: Three of contemporary R&B's most distinctive voices on one track, and somehow they all get room. Summer Walker and 6LACK bring the emotional weight while Jacquees handles the melodic center. Production by a committee that actually listened to each other.

posts.title: Sex In The Porsche (and PARTYNEXTDOOR)
posts.writeup: PND's fingerprints are all over the production here, that signature oceanic R&B atmosphere he's been refining since PARTYNEXTDOOR 2. Diddy benefits from the sonic real estate and doesn't overstay his welcome. A clean collab that plays to both their strengths.

posts.title: Face 2 Face
posts.writeup: Posthumous Juice WRLD releases are always a complicated listen, but 'Face 2 Face' holds up on its own terms. The production has that melodic trap skeleton he worked best over, and the emotion in the vocal is unforced. We're glad this one made it out.

posts.title: Broken
posts.writeup: Little Simz closed 2022 with 'NO THANK YOU,' a record that didn't announce itself so much as demand you recalibrate. 'Broken' is one of its most direct moments, the production leaving room for her voice to do the heavy lifting. She's operating at a level most rappers won't reach.

posts.title: No Merci
posts.writeup: 'No Merci' is Simz at her most unapologetic, the production hard and relentless and the bars even more so. From the same 'NO THANK YOU' session, it shows just how wide her range is across a single project. No warmup, no filler.

posts.title: Far
posts.writeup: SZA stretches into something quieter and more aching on "Far," one of the emotional center cuts from SOS. The production peels back to give her vocal room to breathe, sparse guitar threading underneath while she navigates distance in a relationship that's still technically alive. It hits differently after the more confrontational moments on the record. She's not yelling. She's just tired.

posts.title: Smoking on my Ex Pack
posts.writeup: Short, mean, and perfectly calibrated. SZA built this one around a throwaway premise and turned it into a mood. The beat is minimal and slightly off-kilter, making space for her to be petty in the most relatable way possible. Two minutes of exactly what it needed to be, nothing more.

posts.title: Notice Me
posts.writeup: "Notice Me" sits in that SZA sweet spot where the production sounds breezy but the lyrics are quietly devastating. The track carries a familiar tension between wanting attention and knowing you deserve better than the person you want it from. Her vocal delivery makes it feel offhand, which only makes it cut deeper.

posts.title: It Was Nice To See You
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim navigates the awkward aftermath of a situationship with a voice that's almost too calm for the subject matter. No preview available to dig into the sonics, but the title alone says plenty. One to circle back on.

posts.title: Conceited
posts.writeup: One of the more sonically interesting moments on SOS. "Conceited" runs a vocal loop that feels almost hypnotic, SZA leaning into a slightly detached, self-aware posture that suits the lyric. The production has a weightlessness to it that makes the confidence feel earned rather than performed.

posts.title: Skinny Ape
posts.writeup: Gorillaz dropped this as a standalone in late 2022, and it has the loose, world-worn energy of Damon Albarn working through something in real time. Understated production, a melody that feels both familiar and slightly sad. It doesn't need to be flashy. It just needs to land, and it does.

posts.title: Quickie
posts.writeup: Moneybagg Yo keeps it exactly as advertised. The beat is hard and minimal, his cadence casual and deliberate, letting the subject matter carry the track without over-explaining. Memphis through and through.

posts.title: Welcome To My Island
posts.writeup: Caroline Polachek at peak Caroline Polachek. The production on "Welcome To My Island" is sprawling and cinematic, with her voice doing things that feel physically impossible. She's been operating on a different frequency from most pop music for years, and this track is a clean entry point into that world. Breathtaking and genuinely strange in the best way.

posts.title: Solitude (with BIA & Lucky Daye)
posts.writeup: Snakehips pull in BIA and Lucky Daye for a smooth, slightly melancholy cut that works better than it has any right to. Lucky Daye brings the weight, BIA keeps it grounded, and the production walks that line between club-ready and late-night introspective. Solid piece of work.

posts.title: All The Money (with Gunna) [Bonus]
posts.writeup: Metro Boomin and Gunna find a comfortable pocket together on this Heroes and Villains bonus cut. The beat is lush without being busy, giving Gunna's melodic flow space to settle in. Doesn't break new ground, but both artists operating at their natural register is enough.

posts.title: Around Me (feat. Don Toliver)
posts.writeup: Don Toliver has been one of the most reliable collaborators in this lane for a reason. "Around Me" leans into his spacey, melodic approach over a Metro beat that has more texture than it first appears. The production swells in the right places without telegraphing it.

posts.title: Trance (with Travis Scott & Young Thug)
posts.artist: Metro Boomin, Travis Scott, Young Thug
posts.writeup: Metro stacks Travis Scott and Young Thug on a beat that feels built for 2 AM on a highway with no destination. Travis is in full Astroworld mode, Thug floats through like it's effortless, and the production keeps the whole thing slightly suspended. One of the stronger three-way collaborations Metro has pulled off.

posts.title: Too Many Nights (feat. Don Toliver & with Future)
posts.writeup: Future and Don Toliver are a pairing that makes sense the moment you hear it. "Too Many Nights" is moody and slow-burning, the kind of song that lives in the hours between midnight and whenever you finally sleep. Metro's beat has a melancholy undertow that both artists lean into without pushing.

posts.title: Superhero (Heroes & Villains) [with Future & Chris Brown]
posts.writeup: The lead single from Heroes and Villains carried a lot of weight and mostly delivered. Future and Chris Brown occupy different corners of the song without ever feeling disconnected, and Metro's production has a cinematic scale that earned it the placement it got. The Marvel tie-in was almost beside the point.

posts.title: Shittin' Me
posts.writeup: A$AP Rocky brought Rod Stewart in to clear a sample and built something genuinely unexpected around it. The beat is dusty and unhurried, Rocky's rhyme patterns looser than usual in a way that works. It doesn't sound like anything else he's released, which is the point. Keep an ear on where he takes this energy.

posts.title: Creepin' (with The Weeknd & 21 Savage)
posts.writeup: One of the standout moments from Heroes and Villains. The Weeknd's falsetto against Metro's dark, cavernous production hits a specific emotional frequency, and 21 Savage's feature slot is perfectly placed. This one caught a second life when the Rodriguez sample got people paying attention to the original. Both versions deserved the attention.

posts.title: This Is What I Mean
posts.artist: Stormzy, Black Sherif, Amaarae, Ms Banks, STORRY
posts.writeup: Stormzy assembled something genuinely moving here. "This Is What I Mean" pulls in Black Sherif, Amaarae, Ms Banks, and STORRY across a sweeping, emotional production that feels like a mission statement rather than a feature showcase. Each voice earns its place. Stormzy clearly had something to prove on this one, and he did.

posts.title: AIR
posts.writeup: Sylo keeps the sonic palette minimal on "AIR," building atmosphere out of restraint. The track breathes in a way that a lot of production in this space doesn't bother with. Not much context available on this one yet, but it sounds like someone with a clear idea of what they're after.

posts.title: Fade Away
posts.writeup: Roddy Ricch has been quietly stacking cuts on Feed Tha Streets III that remind you why he broke through in the first place. "Fade Away" has the introspective weight he does best, production that gives him room without being sparse, a melody that sticks. The Compton roots are never far from the surface here.

posts.title: NEW SHOES
posts.writeup: BROCKHAMPTON landing on something warm and self-reflective for TM. "NEW SHOES" has a looseness to it that feels earned after years of the collective working through bigger and messier ideas. The production is bright without being saccharine, and the energy suggests a group comfortable enough to exhale.

posts.title: Do It Right
posts.writeup: Don Toliver solo and locked in. The production on "Do It Right" has that Houston lean, deliberate and melodic, with Toliver's voice doing that half-sung thing he does when a track is sitting exactly where he wants it. Short, focused, exactly right.

posts.title: 37th
posts.writeup: No preview, but "37th" from The Family album carries the elegiac quality that ran through a lot of BROCKHAMPTON's late output. The title references Ameer Vann's address in a complicated chapter of the group's history. Worth sitting with.

posts.title: you've changed your mind
posts.writeup: Dutch producer Duskus works in a lane that sits between future bass and something quieter and more personal. "you've changed your mind" has the melodic lift his best work carries, built around a vocal hook that feels both light and a little heartbroken. Good late-night music.

posts.title: Twin (feat. Lil Durk)
posts.writeup: Roddy and Durk make sense together. Both carry a quiet-storm energy in their delivery, and "Twin" leans into that overlap without forcing it. The beat has room, both artists have space, and the back-and-forth feels organic rather than assembled.

posts.title: Get Light
posts.writeup: Nas wrapping up King's Disease III with the kind of track that reminds you what longevity sounds like. "Get Light" has Hit-Boy's fingerprints on a beat that's clean and confident, and Nas sounds like someone who stopped needing to prove anything a long time ago. That comfort suits him.

posts.title: Thun
posts.writeup: Nas on "Thun" is still operating at a level most rappers can't touch. King's Disease III keeps the NASIR tradition of lush, jazz-inflected production with Nas threading bars through the pocket like he's never missed a step. Hit-Boy builds a bed of warm keys and understated drums, letting the words carry the weight. The chemistry between these two has become one of the most reliable things in rap.

posts.title: Legit
posts.writeup: "Legit" leans harder, Hit-Boy flipping a choppier, more percussive canvas that pulls Nas toward something with more edge. Still QB through and through, but the production has a restlessness that keeps it from feeling comfortable. Short runtime, no wasted motion.

posts.title: Give You What You Want
posts.writeup: Lil Tjay working in his sweet spot: melodic hooks, airy production, the kind of track built for late-night streaming. The Bronx rapper has a real ear for emotional contrast, pairing understated delivery with beats that breathe. This one sits somewhere between ambition and ease.

posts.title: Middle of the Ocean
posts.writeup: From Her Loss, this is Drake at his most emotionally exposed, the production stripped to rippling piano and low-end that feels like it's coming from underwater. The introspection here feels less calculated than his usual fare. One of the album's quieter gut-punches.

posts.title: Jumbotron Shit Poppin
posts.writeup: Big room energy from the jump. "Jumbotron Shit Poppin" is Drake in celebration mode, the beat engineered for arenas and highlight reels. Crowd-tested from day one, no surprises, does exactly what it promises.

posts.title: Circo Loco
posts.writeup: Drake and 21 Savage trading over a beat that borrows from the gritty Atlanta low-end school. The contrast between their deliveries is the whole point: one cool and controlled, the other economical and flat-affect. From Her Loss, one of the harder-edged moments on the project.

posts.title: Broke Boys
posts.writeup: "Broke Boys" channels classic Toronto bravado, Drake and 21 Savage leaning into wealth flexing over a moody, bass-heavy production. The hook lands more than the verses, but the energy is consistent throughout.

posts.title: Hours In Silence
posts.writeup: The introspective cut from Her Loss. Production pulls back to let Drake sit with his thoughts longer than he usually does. Sparse, dark, and a little unresolved by design. The silence between the bars is doing as much work as the words.

posts.title: Privileged Rappers
posts.writeup: One of the sharper cuts from Her Loss. Drake and 21 Savage on competitive mode, the beat hard-edged and confident, both artists leaning into status and skill simultaneously. 21's cameo in particular lands with weight.

posts.title: BackOutsideBoyz
posts.writeup: Post-pandemic release energy fully channeled here. "BackOutsideBoyz" is the anthem version of everything Drake had stored up, the production wide and triumphant. The title says everything about the vibe.

posts.title: Baby Queen
posts.writeup: Gorillaz pulling from a cleaner, more euphoric register on "Baby Queen." Damon Albarn's production tilts toward danceable, with light percussion and a chorus that opens up into something unexpectedly sweet. Cracker Island era material that leans into the pop instincts without losing the texture.

posts.title: Pussy & Millions (feat. Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: Travis Scott's presence on this one gives it a different atmosphere than the rest of Her Loss, his melodic sensibility cutting against the harder production underneath. Drake and 21 set the tone but Travis shifts the register mid-track. One of the more interesting structural moments on the album.

posts.title: Treacherous Twins
posts.writeup: The title says it: Drake and 21 Savage functioning as a unit, the production minimal and confident, letting the tag-team dynamic breathe. There's an ease to their back-and-forth that suggests genuine chemistry, not just proximity.

posts.title: Spin Bout U
posts.writeup: One of the more radio-ready moments from Her Loss, "Spin Bout U" leans melodic with a hook designed for replay. 21 Savage on the chorus is a creative choice that pays off. Clean, efficient, does its job.

posts.title: On BS
posts.writeup: "On BS" opens with a harder edge than most of Her Loss, the beat aggressive and the bars more declarative. Drake switching registers to match 21 Savage's world. From a different angle than his comfort zone, which makes it worth noting.

posts.title: Major Distribution
posts.writeup: "Major Distribution" closes with an understanding of scale. The beat is deliberate, cinematic in the way Atlanta trap production goes cinematic, and both artists sound like they're announcing something. Opener energy even when it's not the opener.

posts.title: Rich Flex
posts.writeup: "Rich Flex" became the moment from Her Loss, 21 Savage's repeated tag turning into a cultural shorthand almost immediately. The production is spare and cold, the chemistry between the two effortless. Sometimes simple execution on a simple idea is exactly the right call.

posts.title: Curtains
posts.writeup: Smino from Luv 4 Rent, and the St. Louis rapper is fully in his bag here. "Curtains" drifts between R&B and jazz-rap in the way only Smino can manage without it feeling forced. The production is warm and lived-in, his melodic phrasing doing things most vocalists can't map out. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Garden Lady
posts.writeup: "Garden Lady" takes the Luv 4 Rent looseness and runs it somewhere tender. Smino's writing here is specific in that understated way, domestic imagery that doesn't sentimentalize. The production sits back and lets the performance lead.

posts.title: All I Never Wanted
posts.writeup: Zzz. working in the space between ambient and song, "All I Never Wanted" heavy with texture and restraint. Production that trusts negative space, letting mood settle before the melodic elements surface. Worth a patient listen.

posts.title: INTERTWINED
posts.writeup: Agung Mango and A81yann building something soft and deliberate on "INTERTWINED." The production floats, R&B instincts pushed toward something hazier and more atmospheric. A find from the discovery end of the playlist.

posts.title: Paranoia
posts.writeup: Que Tha Poet with Vory on the assist, "Paranoia" trading in that moody, introspective lane where bars and melody blur. Vory's presence adds emotional texture to the production's harder edges. Worth tracking if you're not already watching Que.

posts.title: Don't Take Your Love
posts.writeup: dvsn doing what dvsn does, which is make R&B that feels patient and considered in a streaming climate that rarely rewards either. "Don't Take Your Love" rides a smooth production bed with vocals that don't push past what the song needs. OVO imprint, consistently reliable.

posts.title: Aston Martin Truck
posts.writeup: Roddy Ricch in flex mode, the production as smooth and high-gloss as the title suggests. "Aston Martin Truck" sits in the lane he carved with "The Box" but with less urgency, more cruise control. Compton rap that sounds like success.

posts.title: Matinee
posts.writeup: "Matinee" with Kal Banx closes the Smino run in this batch with a gentle, groove-forward cut. The collaboration feels natural, both artists keeping the energy relaxed without going slack. Luv 4 Rent is one of the better R&B projects in this era and cuts like this are why.

posts.title: Hide & Seek
posts.writeup: Stormzy goes somewhere quieter here, stripping back the grime bravado for something that actually aches. "Hide & Seek" rides a sparse piano loop and a vocal performance that earns every second of its runtime. He's not performing grief, he's sitting in it. Keep an ear on what happens when one of the genre's biggest voices decides to get still.

posts.title: Human Too
posts.writeup: The 1975 have always done their best work when they stop trying to be cool and just feel things out loud. "Human Too" lands in that zone: synth-washed, mid-tempo, Healy's vocals front and center without the usual layers of irony insulating everything. Production is warm and deliberate. It's the kind of track that sneaks up on you.

posts.title: Double Down
posts.writeup: Baby runs through "Double Down" like he's got somewhere to be, clipped Atlanta cadence over a beat that rumbles low and stays out of the way. The confidence here isn't loud, it's just settled. Quick hit, no wasted bars.

posts.title: Forever (feat. Fridayy)
posts.writeup: Fridayy's hook is the anchor, melodic and patient against Baby's locked-in verse work. "Forever" has a road-trip feel to it, the kind of track that earns steady rotation without making a big entrance. Production breathes a little more than usual, and both artists find their lane without bumping into each other.

posts.title: Never Hating (feat. Young Thug)
posts.writeup: Two Atlanta institutions trading space on a beat that keeps it lean and functional. Young Thug's contribution is characteristically untethered, melismatic, doing things with a line that nobody else would think to try. Baby holds the center. This one's for the faithful.

posts.title: California Breeze
posts.writeup: "California Breeze" relaxes the usual Atlanta tautness into something looser and sun-warped. The production takes on a different texture, percussion more laid back, bass sitting deeper. Baby sounds genuinely comfortable here, less about the statement, more about the vibe. Works.

posts.title: Real Spill
posts.writeup: Raw and direct, no adornments. "Real Spill" is Baby at his most unfiltered, leaning into conversational delivery over a minimal trap frame. The honesty is the whole point. Fans of his early mixtape energy will recognize this register immediately.

posts.title: Poland
posts.writeup: Yachty made the strangest pop record of 2022 and somehow won the internet with it. "Poland" is barely a song by traditional metrics but it functions exactly like one: melodic hook, hypnotic repetition, a production palette that sounds like it came from a parallel universe where genre never existed. Still hits.

posts.title: Baggage
posts.writeup: j ember writes the kind of introspective pop that doesn't announce itself. "Baggage" is patient, built around delicate guitar and a vocal that stays close-mic and confessional. The emotional honesty is in the specifics, not the volume. We've been watching what this project does next.

posts.title: Crazy Sexy Dream Girl
posts.writeup: Lolo Zouai has always moved between worlds comfortably, French-Algerian roots, Bay Area energy, production that pulls from R&B and electro-pop in equal measure. "Crazy Sexy Dream Girl" keeps that identity intact: slick, a little surreal, grounded by a vocal that knows exactly how much to give. Right up your alley if you're into boundary-fluid pop.

posts.title: SLOW DOWN
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK builds tension by subtracting instead of adding. "SLOW DOWN" pulls the tempo down and lets the pressure build on its own terms, vocals raw and deliberate over production that keeps its cool while everything else feels like it's about to break. This is one of the best things he's put to tape.

posts.title: ACTOR
posts.writeup: Blurring the line between character study and self-portrait, "ACTOR" finds SLOE JACK at his most introspective. The production sits low and uneasy, the vocal delivery controlled but barely. There's something being worked through here and it doesn't resolve neatly. That's the point.

posts.title: BACKSTAB
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK doesn't telegraph emotion, he buries it in the cadence and lets it surface on repeated listens. "BACKSTAB" has that quality: surface cool, real tension underneath. The production is tight and percussive, giving the vocal nowhere to hide. We're paying close attention to everything this project puts out.

posts.title: WIRED
posts.writeup: High-energy and precise, "WIRED" shows a different gear from SLOE JACK, faster tempo, sharper edges, production that keeps pace without getting cluttered. The control is impressive. This one runs.

posts.title: Borstal
posts.writeup: Monster Florence operates in the space between post-punk tension and something more atmospheric and hard to pin down. "Borstal" is unsettled by design, guitar work angular, the rhythm section locked in, vocals cutting through without softening any of the edges. British rock with actual weight to it.

posts.title: Messy
posts.writeup: This one has some history baked in now, but "Messy" was designed as a statement record and the production backs that up. Cinematic, heavy, Quavo and Takeoff trading space on a track built for maximum impact. However you contextualize it, the song itself lands.

posts.title: Mixy (feat. Summer Walker)
posts.writeup: Summer Walker's presence smooths the energy and shifts the register considerably. "Mixy" is looser and more groove-oriented than most of what Quavo and Takeoff were putting out around this time, Walker's vocals doing the heavy lifting on the melodic side. The contrast works.

posts.title: To The Bone (feat. YoungBoy Never Broke Again)
posts.artist: Quavo, Takeoff, YoungBoy Never Broke Again
posts.writeup: Three heavy hitters sharing space on a production built for big rooms and late nights. YoungBoy's contribution is frenetic and emotionally unpredictable in his usual way, which plays well against Quavo's more measured delivery. Takeoff adds the anchor. Dense and rewarding on repeat.

posts.title: KIDS EAT PILLS*
posts.writeup: Jean Dawson is doing something genuinely strange and it keeps working. "KIDS EAT PILLS*" blends distorted guitars with melodic chaos in a way that doesn't feel borrowed from anywhere obvious. The asterisk in the title feels right. Experimental without being indulgent, emotional without telegraphing it.

posts.title: GLORY*
posts.writeup: "GLORY*" is sprawling and visceral, Dawson's voice moving through the track like it's searching for a resolution that isn't coming. Production layers stack and peel back at unpredictable intervals. This is the kind of record that rewards a good pair of headphones and your full attention.

posts.title: My Friends (feat. Lil Durk)
posts.writeup: Mustard keeps things clean and functional while Ty Dolla $ign and Lil Durk find their rhythm together on "My Friends." The hook is economical, the verses conversational. It's not trying to reinvent anything, it's just a well-made track from people who know exactly what they're doing.

posts.title: Oh No!
posts.writeup: aboynamedblu moves between alt-R&B and something more kinetic without losing the thread. "Oh No!" has energy and charm, production that leans into the unexpected, and a vocal performance that doesn't take itself too seriously. This is a name to keep close.

posts.title: Automatic License
posts.writeup: More introspective than "Oh No!" but from the same creative place, "Automatic License" shows range. The production is textured and deliberate, the emotional center quiet but present. Two tracks in and aboynamedblu already sounds like someone worth following.

posts.title: HALOS
posts.writeup: Kenny Mason raps from Atlanta but sounds like he's solved a problem nobody else knew existed. "HALOS" is dense and atmospheric, production sitting at the intersection of trap and rock without committing to either, vocals riding the tension with genuine confidence. Distinct and fully realized.

posts.title: DOUBLE UP
posts.writeup: "DOUBLE UP" accelerates where "HALOS" broods, harder edges, more percussive, Mason pushing the pace and holding it. The sonic identity stays consistent across both tracks, which is a good sign for what this project becomes. We've been watching Kenny Mason for a while now. Still paying attention.

posts.title: ZOOMIES
posts.writeup: Kenny Mason operates at a frequency most artists can't locate. ZOOMIES is elastic and unhinged in the best way, layering his rapid-fire cadence over a production that keeps shifting the floor beneath your feet. Atlanta energy, experimental nerve. Keep an ear on where he takes this.

posts.title: im so happy
posts.writeup: EKKSTACY trades in controlled contradiction. im so happy is drenched in distorted guitars and a voice that sounds anything but settled, that push-pull between the title and the delivery doing all the heavy lifting. The emo-trap lane is crowded but he moves through it like he invented it.

posts.title: 90 Proof (with J. Cole)
posts.writeup: Two of the most technically gifted voices in hip-hop sharing a track that actually earns its runtime. Smino's melodic ease and J. Cole's precision trade verses over production that breathes instead of bruises. This is the kind of collab that rewards a second listen more than the first.

posts.title: Sober (feat. Roddy Ricch & Post Malone)
posts.writeup: Three West Coast signatures on one record, each doing exactly what they do best. YG anchors it, Roddy brings the melodic weight, and Post slides in like he was always supposed to be there. Sober has the effortless confidence of something that didn't need to try this hard.

posts.title: New Religion
posts.writeup: Refs build songs that feel like a slow-burning realization. New Religion leans on layered vocals and textured instrumentation that earns its emotional payoff without rushing toward it. Deliberate, patient, and worth the time.

posts.title: Ripple
posts.writeup: Sycco teams up with Flume and Chrome Sparks and the result lands exactly where you'd expect those three coordinates to intersect. Ripple is liquid and precise, Sycco's vocals floating above a bed of production that has real structural depth. The Australian singer-songwriter is building something serious.

posts.title: Vibe Like This (feat. Ty Dolla $ign & Lucky Daye)
posts.artist: SG Lewis, Ty Dolla $ign, Lucky Daye
posts.writeup: SG Lewis has been chasing a specific feeling across his whole catalog and Vibe Like This is as close as he's gotten. Ty Dolla $ign and Lucky Daye are natural fits here, their voices warm against the glossy disco-adjacent production. Right up your alley if you've been on the Body album cycle.

posts.title: arizona
posts.writeup: gcmayn works in quiet tension. arizona is spare and unhurried, a track built on space as much as sound, his vocal delivery sitting low in the mix in a way that makes you lean in. One to watch.

posts.title: An Alien In Minneapolis - Edit
posts.writeup: J.S. Ondara moved to Minneapolis from Nairobi because Bob Dylan did, and that origin story hasn't stopped informing his music. An Alien In Minneapolis carries that weight lightly, his voice enormous and unadorned over acoustic folk production. Singular perspective, singular sound.

posts.title: See Through
posts.writeup: HANNI writes pop songs with real emotional legibility. See Through keeps the production clean enough to let the vocal performance do the work, and that turns out to be the right call. A name worth tracking.

posts.title: ANECDOTE
posts.writeup: Rahul operates in the interior spaces. ANECDOTE is introspective indie-pop built around restraint, the production pulling back so the narrative detail can push forward. The kind of song that improves with context.

posts.title: Adam Sandler
posts.writeup: AG Club have been building one of the more genuinely weird and rewarding catalogs in independent hip-hop. Adam Sandler is disarmingly direct given the reference, sharp and self-aware over production that walks the line between throwback and forward-facing. The Bay Area crew stays unpredictable.

posts.title: Long Division
posts.writeup: Long Division hits harder in the AG Club run, the production looser and more confident, the group chemistry audible in how the verses hand off. They're making music for people paying attention.

posts.title: Candice (feat. kiyomi)
posts.writeup: kiyomi's presence shifts the texture. Candice is the most melodic entry in the AG Club stretch here, the collaboration bringing out something warmer without softening the edges. Good foil.

posts.title: Tattoo
posts.writeup: AG Club closing out their corner of this batch with Tattoo, and it's probably the most emotionally direct thing they've put here. The production is minimal and the writing carries the weight. That's the harder bet and they make it work.

posts.title: Far From God
posts.writeup: 88GLAM and WondaGurl is a combination that makes sense the second you hear it. Far From God has the cold atmospheric production she builds toward and 88GLAM's delivery sitting perfectly inside it. Toronto's fingerprints are all over this.

posts.title: A Seminar In Tokyo
posts.writeup: Ondara with the second entry in this batch and it holds up just as well. A Seminar In Tokyo has the literary quality that defines his songwriting, that voice carrying ideas most folk-adjacent artists wouldn't attempt. We've been watching him for years.

posts.title: PIRATE RADIO*
posts.writeup: Jean Dawson doesn't make music that fits cleanly into anything, and PIRATE RADIO is a good example of why that's working in his favor. The track moves between textures and tempos with genuine purpose, guitars and electronics and his voice holding it together. San Diego via everywhere.

posts.title: Love In The Way
posts.writeup: Yung Bleu and Nicki Minaj trading bars on a slow-burn R&B record that leans into its melodrama without apology. Love In The Way has commercial weight but the production gives it room to breathe. Bleu knows how to build a moment.

posts.title: BILL$ (SPED UP)
posts.writeup: DESTIN CONRAD has been putting in serious work in the R&B space and BILL$ lands with the confidence of someone who knows exactly what they're building. The sped-up treatment fits the energy here rather than fighting it. Watch the trajectory on this one.

Fresco Trey moves through 7/11 like he has nowhere to be and nothing to prove, which in turn makes you lean all the way in. The production is laid-back Atlanta in the best tradition, the flow unhurried and fully in pocket.

posts.title: ANTIFREEZE
posts.writeup: hard life and Gus Dapperton make for an unexpected but effective combination. ANTIFREEZE has an off-kilter indie-pop energy that doesn't resolve the way you expect, and that unpredictability is the point. Gus brings his signature weird into a track that was already comfortable with it.

posts.title: more than a woman
posts.writeup: eee gee works in a softer register and more than a woman is the kind of song that rewards a quiet room. The production is intimate and the writing doesn't overreach, which is the correct instinct. A find.

posts.title: CENTIPEDE (with KENNY MASON)
posts.writeup: Amindi and Kenny Mason make a strange and compelling match. CENTIPEDE has a rhythmic unpredictability that keeps you recalibrating, the two artists' energies creating genuine friction rather than just coexisting. Worth the attention.

posts.title: Waiting Outside
posts.writeup: Big Wild has been one of the more consistent names in electronic-adjacent indie and Waiting Outside holds that standard. The production has real emotional architecture, building and releasing with intention. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Bad Juice
posts.writeup: Lila Drew wraps a slow-burn indie pop groove around a deceptively heavy subject — the sour aftertaste of a relationship that looked sweet on the outside. Her voice sits low and confident over warm guitar and understated percussion, letting the lyrics do the heavy lifting. The production never overreaches, which is exactly right for this kind of gut-punch songwriting. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: White Mercedes
posts.writeup: "White Mercedes" moves at the pace of a late-night drive with no real destination. Payday keeps the energy measured, floating over a sparse, moody beat that leans more into atmosphere than anything else. There's a quiet confidence here that suggests someone with a lot more to show.

posts.title: BIRDS
posts.writeup: ayrtn and bb build something hazy and nocturnal on "BIRDS," all soft edges and layered vocals sitting inside a beat that barely touches the floor. It's the kind of track that rewards headphones. Minimal and deliberate, exactly as it should be.

posts.title: Break!
posts.writeup: Fazerdaze returns with something louder and more urgent than anything in her catalog before this. "Break!" runs on fuzz-drenched guitars and an energy that feels genuinely cathartic, New Zealand's Amelia Murray stepping fully into a sound that suits her. The chorus has real momentum. Worth your attention.

posts.title: Buena Park
posts.writeup: Teenage Priest sets "Buena Park" in the suburban Southern California sprawl and finds something melancholy in the ordinariness of it. Guitar-forward with a slightly dusty production aesthetic, it's the kind of track that sounds better through car speakers with the windows down. Understated and honest.

posts.title: Two Friends
posts.writeup: Newport's "Two Friends" sits in that specific indie pop pocket where intimacy and polish coexist without tension. Clean guitar tones, a steady rhythm section, and vocals that prioritize sincerity over technique. It's a small song about something real, and that's more than enough.

posts.title: Mondeo (feat. PLK)
posts.writeup: Parisian rapper Doums links with PLK on this one, and the result is exactly what you'd want from both of them. Understated French rap production, relaxed but precise delivery, the two artists sharing space without competing. If you've been sleeping on the French scene, this is a solid entry point.

posts.title: Rollercoasters
posts.writeup: "Rollercoasters" captures the specific emotional vertigo of something you know is bad for you but can't stop riding. Bye Malo keeps the production warm and slightly woozy, guitars and synths blending into something that feels as unstable as the subject matter. Well-crafted pop with actual emotional intelligence behind it.

posts.title: YUKON (INTERLUDE)
posts.writeup: Joji doesn't waste a second on this one. "YUKON (INTERLUDE)" is brief by design, a palette cleanser from the 88rising artist that somehow carries more emotional weight per second than most full songs. Delicate piano, that unmistakable voice, and a production that knows when to get out of the way.

posts.title: Beat the Odds
posts.writeup: Lil Tjay recorded "Beat the Odds" from a place most people wouldn't come back from. The Bronx rapper, who survived being shot six times in June 2022, channels that survival into something that sounds less like triumph and more like quiet resolve. The melodic rap production is polished and radio-ready, but the weight behind it is real.

posts.title: all on me
posts.writeup: kid apollo builds "all on me" around a warm, aching production that sits comfortably somewhere between indie R&B and bedroom pop. His voice carries the vulnerability of the writing without overselling it. The kind of track that earns repeat plays without announcing itself loudly.

posts.title: blessing me (feat. Kali Uchis) (remix)
posts.artist: Mura Masa, Pa Salieu, Skillibeng, Kali Uchis
posts.writeup: The original was already excellent. This remix brings Pa Salieu and Skillibeng into the Kali Uchis-featured version, widening the sonic geography considerably. Mura Masa's production holds everything together across what could easily become cluttered. It doesn't.

posts.title: sometimes
posts.writeup: Jonah Zed and KESHORE build something quietly devastating on "sometimes." Sparse production leaves plenty of room for the vocal interplay to breathe, and the song uses that space well. This is the kind of late-night track that hits harder with each listen.

posts.title: I Did Drugs For You
posts.writeup: Rook Monroe puts a dark offering on the table with this one. "I Did Drugs For You" is sharp-edged indie rock with a confessional streak, the production leaning into controlled chaos without losing the melody underneath. It's the kind of song that needs the volume up.

posts.title: Camp Anonymous
posts.writeup: Cautious Clay has always threaded jazz sensibility through his pop framework, and "Camp Anonymous" pulls that thread tighter. The production is layered and considered, with live instrumentation pushing against programmed elements in interesting ways. His vocal phrasing remains one of the more distinctive things in this lane.

posts.title: sophisticated
posts.writeup: bbno$ leans into the absurdist humor that's been his calling card, but "sophisticated" has a hook that doesn't need the joke to work. The production is polished and deliberately playful, and he lands the self-aware swagger without it curdling into irony. Silly on the surface, well-constructed underneath.

posts.title: ‎whiskey
posts.writeup: maxime. keeps it direct and slightly melancholy on "whiskey," a sparse acoustic-forward track built around a voice that sounds like it means every word. The restraint in the production is what makes it land. No flourishes, no fuss, just the song.

posts.title: On My Own
posts.writeup: Samuel Petra's "On My Own" is polished indie pop with a clean emotional throughline. The production is warm without being overwrought, and Petra's vocal sits confidently at the center of it. Well-executed and genuinely affecting.

posts.title: MINE
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK's "MINE" lands with the kind of quiet authority you'd expect from an artist who's been building toward something. The production is cool and deliberate, giving his delivery room to register. Worth knowing this one before it becomes obvious.

posts.title: Dance Now (with Kenny Mason)
posts.writeup: JID and Kenny Mason have an easy chemistry that makes "Dance Now" feel effortless without being lazy. JID's wordplay is as tight as ever, Kenny Mason brings a rawer energy, and the two elevate each other throughout. From JID's "The Forever Story," one of 2022's best rap albums.

posts.title: Haitian Scarface
posts.writeup: Kodak Black traces his Haitian roots on this one with a track that sounds like a mission statement. The production leans menacing and atmospheric, and Kodak's delivery has more focus than usual. The pride in the heritage is palpable.

posts.title: BIG MAN
posts.writeup: Kevin George's falsetto is one of R&B's more underrated instruments right now. "BIG MAN" puts it front and center over a sleek, minimal production that trusts the voice to carry the weight. The Atlanta singer has been building quietly, and tracks like this are why we keep watching.

posts.title: LORD FORGIVE ME
posts.artist: Tobe Nwigwe, Pharrell Williams, EARTHGANG, Fat Nwigwe
posts.writeup: Tobe Nwigwe assembled a legitimate roster for this one: Pharrell Williams, EARTHGANG, and his wife Fat Nwigwe all show up, and none of it feels like a flex for its own sake. The production has a gospel weight to it, and the performances match the gravity of the subject. One of the more spiritually honest rap tracks of 2022.

posts.title: Faster
posts.writeup: "Faster" finds Lil Tecca in a lean, efficient mode. The production hits quick and doesn't overstay, and his melodic delivery navigates the pocket with the ease of someone who's been doing this long enough to make it look simple. Short, sharp, effective.

posts.title: STAYING ALIVE (feat. Drake & Lil Baby)
posts.writeup: DJ Khaled got Drake and Lil Baby in a room and built something that lives up to the collaborators. The production is lush and confident, Drake's verse is measured and precise, and Lil Baby carries the outro with the effortless energy that's made him ubiquitous. Another one.

posts.title: DAY PARTY
posts.writeup: Destin Conrad has been building toward something, and 'DAY PARTY' shows exactly what. The LA-based singer wraps velvet R&B production around a simple, perfect premise: sun up, vibes high, company good. Layered harmonies stack behind a groove that never oversells itself. Keep an ear on Conrad.

posts.title: Propeller (feat. Dave & BNXN)
posts.writeup: JAE5 pulls together two of the UK and Nigerian scenes' sharpest voices and lets them breathe. Dave's deliberate cadence contrasts BNXN's melodic ease over production that sits somewhere between Afrofusion and London drill without fully landing in either. The result is effortless in a way that clearly took work.

posts.title: GLOCK IN MY PURSE
posts.writeup: Uzi fully leaning into the Pink Tape era, where gender provocation is just another production layer. The beat is sparse and menacing, leaving room for the bars to do the strutting. Short, punchy, the kind of track that sounds better at volume.

posts.title: FLEX UP
posts.writeup: From the Pink Tape sessions, 'FLEX UP' leans on a bouncy, chaotic energy that suits Uzi's attention span perfectly. The vocal processing is heavy, the ad-libs are doing real structural work, and the whole thing runs about 30 seconds longer than it needs to. Still pops.

posts.title: I KNOW
posts.writeup: Uzi going melodic over a dreamy, slightly woozy beat. The hook is pure ear candy, built for the kind of playlist that runs on repeat without anyone pressing skip. Pink Tape continuity in full effect.

posts.title: CASH IN THE CLUB
posts.writeup: Kevin George and Foxx on a low-lit, mid-tempo R&B joint built for exactly the title says. The production keeps things club-adjacent without committing to a full four-on-the-floor, smart restraint. George's tone is warm and easy, never overreaching.

posts.title: East End Coast
posts.writeup: Glasgow's Joesef writes about love and geography like they're the same subject, and 'East End Coast' is his best proof. The production is warm indie-soul, acoustic intimacy blown up slightly for radio without losing the corner-of-a-pub feeling. His phrasing is conversational in a way that actually lands.

posts.title: hollywood *starlette
posts.writeup: Halo Kitsch traffic in a very specific early-2000s aesthetic: grainy, slightly off-kilter pop with hooks that arrive before you expect them. 'hollywood *starlette' leans into that nostalgia without becoming pastiche. The asterisk in the title is doing real tonal work.

posts.title: Loner
posts.artist: See You Next Year, Terry Presume
posts.writeup: See You Next Year and Terry Presume build 'Loner' around a hook that hits harder than its gentle arrangement suggests. The production is patient, indie-pop with a bedroom warmth that doesn't feel small. Right up your alley if you've been tracking the quieter end of the alternative R&B space.

posts.title: didn't I
posts.writeup: Lou Val keeps it stripped: acoustic guitar, a voice that carries weight without straining for it, and a lyrical directness that doesn't need embellishment. 'didn't I' is one of those songs that sounds like it was written in one sitting and probably was. We've been watching her since her first few singles.

posts.title: Lights
posts.writeup: BEL's 'Lights' operates in that specific pocket of late-night synth-pop where everything feels slightly cinematic. The production is clean without being sterile, and the vocal sits right at the top of the mix without dominating. Short, effective, leaves you wanting the next one.

posts.title: Jet Lag
posts.writeup: BEL leans more atmospheric here, production that breathes a little wider than 'Lights.' The disorientation of the title translates into the arrangement, a subtle temporal drift in the mix that keeps it interesting on repeat listens.

posts.title: JACKIE BROWN
posts.writeup: From *Wasteland*, one of the better album cuts that didn't get the single treatment. Faiyaz channels the Tarantino reference into something genuinely cinematic, a track with texture and patience that rewards full listens over background play. The production from Dpat is immaculate.

posts.title: ADDICTIONS (FEAT. Tre' Amani)
posts.writeup: Tre' Amani holds his own next to Faiyaz, which is not a given. 'ADDICTIONS' is one of the looser, more vulnerable moments on *Wasteland*, both artists trading confessional energy over production that's warmer and less structured than the album's heavier cuts.

posts.title: FYTB (FEAT. JOONY)
posts.writeup: Joony's melodic approach slots into Faiyaz's world naturally. The track moves between singing and speaking in that way Brent does better than almost anyone right now, emotional without ever over-explaining itself.

posts.title: ROLLING STONE
posts.writeup: Pure *Wasteland* sequencing at work. 'ROLLING STONE' is Faiyaz in full control of his narrative, the production sparse enough to make every vocal choice count. The Dpat / Brent collaboration hit different on this album and this is one of the reasons why.

posts.title: ALL MINE
posts.writeup: 'ALL MINE' is where *Wasteland* gets vulnerable without flinching. Faiyaz's falsetto carries the weight here, production scaled back to let the emotion sit front and center. The kind of track that turns casual listeners into real ones.

posts.title: LOOSE CHANGE
posts.writeup: Earlier Faiyaz, before *Wasteland* locked his sound in place. 'LOOSE CHANGE' shows where the instincts came from, a looser, more exploratory R&B cut that still has the emotional specificity that separates him from his contemporaries.

posts.title: OTT
posts.writeup: BENEE's feature lifts this considerably. 'OTT' (over the top) is indie-pop with some self-awareness about its own emotional excess, which makes it work better than the straight version would have. The production from hard life is breezy but not weightless.

posts.title: Part Of The Band
posts.writeup: Matty Healy in full literary mode, the verses reading more like spoken word before the track opens into something unexpectedly moving. 'Part Of The Band' is the clearest example of what the 1975 can do when they resist the impulse to over-produce: acoustic instrumentation, real arrangement, and a hook that earns its length.

posts.title: quick
posts.writeup: Brisbane's Golden Vessel with Akurei, and the pairing works. 'quick' is bedroom electronic at its most refined, a track that feels intimate at any volume. The mixing is airy and precise, nothing overstays.

posts.title: sun+tide
posts.writeup: Golden Vessel solo here, and the production speaks for itself. 'sun+tide' is patient and warm, the kind of ambient-leaning electronic that rewards headphone listening. The texture shifts are subtle but they're doing real work underneath.

posts.title: 9pm
posts.writeup: Safiyah Hernandez writes late-night pop with actual stakes. '9pm' is built around a specific emotional moment and doesn't try to universalize it, which is what makes it connect. The production is minimal and the vocal performance is the whole argument.

posts.title: Bigger Than Life
posts.writeup: Headie One is one of UK drill's most consistent exports, and 'Bigger Than Life' with Frenna leans into an anthemic register that suits both of them. The production is bigger than his usual, but the delivery grounds it. European collaboration that actually travels.

posts.title: I'D GIVE IT ALL UP
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim is working in that particular alt-R&B space where vulnerability reads as confidence, and 'I'D GIVE IT ALL UP' is a clean example of that balance. The production is warm and slightly hazy, the kind of track that lands differently on a second listen.

posts.title: Apartment Complex
posts.writeup: Day Wave and Hazel English are a natural fit, two artists who've built careers on sun-faded guitars and vocals that float just above the surface. 'Apartment Complex' locks into that sweet spot where indie pop feels lived-in rather than constructed. Jackson Phillips keeps the production breezy but grounded, English's presence adding warmth that keeps it from drifting too far into daydream territory. A clean, unhurried listen.

posts.title: I Guess
posts.artist: See You Next Year, EKKSTACY, MIKE DEAN
posts.writeup: EKKSTACY's influence hangs heavy here, that post-emo digital haze that blurs the line between sad and serene. MIKE DEAN's production credit adds a layer of prestige to what's otherwise a fairly raw, emotionally direct track. 'I Guess' leans into resignation without collapsing under it. Worth keeping an ear on See You Next Year.

posts.title: See You When The End's Near
posts.writeup: Day Wave linking with KennyHoopla is a logical pairing: both operate in that hazy, reverb-soaked space where pop structure meets something looser and more anxious. 'See You When The End's Near' channels that shared frequency well, Jackson Phillips' production giving Hoopla's signature tension some room to breathe. It's the kind of song that sounds better at dusk than at noon.

posts.title: Charlie Went Blind
posts.writeup: Emma Rena works quietly in the singer-songwriter space, and 'Charlie Went Blind' is a patient, carefully constructed piece. The title alone earns your attention. Her writing leans on narrative detail over emotional shorthand, which gives the song more staying power than most.

posts.title: Lost Me
posts.writeup: GIVEON doesn't need much. That baritone is its own production element, and 'Lost Me' knows to build around it rather than compete. The arrangement stays sparse and soulful, giving his voice the space to land heavy on every note. Off the Certified Lover Boy collab buzz, GIVEON was proving he had a solo lane worth watching.

posts.title: Get To You
posts.writeup: Another cut from GIVEON's Give or Take era, 'Get To You' is slower and more deliberate than his major label breakout moments. He's not in a hurry to impress and the track reflects that. The production is clean and moody, and his vocal runs feel earned rather than showy. Quietly one of the better pure R&B voices of his generation.

posts.title: Tootsies
posts.writeup: Roddy Ricch sounds the most at ease when the production gives him space to talk rather than perform, and 'Tootsies' is one of those moments. West Coast warmth in the beat, melody-forward flow, and Roddy in a reflective mode rather than a flexing one. Compton's grip on melodic rap hasn't loosened.

posts.title: Real Talk
posts.writeup: Stripped back and conversational, 'Real Talk' finds Roddy in a more direct headspace. Less bounce than his radio cuts, more weight. The production pulls back and lets the bars do the work. A reminder that the best moments from artists like him often come when the pressure to have a hit is off.

posts.title: Count Me Out
posts.writeup: SoFaygo came up through the Travis Scott orbit and carries that Houston-adjacent atmospheric energy into his own sound. 'Count Me Out' runs on distorted 808s and a melodic hook that sits somewhere between menacing and melancholy. Atlanta via outer space. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: A Keeper
posts.writeup: Honestly Nevermind era Drake was a polarizing release, but 'A Keeper' was one of the moments that worked. The UK garage and house production from Black Coffee and Gordo gives it a texture that felt genuinely new for him. Less about lyrics, more about feeling. That's not a critique.

posts.title: Down Hill
posts.writeup: From Certified Lover Boy's more understated corners, 'Down Hill' is Drake in low-stakes mode, smooth production, casual delivery, a song designed to play well in the background without disappearing entirely. It doesn't demand attention but rewards it when given.

posts.title: Massive
posts.writeup: One of the standout cuts from Certified Lover Boy, 'Massive' rides a sample flip with a laid-back confidence that Drake does effortlessly. The hook is deceptively simple and extremely sticky. This one held up better than much of the album.

posts.title: By MySelf
posts.writeup: TyFontaine is working in a lane that combines SoundCloud-era rawness with more polished melodic sensibilities, and 'By MySelf' shows him at his most introspective. The production is airy and slightly claustrophobic at once, matching the self-isolation theme. He's developing a distinct voice in a crowded space.

posts.title: CORSA
posts.writeup: Hit-Boy and Dom Kennedy is a West Coast meeting of two very different but compatible energies. Hit-Boy's beats have always had a luxury quality and 'CORSA' leans fully into that, all gloss and confidence. Dom sounds like he's had this car his whole life. Smooth without being boring.

posts.title: Sticky
posts.writeup: Honestly Nevermind had its detractors but 'Sticky' was a rare moment where the pivot to dance floor energy felt earned. Black Coffee's production is the real draw here, the South African house influence giving the track a rolling, hypnotic quality that holds up on repeat. Drake sounds liberated on it.

posts.title: The Highs & The Lows
posts.writeup: Chance and Joey Bada$$ sharing a record is the kind of collab that feels overdue.
