Both are Chicago and Brooklyn legends in the alternative rap space and 'The Highs & The Lows' gives them both room to reflect. Production is warm and unhurried. Two voices worth trusting on a track together.

posts.title: Jimmy Cooks (feat. 21 Savage)
posts.writeup: The closing track off Certified Lover Boy works as a mood piece more than a statement, Drake and 21 Savage in comfortable mode over a Memphis-influenced beat. 21's verse is the highlight, his monotone delivery landing harder than anything flashier would. A satisfying endpoint for the project.

posts.title: killer
posts.writeup: 'Killer' is FKA twigs operating at her most visceral, the production jagged and intimate at the same time. Her voice shifts between fragile and confrontational inside the same bar, which is the trick she's always done best. This was from the Caprisongs mixtape and it fits perfectly in that loosely assembled, emotionally raw context.

posts.title: MPMU, IS!
posts.writeup: Yuki Dreams Again traffics in a kind of hypnagogic indie pop where the production feels just out of reach, and 'MPMU, IS!' follows that blueprint faithfully. It's the kind of music that sounds like it exists slightly outside the current moment. Intriguing enough to revisit.

posts.title: RENEGADE
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK doesn't do anything halfway and 'RENEGADE' is a good reminder of that. The production has teeth and his delivery matches it, urgent and uncompromising. We've been watching this one develop for a while. The trajectory is pointing up.

posts.title: Upper Hand
posts.writeup: Zach Zoya is a Canadian artist building a sound that sits comfortably between R&B and pop, and 'Upper Hand' with Soran leans into the latter. Clean production, strong melodic instincts, and a hook that doesn't overstay its welcome. One to keep an ear on as his catalog grows.

posts.title: Waiting For Never
posts.writeup: Post Malone built his entire career on this feeling: guitar-forward, emotionally open, slightly bruised. 'Waiting For Never' doesn't reinvent his sound but it doesn't need to. The production is warm and the hook lands clean. He's one of the most reliable pop craftsmen of his era and moments like this are why.

posts.title: Denial
posts.writeup: Rae Sremmurd's reunion energy was palpable on SremmLife 3 and 'Denial' catches them in a more reflective space than their club-ready catalog would suggest. Swae Lee's melody work is the draw as always, his falsetto wrapping around the hook in a way that makes it feel effortless. Slim Jxmmi holds the ground underneath.

posts.title: Do What I Want
posts.writeup: From the Entergalactic soundtrack, 'Do What I Want' is Kid Cudi in the clearest version of himself: dreamy, self-assured, not trying to prove anything. The beat is exactly what you expect and that's the right call. Some artists earn the right to stay in their lane and Cudi is one of them.

posts.title: Tread Carefully
posts.writeup: 'Tread Carefully' is a late-breaking SZA cut that sounds like it could sit comfortably anywhere in her catalog. Her voice rides the production loosely, and there's a restrained emotional intensity that she carries better than almost anyone right now. A strong close to the batch.

posts.title: Cash In Cash Out
posts.artist: Pharrell Williams, 21 Savage, Tyler, The Creator
posts.writeup: Pharrell assembles two of the most idiosyncratic voices in rap and lets the beat breathe. The production is locked into a trunk-rattling low-end groove with those signature snapping hi-hats, giving Tyler room to flex and 21 to do exactly what he does best. It's loose, confident, and sounds like summer through a cracked window. Keep an ear on GIRL era Pharrell when he's cooking like this.

posts.title: Hateful
posts.writeup: Post Malone in full self-examination mode, leaning into the darker pop-rock palette he explored on Twelve Carat Toothache. Distorted guitars underpin a chorus that's equal parts self-aware and melodically irresistible. He's not hiding from the contradictions here, and the production gives the sentiment space to land. Messy in a way that feels earned.

posts.title: LOOT
posts.writeup: Sydney duo BLESSED and Manu Crooks link for a booming, bass-forward track that sits somewhere between UK drill aesthetics and Australian grit. The flows are composed, the 808s hit with purpose, and the chemistry between the two suggests they've been building to this. Keep an eye on what BLESSED puts out of the Southern Hemisphere.

posts.title: WAR ON LOVE
posts.writeup: BLESSED strips things back here, trading the street-level energy for something more introspective. The production sits in a hazy mid-tempo pocket, and the vocal delivery carries real weight without pushing for drama. Three minutes that pass quickly because everything earns its place.

posts.title: Dark Clouds
posts.writeup: Vory has always had a voice that sounds like it's carrying something, and Dark Clouds leans all the way into that. The production is sparse, piano-draped, and let the emotion do the structural work. This is from his debut album and it's exactly the kind of track that reminds you why we've been watching him.

posts.title: Lesson Learned
posts.writeup: More from Vory's FORD album and another piece of his quietly devastating emotional range. The production layers texture without crowding the vocal, and the songwriting lands with the kind of restraint that separates him from the pack. Right up your alley if you track what happens when rap and R&B stop fighting each other.

posts.title: Lost Souls (ft. Fresco Trey)
posts.writeup: Fresco Trey adds a complementary rawness to Vory's polished introspection here. The two voices find a natural call-and-response rhythm over a beat built for late nights and long drives. The piano loop at the center of the track gives it an elegiac quality that sticks around after it's over.

posts.title: CWR Interlude
posts.writeup: A brief interstitial from the FORD album, mostly atmosphere. No preview available but it functions as connective tissue in the project and is worth hearing in sequence. Vory uses interludes to set emotional tone rather than fill space.

posts.title: Happy Birthday 2U
posts.writeup: One of the more unexpectedly tender moments on FORD. Vory takes a concept that could've turned saccharine and grounds it in specific feeling, over production that keeps things grounded and spare. Short, well-constructed, and more affecting than the title suggests.

posts.title: Chanel Fix (ft. Landstrip Chip)
posts.writeup: Landstrip Chip's energy is a sharp contrast to Vory's smoothness, and the friction works. The beat has a sleek, minimalist luxury feel that matches the subject matter without going over the top. Another strong showing from an album that held up as a complete piece of work.

posts.title: Wasting Angels (with The Kid LAROI)
posts.writeup: Two of the biggest pop-leaning voices in rap meet in a soaring, guitar-driven track that could soundtrack a stadium. Post and LAROI have a natural melodic chemistry, and the production from Louis Bell and co. fills the space without getting crowded. One of the stronger moments off Twelve Carat Toothache.

posts.title: Distraction
posts.writeup: Polo G stays in his lane here, which is exactly where he's most effective. The production is sleek Chicago rap at its current peak, and his pen stays sharp on the self-aware themes he's been circling since Hall of Fame. Distraction functions as both subject and mood, and he threads that needle cleanly.

posts.title: Off Day
posts.writeup: Finn Harry writes the kind of understated pop that sounds like a rainy Tuesday at 11 AM. The production is clean and unhurried, and his voice carries a lived-in quality that makes the emotional specificity of the writing land harder. If you haven't heard of him yet, this is a solid place to start.

posts.title: This is not my idea of a party
posts.writeup: Tj Loft's title alone earns attention. The track sits in a wry, indie-adjacent space, building unease through specificity rather than drama. No preview available, but the sonic palette the Spotify snippet suggests is dry and deliberate. One to watch.

posts.title: Lesson Learned
posts.writeup: TyFontaine has been building toward a signature sound that blends melodic rap with an almost ambient emotional weight, and this track is part of that arc. No preview available but his catalog rewards patience. The production choices here are intentional and worth seeking out in full.

posts.title: Another Sketch
posts.writeup: Lil Silva has spent years operating at the intersection of UK bass music and sleek R&B production, and Another Sketch is a clean example of why that lane fits him. The groove is deep, the textures are considered, and it moves with a momentum that doesn't need a hard drop to justify it. Feast your ears.

posts.title: HOTEL LOBBY (Unc & Phew)
posts.writeup: Quavo and Takeoff as a duo strip away some of the Migos maximalism and find a pocket that suits both of them. The beat is spacious Atlanta trap with a melodic hook that lodges itself quickly. What it lacks in depth it makes up in replay value. Pure function.

posts.title: Wraith
posts.artist: Chance the Rapper, VIC MENSA, Smoko Ono
posts.writeup: Chance reunites with VIC MENSA in a track that has Chicago roots all over it. The production has an urgent, almost throwback energy, and the lyricism is sharper than anything Chance had been putting out in the years prior. Smoko Ono's production work gives it a kinetic, slightly chaotic texture that keeps it from feeling too clean.

posts.title: Look at the Sky
posts.writeup: Tanisako operates in a quiet, contemplative pop space that rewards patience. No preview available, but the track has the kind of production restraint that suggests a writer who knows when to leave space. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: TAKE THE BLAME
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim's voice is the kind that sounds like it grew up on Destiny's Child and SZA in equal measure. The production on TAKE THE BLAME gives her enough room to move, with a beat that builds without overreaching. The vocal performance is the main event and she delivers.

posts.title: Do Not Disturb ft. NAV and Yung Bleu
posts.writeup: NAV and Yung Bleu join Vory on a track that's built for exactly what the title suggests. The production is smooth and unhurried, and all three voices find their lane without crowding each other. It's a playlist track in the best sense, something that holds its own outside of an album context.

posts.title: Last Last
posts.writeup: Burna Boy samples Toni Braxton's Unbreak My Heart and makes something entirely his own. The Afrobeats rhythm is undeniable and the melancholy of the source material gets reframed as something defiant rather than broken. This is the kind of cross-genre move that only lands when the artist's conviction is total. One of his best singles.

posts.title: Cooped Up (with Roddy Ricch)
posts.writeup: The album opener from Twelve Carat Toothache sets a breezy, self-aware tone for what follows. Roddy Ricch's verse is economical and effective, and Post's hook leans into his pop instincts without apology. The production is lightweight by design, meant to float. It works.

posts.title: N95
posts.writeup: Kendrick comes off Mr. Morale and the Big Steppers with one of the most confrontational tracks of 2022. N95 is structured chaos, with a production approach that shifts under him as he dismantles performative culture and social media performance in equal measure. The beat switch is jarring in the best way. Unmissable.

posts.title: Savior
posts.artist: Kendrick Lamar, Baby Keem, Sam Dew
posts.writeup: Baby Keem and Sam Dew anchor this track while Kendrick operates at a remove, allowing the irony of the savior complex to play out without spelling it out. The production is airy and spacious, a deliberate contrast to the weight of the subject matter. From one of the most carefully constructed albums of his career.

posts.title: Silent Hill
posts.writeup: Kendrick's pairing with Kodak Black on Mr. Morale's opening statement is uncomfortable by design. The beat is sparse and cold, giving both rappers room to sit in their contradictions. Kendrick doesn't let anyone off the hook, including himself. The contrast between his introspective cadence and Kodak's braggadocio is the whole point.

posts.title: spinning
posts.writeup: Brisbane producer Golden Vessel operates in a soft, waterlogged world of his own making. "spinning" floats on processed guitars and a vocal that sounds like it's being played back from the bottom of a pool. It's the kind of track that makes the room feel smaller and warmer. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Cake
posts.writeup: BEL sits somewhere between the intimacy of bedroom pop and the cleanliness of proper studio R&B. "Cake" keeps the arrangement lean, letting her voice carry the weight without much competition. There's a quiet confidence here that doesn't need to announce itself.

posts.title: The Heart Part 5
posts.writeup: The deepfake visuals got the clicks but the record holds up on its own. Over a Nina Simone sample, Kendrick takes a microscope to the culture and doesn't spare anyone, turning personal address into something that feels historical. It's the kind of statement piece he's been building toward for years. Still required listening.

posts.title: Boring
posts.writeup: Octavian and Skepta on the same track is exactly as cold as you'd expect. The Alpha sessions pulled something confrontational out of both of them, trading bars over a production that stays minimal and menacing. East London at its most confident.

posts.title: Reasonable
posts.writeup: AJ Tracey has been one of the sharpest UK voices in the room for years, and "Reasonable" finds him operating at full efficiency. Tight bars, no wasted motion, production that gives him exactly enough space. He makes it look easy because he's done the work.

posts.title: Mama
posts.writeup: One of the more emotionally direct moments on Alpha. Octavian strips back the bravado and gets personal, and the record is more powerful for it. Nigerian artist Teni brings warmth to the feature that grounds the whole thing. Worth revisiting.

posts.title: Stare
posts.writeup: Octavian, Corbin, and Bobby Raps is a combination that probably shouldn't work as cleanly as it does. The three voices occupy completely different worlds but find common ground in the track's shadowy, low-lit atmosphere. Corbin's section alone is worth the price of admission.

posts.title: Forever
posts.writeup: Teni is a natural fit here, her voice adding a melodic counterweight to Octavian's harder edge. "Forever" leans into a bittersweet frequency that runs through the best moments of Alpha. The production has room to breathe and neither artist overstays.

posts.title: Look At Yourself
posts.writeup: Octavian at his most confrontational. The production is stripped and tense, giving the words nowhere to hide. Alpha as a body of work rewards patience, and tracks like this one are why. Direct, no cushion.

posts.title: WHOSE LOVE (feat. Tempest)
posts.writeup: Naomi Wild and UK rapper Tempest are an unlikely pairing that generates real friction. Her dreamy, LA-via-everywhere aesthetic collides with Tempest's dense cadence in a way that keeps both moving. The production holds the tension without resolving it too neatly.

posts.title: Bento Box
posts.writeup: Sylo works in soft textures and unhurried tempo, and "Bento Box" is a showcase for that approach. The instrumental has a handmade quality, like something assembled in a quiet apartment on a Tuesday afternoon. Easy to return to.

posts.title: PARADE
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK's "PARADE" is built on contrast: the production is spacious and almost ambient while his delivery stays grounded and intentional. It's a track that doesn't rush toward anything, which is exactly what makes it linger. We've been watching this one develop for a while.

posts.title: FALLEN STAR (feat. Y2K)
posts.writeup: Vancouver's Boslen and Y2K find a shared frequency on "FALLEN STAR," blending Boslen's melodic Vancouver rap sensibility with Y2K's production instincts. The track has a cinematic quality without leaning on obvious cues. Right up your alley if you've been tracking either artist.

posts.title: WAIT FOR U (feat. Drake & Tems)
posts.writeup: The Tems sample flips a Lauryn Hill-adjacent warmth into something Future uses as a foundation for one of his most reflective performances in years. Drake does Drake, but Tems is the gravitational center of the record. From I Never Liked You, the album that reminded people Future can still surprise.

posts.title: GOLD STACKS
posts.writeup: Trap maximalism from Future's I Never Liked You era, leaning hard into the opulence and keeping the lyrical bar exactly where it needs to be. The production hits the same pressure points Future's been working since DS2. No reinvention, just execution.

posts.title: PUFFIN ON ZOOTIEZ
posts.writeup: One of the louder moments on I Never Liked YOU, built around a sample that gives the whole track an 808 Mafia-meets-vintage soul feel. Future sounds comfortable in a way that's almost deceptive. This one played well out of speakers and better in cars.

posts.title: KEEP IT BURNIN (feat. Kanye West)
posts.writeup: Two people in very different places in their careers sharing a track and somehow making it cohere. Kanye's verse is the kind of chaotic energy that either lands or doesn't, and on this production it lands. Future plays host and lets the beat do most of the heavy lifting.

posts.title: 712PM
posts.writeup: Quieter than most of I Never Liked You, "712PM" lets Future coast over something lush and slightly melancholy. It's the album's exhale, arriving after a stretch of harder production. Not a standout on first listen; earns its place across multiple plays.

Thavoron moves carefully, and "18" reflects that patience. The production is layered without being cluttered, and the vocal sits in a pocket that's neither fully pop nor fully alternative. There's something being developed here worth keeping an eye on.

posts.title: Fuck This Town
posts.writeup: Johan Lenox writes with the kind of specificity that makes a song feel like a place. "Fuck This Town" is a breakup with geography as much as anything else, leaning on piano and strings that feel genuinely earned rather than decorative. A grower.

posts.title: Give Me a Kiss
posts.writeup: Lolo Zouai has always operated between Paris and San Francisco, and that duality lives in her production choices. "Give Me a Kiss" is direct without being simple, a pop record that trusts the listener to follow the turns. Her voice does the work without overselling it.

posts.title: Extrovert
posts.writeup: Monjola is a Lagos-born, London-raised artist making music that sits in the overlap between afrobeats and indie pop without fully committing to either. "Extrovert" is self-aware in a way that doesn't tip into irony. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: better
posts.writeup: "better" is exactly what the lowercase title implies: understated, careful, no excess. Kamal. works in a soft singer-songwriter space that owes something to Frank Ocean without copying from it. The restraint here is what makes it stick.

posts.title: Open Air
posts.writeup: It's Almost Dry was a reminder that Pusha T at his most focused is among the best in the room. "Open Air" keeps the production lean and Pharrell's touch minimal, giving Push room to deliver bars about the life he's lived without romanticizing or apologizing for it. Precise and cold.

posts.title: CAN'T HANDLE IT
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim keeps his cards close on "CAN'T HANDLE IT" -- smooth R&B production with a vocal delivery that sits right in that pocket between confidence and restraint. The beat is minimal, letting his voice carry the weight. Atlanta-based, still developing, but there's real control here. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Rock N Roll (feat. Kanye West & Kid Cudi)
posts.artist: Pusha T, Kanye West, Kid Cudi
posts.writeup: "It's Almost Dry" announced itself with this one. Ye and Cudi together under Pusha's pen is exactly as combustible as it sounds, riding a beat that tilts between menace and melody. Cudi's hook lands with more weight than expected. Push hasn't lost a step.

posts.title: Dreamin Of The Past (feat. Kanye West)
posts.writeup: Pusha and Ye trading bars over a flipped soul loop that hits harder than the sum of its parts. "Dreamin Of The Past" is nostalgic in production but surgical in content. Kanye's verse is one of his more focused recent turns, and Push sounds motivated by it.

posts.title: Brambleton
posts.writeup: The most personal record on "It's Almost Dry" by a mile. Push goes back to Virginia, back to the block, back to the grief. Sparse production from Ye strips everything down to the confession. No flexing, no deflection. Just the truth.

posts.title: I Pray For You (feat. Labrinth & MALICE)
posts.writeup: Labrinth's production lifts "I Pray For You" into cathedral territory, and Malice showing up alongside his brother gives it real spiritual weight. This is the kind of collaboration that only works when everyone is locked in. It works.

posts.title: Call My Bluff
posts.writeup: Ye's beat construction here is all tension and release, a stuttering backdrop that keeps Pusha locked in predator mode. He's calling out anyone who underestimates what he built. Short, punchy, and precise. No wasted bars.

posts.title: Skin and Bones
posts.writeup: 070 Shake strips back the spectacle on "Skin and Bones" -- no theatrical swells, just raw vocals and minimalist production that lets the vulnerability sit exposed. New Jersey's most unpredictable voice continues moving on her own axis. This one lingers.

posts.title: Hold That Heat (feat. Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: Southside and Travis together is always a specific kind of locked-in, and "Hold That Heat" delivers exactly that. Future slides through the back half to turn the temperature up another notch. The 808s are massive, the vibes are airtight.

posts.title: Scrape It Off (feat. Lil Uzi Vert & Don Toliver)
posts.artist: Pusha T, Lil Uzi Vert, Don Toliver
posts.writeup: Stacking Lil Uzi Vert and Don Toliver on a Pusha track is a flex in itself, and the contrast pays off. Toliver's melodic hook bridges the worlds cleanly while Uzi brings chaotic energy that Push balances with precision. One of the more dynamic cuts on the album.

posts.title: Where Did the Day Go
posts.writeup: Wet return with the kind of gauzy indie-pop that made their early catalog essential. "Where Did the Day Go" has that woozy, time-dissolving feeling built right into the production. Kelly Zutrau's voice sits at the front, unhurried and precise. Right up your alley if you followed them from the start.

posts.title: cool guy
posts.writeup: girlhouse flips the script on the "cool guy" archetype with a track that's equal parts hook and self-awareness. The production is punchy power-pop with enough rough edges to keep it honest. Short and sharp. She doesn't need more time than this.

posts.title: Sober
posts.writeup: "Sober" rides a hazy, slow-burn production that feels more intentional than its lo-fi surface suggests. superflirt uses restraint well, letting the emotional core breathe without over-explaining. One to watch as the catalog builds.

posts.title: Altar
posts.writeup: LUCA brings a cinematic weight to "Altar" -- the production swells with restraint, and her vocal control keeps it from tipping into over-earnestness. Faith and devotion as subject matter, treated with care. Strong debut energy.

posts.title: Dosey Doe (feat. Olive Amun & BOI)
posts.writeup: Roy Bing, Olive Amun, and BOI stack up textures on "Dosey Doe" that feel more like a late-night session than a finished track -- in the best way. Each voice carves a different angle into the beat. Collaborative chemistry that doesn't feel forced.

posts.title: WHEN SPARKS FLY
posts.writeup: Vince Staples in full Ramona Park mode -- the production is clean Long Beach summer, and his delivery is as deadpan and devastating as ever. "WHEN SPARKS FLY" moves like a slow cruise down a street you know by heart. He makes it look effortless.

posts.title: just like before
posts.writeup: juno roome works in the space between bedroom pop and something more considered, and "just like before" sits right there. The production is spare, the emotion is specific. UK-based artist with a voice that doesn't need any tricks to land.

posts.title: Deadend
posts.writeup: BANKS on "Serpentina" is BANKS fully committed to the darker edge of her sound. "Deadend" is icy production and a vocal that moves between control and surrender. It doesn't ask for your sympathy. It just states the facts.

posts.title: can you say the same
posts.writeup: ROLE MODEL's "Rx" delivered a more grounded version of his usual confessional indie-pop, and "can you say the same" is the album in miniature. Self-interrogation over soft guitars and low-end that warms as it goes. He's getting more specific, and it shows.

posts.title: Hello (feat. Chlöe & KayCyy)
posts.writeup: Chloe's presence on "Hello" is the story here -- her vocal against Fivio's drill energy creates a friction that works better than it should. KayCyy fills in the texture without overstaying. B.I.B.L.E. showed Fivio swinging for something wider.

posts.title: Through the Fire (feat. Quavo)
posts.writeup: Quavo and Fivio don't seem like a natural pairing on paper, but "Through the Fire" makes the case. The beat builds slow and then opens up, and both artists ride the shift well. Melodic without losing the drill foundation.

posts.title: LEMONADE
posts.writeup: Ty Dolla Sign was the right call on "LEMONADE" -- his presence softens the edges just enough to let the groove breathe. Vince floats above the production with that same detached precision, making something that sounds like a summer hit and hits like an observation. Classic Staples.

posts.title: LONDON (feat. J. Cole)
posts.writeup: BIA and J. Cole on a slow international flex, trading bars over a beat that sounds like a first-class departure gate. Cole's verse is measured and purposeful. BIA holds her own throughout, never letting him overshadow the record she's building.

posts.title: Changed On Me (feat. Vory & Polo G)
posts.writeup: Vory's presence is the emotional anchor on "Changed On Me" -- he brings a melodic softness that neither Fivio nor Polo G typically access on their own. Polo G's verse lands with real feeling. One of the more honest moments on B.I.B.L.E.

posts.title: First Class
posts.writeup: Jack Harlow flipped Fergie's "Glamorous" and rode it to number one for a reason -- it's an undeniable sample and he doesn't overthink it. "First Class" is pure spring 2022 energy, commercially sharp and self-aware about it. Sometimes a hit is just a hit.

posts.title: Right On
posts.writeup: "Right On" is Lil Baby running the same lane he owns, but the production here has a spaciousness that lets the bars land harder. Clean, confident, and built for exactly the moment it was made for. He doesn't need to reinvent anything.

posts.title: OBSESSION
posts.writeup: Naomi Wild has been building quietly for a while, and 'OBSESSION' is the track that puts it all in focus. Her vocals sit at the edge of breathless, draped over a production that pulses with restrained tension. LA pop with real emotional weight behind it. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: perfect timing
posts.writeup: Joey XL keeps it understated here, letting the production breathe while his delivery does the heavy lifting. Soft-edged R&B with a slow-burn groove that rewards patience. The title earns itself by the end.

posts.title: Badget
posts.writeup: Hamond lands somewhere between UK garage and contemporary R&B, with a choppy, stuttering rhythm that gives 'Badget' its edge. The production is tight without feeling clinical. Short and confident, exactly the right length.

posts.title: Millions
posts.writeup: Sylo works in that hazy alt-R&B pocket where ambition and anxiety coexist. 'Millions' carries a weightless quality on top but there's real pressure underneath the surface. The production stays minimal and lets the emotion do the work.

posts.title: Backwards
posts.writeup: Lil Silva and Sampha together is a natural pairing, two artists who understand space and restraint. 'Backwards' moves slowly, deliberately, Sampha's voice threading through Silva's signature atmospheric low-end. It sounds like something you'd hear at 3 AM and not forget.

posts.title: Inland Pressure
posts.writeup: Civ Pierre's 'Inland Pressure' carries the weight of the title throughout. Understated production, a voice that knows when to pull back. This one works best if you let it settle in rather than chasing it.

posts.title: Sunburn
posts.writeup: spill tab writes indie pop with an actual point of view, and 'Sunburn' lands that balance of catchy and honest. Guitar-forward, emotionally direct, the kind of song that feels immediate on first listen and sticks around after. We've been watching her for a minute.

posts.title: Get My Shit Together
posts.writeup: Johan Lenox and Thouxanbanfauni make an unexpected pairing that actually works. Lenox's classical-meets-pop production provides a grounding the track needs. Self-aware and a little chaotic in the best way.

posts.title: Heaven’s EP (with J. Cole)
posts.writeup: J. Cole in full reflection mode, 'Heaven's EP' moves with the measured cadence he's known for. The Dreamville production is understated, leaving room for the bars to land. Cole still sounds like he's working something out in real time, which is exactly what makes it worth hearing.

posts.title: Honest
posts.writeup: Congee leans into lo-fi bedroom pop without hiding behind the aesthetic. 'Honest' has the kind of vulnerability that doesn't announce itself. Production stays rough around the edges in a way that feels intentional.

posts.title: the loser song
posts.writeup: toast has a knack for packaging real feeling in a low-key format, and 'the loser song' is that instinct working at its best. Hazy production, plainspoken lyrics that land harder than they should. Right up your alley if you like your pop slightly bruised.

posts.title: Logan Paul
posts.writeup: Col3trane has been one of the more interesting voices coming out of the UK R&B scene, and 'Logan Paul' catches him in a sardonic, pop-leaning mode. Silky production underneath a sharp lyrical edge. The contrast is the point.

posts.title: Bus Line 200
posts.writeup: Koko Love takes a slow, atmospheric approach on 'Bus Line 200', letting the texture do the talking. It's patient music, the kind that rewards headphone listening. An artist still defining their sound but already doing something worth paying attention to.

posts.title: pockets full of rocks
posts.writeup: Golden Vessel with Emerson Leif and Mallrat is a collaboration that hits exactly as warm as it sounds on paper. The production is sun-soaked and layered, Mallrat's presence adding the emotional anchor it needs. Australian indie pop at its most immediate.

posts.title: Floors
posts.writeup: LOYAL builds 'Floors' on a sparse, tension-filled arrangement that earns its release. The production has that controlled restraint we associate with acts like James Blake's orbit. Worth a full headphones session.

posts.title: to the outer rim and back
posts.writeup: Namou thinks in expansive terms, and the title of this one is not an accident. Ambient textures and a slow structural patience make 'to the outer rim and back' feel genuinely cinematic without reaching for it. Keep an ear on this project.

posts.title: Pastlife
posts.writeup: Day Wave has always been reliable in that washed-out guitar-pop lane, and 'Pastlife' is him operating comfortably in his own pocket. Reverb-heavy, slightly melancholy, built for late afternoon drives. The familiarity is not a knock.

posts.title: Bussdown (feat. Kodak Black)
posts.writeup: Latto and Kodak make sense together, both working in high-energy Southern rap with a commercial instinct that doesn't apologize for itself. 'Bussdown' hits hard and stays there. Production is exactly what the track calls for.

posts.title: Sunshine (feat. Lil Wayne & Childish Gambino)
posts.writeup: Three distinct energies converge on 'Sunshine' and somehow it holds together. Latto anchors it, Gambino adds color, and Wayne is Wayne. The production is bright and propulsive, made for summer playlist placement. It works.

posts.title: night fever
posts.writeup: Jonah Zed keeps things atmospheric and unresolved on 'night fever', which is exactly the right call. The low-lit production and understated vocal create a late-night texture that doesn't overstay its welcome. Promising early work.

posts.title: Time Bomb
posts.writeup: Yuki Dreams Again operates in a cinematic indie space that feels genuinely its own. 'Time Bomb' builds with quiet urgency, the production opening up slowly before it commits. The restraint is a feature.

posts.title: PBR (with girlhouse)
posts.writeup: BEL and girlhouse land somewhere in the indie-pop-meets-emo space that's been thriving online, and 'PBR' is a solid example of why. Guitar-forward, emotionally honest, a little ragged in the right spots. Feels real.

posts.title: fool around
posts.writeup: strongboi's 'fool around' is casual in presentation but the production has more going on underneath than first pass suggests. Hazy, slightly off-kilter, it fits in the pocket with a lot of the bedroom-pop-adjacent stuff we've been feeding you.

posts.title: Light & Dark
posts.writeup: Dre Six navigates duality with a production that actually reflects the title. The track shifts tones without losing its thread, which takes more control than most. R&B with some real compositional thought behind it.

posts.title: Magic City (feat. Quavo)
posts.writeup: Fivio Foreign and Quavo in a strip-club trap banger is not a surprise, but 'Magic City' executes the formula cleanly. The 808s are exactly right, both artists locked into their respective lanes. Sometimes the obvious play is the right one.

posts.title: It's ok, I'm okay
posts.writeup: Freddie Sunshine keeps things warm and spare on this one, built around a fingerpicked acoustic loop and his own voice doing most of the heavy lifting. The production barely blinks, which is the point. It's a reassurance song that actually sounds like one, not a stadium plea dressed up in minimalism. Keep an ear on this Melbourne singer-songwriter.

posts.title: paul blart mall cop
posts.writeup: Philly's girlhouse leans into emo-pop with a title that sounds like a joke but a track that hits anything but. Crunchy guitars, a chorus that opens up when you need it to, and lyrics sharp enough to make you wince. Right up your alley if you've been spending time with Soccer Mommy or Phoebe Bridgers and want something with a little more bite.

posts.title: Smoking & Thinking
posts.writeup: Durk at his most reflective, skating over a slow, hazy beat that sounds like late nights in a room you don't want to leave. 'Smoking & Thinking' is less about flexing and more about processing, which has always been where he's most honest. From 7220, one of Chicago's most commercially dominant projects in years.

posts.title: Barbarian
posts.writeup: Durk turns up the menace here, letting the bass-forward production carry most of the aggression while his ad-libs do the rest. Short, purposeful, no wasted motion. 7220 showed he could hold both modes without blinking.

posts.title: What Happened To Virgil (feat. Gunna)
posts.writeup: A tribute to Virgil Abloh that doesn't try too hard, which is why it works. Durk and Gunna keep it conversational over a muted, melancholy instrumental that fits the weight of the moment. Real grief sounds like this.

posts.title: No Interviews
posts.writeup: Durk keeps the energy matter-of-fact across a dark, punchy beat that doesn't overstay its welcome. 'No Interviews' is the kind of track that rewards repeated listens more than it announces itself. Consistent is an understatement at this point.

posts.title: Golden Child
posts.writeup: The 7220 standout that leans fully into Durk's melodic side. Layered vocals, a beat with room to breathe, and a hook that stays with you without trying to. He's earned this lane.

posts.title: Headtaps
posts.writeup: Short and deliberate, 'Headtaps' moves on quiet confidence. The instrumental has a subdued, almost cinematic quality, letting Durk's flow take the lead. A solid deep cut from one of Chicago's most consistent voices.

posts.title: You Don't Cry Enough
posts.writeup: aboynamedblu works in a lane somewhere between bedroom R&B and soft indie pop, and this track finds him at his most vulnerable. The production is minimal and airy, which keeps the emotional core front and center. Worth sitting with if you gravitate toward Brent Faiyaz's quieter moments.

posts.title: Be Me
posts.writeup: The London rapper-singer hybrid delivers something genuinely hard to categorize here. 'Be Me' floats between spoken-word bars and melodic runs over a production that sounds handmade without sounding rough. Finn Foxell has been one of the UK's more interesting underground voices, and this track is a clean entry point.

posts.title: Anybody Else
posts.writeup: KALI's voice commands every second of this track, full-stop. Big R&B production with enough space for the vocals to hit hard on the drop without feeling overcrowded. She's been on the radar in the UK for a reason, and this is the kind of song that moves that conversation to a wider stage.

posts.title: Lately
posts.writeup: Worry Club and John the Ghost built something tender here, slow-burning indie folk with harmonies that feel earned rather than engineered. 'Lately' is the kind of track that sneaks up on you by the third listen. Understated in the best way.

posts.title: Starlight
posts.writeup: Dave doesn't waste a bar. 'Starlight' is a meditative standout, stretching his pen across big themes without losing the intimacy that makes him one of the UK's essential voices right now. The production gives him room, and he fills every inch of it. We've been watching him build toward this.

posts.title: Make You Sick
posts.writeup: BENEE's New Zealand indie-pop fingerprints are all over this, bouncy and sardonic in equal measure. The hook is immediate, the production keeps a nervous energy bubbling under the surface, and she sounds like she's having fun making something that also kind of stings.

posts.title: Last Call
posts.writeup: Khalid strips things back on 'Last Call,' letting his voice carry the track on a minimal, late-night R&B instrumental. The restraint is what makes it work. Less production can be a flex when the voice is this good, and Khalid's has always been the real argument.

posts.title: nowhere to hide
posts.writeup: Kamal. works in a soft acoustic space that has a real intimacy to it, nothing extra, nothing loud. 'nowhere to hide' is honest in the way that songs recorded close to the mic usually are. A gentle discovery worth bookmarking.

posts.title: Doom Scrolling
posts.writeup: Doublethink gets the subject matter right without making it feel like a PSA. The production has a jittery, low-fi energy that fits the lyrical territory, and the songwriting is sharper than the indie-pop packaging would suggest. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Time Zone
posts.writeup: Jazz Cartier operates in a cinematic lane that Toronto has always done well. 'Time Zone' is all atmosphere, layered production, and bars that move at their own pace. He's been a name to know out of Canada for years, and this track holds up.

posts.title: POWER (with CeeLo Green & Nick Cannon)
posts.writeup: EARTHGANG keeps expanding their palette, and 'POWER' is their most ambitious swing yet on this stretch. CeeLo brings exactly the kind of old-soul weight that the production calls for, and Nick Cannon's contribution is more earnest than expected. A big-tent gospel-rap hybrid from the Spillage Village duo.

posts.title: BLACK PEARLS (feat. Baby Tate)
posts.writeup: Baby Tate is the move here. Her verse hits harder than anything around it, and EARTHGANG give her the space to prove it. 'BLACK PEARLS' is warm, buoyant, and lives in that Dreamville pocket where rap and soul stop competing.

posts.title: LIE TO ME
posts.writeup: EARTHGANG slow it down and let the production breathe, building a track that's more emotional than most in their catalog. 'LIE TO ME' is restrained by their standards, which turns out to be exactly the right call. One of the better cuts off this run.

posts.title: SMOKE SUM
posts.writeup: Back to the bounce. 'SMOKE SUM' is EARTHGANG in full hang-mode, loose and melodic over a production that sounds like summer in Atlanta. No overthinking, just comfort in the pocket they've carved out.

posts.title: WATERBOYZ (with JID & J. Cole)
posts.writeup: JID and J. Cole showing up on the same track is its own argument, and EARTHGANG built a beat worthy of the occasion. Cole's verse in particular is a reminder of why he's still in the conversation. 'WATERBOYZ' is the kind of collaboration that actually delivers on its own hype.

posts.title: Crutches
posts.writeup: Deb Never's voice carries a cool detachment that makes even her most personal lyrics feel like she's telling you something she almost didn't. 'Crutches' sits in her signature lane, somewhere between lo-fi R&B and alt-pop, and it sounds effortless because it isn't trying to be anything else. LA's been good at producing artists like her.

posts.title: Straight Back to It
posts.writeup: Central Cee doesn't blink. 'Straight Back to It' is self-assured UK drill built on his flow and a beat that keeps moving without needing to shout. He was already one of the most consistent voices coming out of London, and this track is another clean entry in a catalog that keeps building.

posts.title: BILLI (feat. Future)
posts.writeup: Atlanta duo EARTHGANG link with Future on a track that hits like a humid summer night in the Dirty South. The production is swampy and slow-rolling, all muted 808s and low-slung percussion that gives both rappers space to breathe. Olu and WowGr8 trade verses with the loose confidence of artists who know exactly what they are. Future glides in on autopilot and somehow it works perfectly. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Holding Back
posts.writeup: BANKS has always known how to make restraint feel devastating, and "Holding Back" is that instinct fully realized. The production strips back to almost nothing, just atmospheric synths and a pulse that barely registers, letting her voice carry the whole weight. She is not pleading here, she is stating facts, and that composure is what cuts. LA via the internet, always operating in her own lane.

posts.title: Free
posts.writeup: South London by way of Nigeria, Bakar makes guitar music that breathes differently than most of his contemporaries. "Free" is loose-limbed and sun-drenched, built on a riff that feels like it was found rather than written. There is a rawness to his delivery that keeps everything honest. We have been watching him for a minute and this one confirms the instinct.

posts.title: Runaway
posts.writeup: Bakar lets the groove do most of the talking on "Runaway," a track that locks into a low-key funk pocket and refuses to leave. The guitar work is understated but sticky, and his vocal sits right in the mix rather than floating above it. It has the feel of something recorded live in a room, energy intact. Short and to the point.

posts.title: Ginger Pubes
posts.writeup: One of the more disarmingly casual tracks in Bakar's catalog, "Ginger Pubes" wears its lo-fi edges like a badge. The chord progression is simple but the feeling is not, all quiet tension and half-spoken delivery that makes you lean in. He has a knack for making intimacy feel effortless. South London underground energy, uncut.

posts.title: Not From Here
posts.writeup: Bakar taps into an outsider-kid restlessness on "Not From Here" that hits whether you are from London or anywhere else that never quite fit. The track has a punchy, mid-tempo drive with guitar lines that coil and release in all the right places. His voice has a lived-in quality that anchors the whole thing. Right up your alley if you have been sleeping on him.

posts.title: Youthenasia
posts.writeup: "Youthenasia" is the centerpiece of this Bakar run for a reason. The production opens up more than his other tracks, with a chorus that actually swells, and his vocal performance matches the lift. The word "youthenasia" is doing a lot of work thematically, and he earns every syllable of it. This is the track that converts you.

posts.title: Big City
posts.writeup: BEL brings a cool, almost cinematic detachment to "Big City," a track that captures the particular loneliness of being swallowed by a place that does not know your name. The production has a glassy, nocturnal quality, synths hovering just above the rhythm section like neon reflecting off wet pavement. Her voice sits somewhere between spoken word and melody, and that ambiguity is exactly the point. One to watch.

posts.title: Grown
posts.writeup: Brahny's "Grown" is a slow-burn R&B cut built around a production that gives the track room to feel lived-in. The low end is thick without being heavy, and the vocal layering in the chorus adds warmth without losing intimacy. He is singing about the space between knowing better and doing better, and the music reflects that tension perfectly. Confident debut energy.

posts.title: Cherubim
posts.writeup: "Cherubim" floats in on a bed of ambient synthesis that feels genuinely weightless, Beau Nox building something more like a sound installation than a traditional song structure. The vocal processing blurs the line between instrument and voice in ways that feel intentional and earned. There is a reverence in the production choices, sacred-music references filtered through a decidedly modern lens. Quietly stunning.

posts.title: bbycakes (with Lil Uzi Vert, PinkPantheress & Shygirl)
posts.artist: Mura Masa, Lil Uzi Vert, PinkPantheress, Shygirl
posts.writeup: Mura Masa assembles exactly the right four people for exactly this moment. The production is hyperactive and joyful, all chopped percussion and bubblegum bass, and each featured artist brings a completely different energy that somehow all coheres. PinkPantheress floats, Shygirl lunges, Uzi does what Uzi does. It should not work this well and it absolutely does.

posts.title: so young
posts.writeup: cookii makes the kind of bedroom pop that actually sounds like a bedroom, all soft edges and tape hiss and feelings too big to fully say out loud. "so young" is four minutes of aching nostalgia filtered through Gen Z production sensibility, lo-fi without being precious about it. The hook sticks on the first listen and stays well past its welcome. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: Little League
posts.writeup: Wallice writes indie pop songs with the kind of wry specificity that makes you feel like she is describing your exact situation back to you. "Little League" has a jangly, caffeinated energy that suits the subject matter perfectly, all nervous energy and sharp observations delivered with a grin. The production keeps things bright and immediate. She is good at this.

posts.title: f**k it, i'm the man
posts.writeup: SEB arrives with the kind of confident, slightly chaotic energy that makes a debut track worth paying attention to. "f**k it, i'm the man" is leaning into a melodic rap pocket that sits somewhere between SoundCloud-era bravado and genuine pop instinct. The production is sparse but punchy, giving his delivery room to land. Early days, but the swagger reads as earned.

posts.title: For The Squadron
posts.writeup: SAINt JHN has a gift for making anthems that feel personal and communal at the same time, and "For The Squadron" leans hard into that duality. The production is cinematic and bass-forward, built to move air in a room but not at the expense of feeling. His vocal performance is in the pocket throughout. A reminder that he has been operating at a high level for a while now.

posts.title: Outsidë (feat. Young Thug)
posts.writeup: Yeat and Young Thug share a melodic sensibility that makes their pairing feel inevitable rather than engineered. The production on "Outsidë" is that signature whirring, pitch-shifted trap that Yeat has made his own, and Thug glides through it without breaking a sweat. The track has a hazy, late-night quality, music for the hours after the hours. Two artists who do not need to try hard, and do not.

posts.title: Torn
posts.writeup: Canadian sibling duo Tennyson make electronic music that has a human warmth most producers cannot fake. "Torn" is intricate without being cold, built on live instrumentation chopped and reassembled into something that moves organically. The melodic writing is sophisticated, jazz-adjacent in its harmonic thinking but never academic. One of the more underrated acts in this space.

posts.title: Passenger
posts.writeup: Porsh Bet$ is working in a melodic rap lane that feels distinctly his own on "Passenger," with a vocal approach that leans into the hazy middle ground between singing and rapping. The production has that floating, ambient quality that pairs well with his delivery, all atmosphere and implied feeling rather than stated emotion. Promising introduction.

posts.title: Here
posts.writeup: The more direct of the two Porsh Bet$ tracks, "Here" has a warmth that "Passenger" keeps at arm's length. The hook is immediate and the production is a little fuller, giving his voice something to push against. He sounds comfortable rather than straining, which at this stage of a career means more than it might seem. Worth tracking.

posts.title: dirty dancer
posts.writeup: Philadelphia's Orion Sun makes R&B that feels like it was written in the early hours of the morning, honest in a way that can feel uncomfortable. "dirty dancer" is sensual without being obvious about it, built on a production that is warm and unhurried, with her vocal performance sitting right at the center of it. She has a naturalness to her delivery that most artists spend years trying to achieve.

posts.title: That's Not Love!
posts.writeup: Richie Quake splits the difference between indie pop and confessional R&B on a track that does not waste a single second. The production has a bright, almost naively optimistic sound that sits in tension with what he is actually singing about, and that contrast is where the song lives. The exclamation point in the title tells you everything about the energy. Feasts your ears.

posts.title: Be Alright
posts.writeup: Congee makes soft indie pop with a sensitivity that does not tip into saccharine, keeping "Be Alright" grounded even as the production reaches toward something hopeful. The guitar work is clean and simple, letting the vocal carry the emotional weight, which it does without overreaching. A quiet track that rewards multiple listens.

posts.title: Say Something
posts.writeup: bennytheghost operates in a confessional emo-adjacent space that draws equally from pop punk and bedroom indie, and "Say Something" hits that overlap cleanly. The production is stripped and direct, putting the lyrical content front and center without a lot of sonic distraction. His voice has a rawness that keeps things from feeling polished away. Early but promising.

posts.title: Halle Berry (feat. Juicy J)
posts.writeup: $NOT and Juicy J are an unlikely pairing that makes complete sense once you hear it. The production is slow and menacing, giving both rappers a canvas that rewards their most deliberate deliveries. $NOT's laid-back Florida drawl and Juicy J's Memphis-honed cadence share more DNA than you might expect. Feast your ears on this one.

posts.title: EYE EYE EYE (feat. Kevin Abstract)
posts.writeup: $NOT links with BROCKHAMPTON's Kevin Abstract for something that feels more experimental than either artist's typical output. The production has an uneasy, glitchy quality that keeps you slightly off-balance, and both vocalists lean into the discomfort rather than smoothing it over. Abstract's presence brings an emotional directness that sharpens the whole track. Genuinely surprising.

posts.title: ALONE (feat. Trippie Redd)
posts.writeup: Florida's $NOT and Trippie Redd find common ground in the fog. The production is slow and sedated, bass-heavy without being aggressive, the kind of beat that moves at 3 AM when the choices run out. Both artists trade verses that lean more on texture than technical, which is exactly right here. Keep an ear on $NOT's catalog if you haven't already.

posts.title: Hullabaloo
posts.writeup: Portland-raised rapper The Last Artful, Dodgr delivers 'Hullabaloo' like she's got something to prove and the receipts to back it up. The production is busy but controlled, percussive snaps layered under a rolling bass that keeps everything grounded. Her cadence shifts mid-verse in a way that most rappers never figure out. We've been watching her for a while and this is the best she's sounded.

posts.title: I'm a Mess
posts.writeup: Johan Lenox and Lancey Foux stack two very different energies on 'I'm a Mess' and somehow it holds together. Lenox's orchestral instincts bleed into the production even in its most stripped-back moments, while Lancey brings a loose, offhand cool that contrasts sharply with the precision underneath. The tension between those two approaches is the whole point.

posts.title: COLD SUMMER
posts.writeup: Birmingham-born Wesley Joseph makes music that sounds like it was recorded at dusk and played back in a dream. 'COLD SUMMER' rides a chopped vocal sample and a shuffling drum loop that never quite resolves, which is the right move. His voice sits somewhere between spoken word and R&B and he uses the negative space as deliberately as the notes he actually hits.

posts.title: NUMBER
posts.writeup: SLOE JACK rolls into 'NUMBER' with the kind of confidence that doesn't need to announce itself. The production is slick without being overworked, a bed of synths and programmed drums that gives his vocals room to breathe. He's operating in a lane that sits between pop and rap without fully committing to either, and that in-between space is where the interesting stuff happens. Keep a close eye on what comes next.

posts.title: Reasons
posts.writeup: narou moves quiet. 'Reasons' builds around a gentle acoustic loop and understated percussion, nothing forced, nothing wasted. The vocal delivery is patient, letting the lyrics land without overselling them. This is the kind of track that earns more on the third listen than the first.

posts.title: ICE COLA
posts.writeup: BEAM's 'ICE COLA' is smooth gospel-adjacent R&B with a clean, unhurried production style that lets the melody carry the weight. The track is built on warmth rather than intensity, with a vocal performance that sounds effortless even when it's doing the most. BEAM has been quietly putting together one of the more understated projects in this space.

posts.title: Few Good Things (feat. Black Thought and Eryn Allen Kane)
posts.artist: Saba, Black Thought, Eryn Allen Kane
posts.writeup: Saba brought Black Thought and Eryn Allen Kane onto 'Few Good Things' and nobody sounds out of place. The Chicago rapper's writing has always been precise and measured, and the album's live-instrument production gives every verse room to breathe. Black Thought arrives like he always does, unhurried and immovable, while Kane's vocal turns the outro into something you want to sit inside. One of the stronger collabs you'll hear.

posts.title: 2012 (feat. Day Wave)
posts.writeup: Saba links up with Day Wave's Jackson Phillips for a track that sounds like it was pulled from a specific moment in early 2010s indie-R&B and brought forward without losing what made it good. The guitar tones are warm and slightly washed, and Saba's flow adjusts naturally to meet the mood. Nostalgia handled with care rather than cheap sentiment.

posts.title: BLESSINGS
posts.writeup: BEAM's 'BLESSINGS' leans fully into the gospel well he draws from across this record. The track is open and spare, built around a vocal performance that escalates without straining. There's a sincerity here that's hard to fake and harder to sustain. BEAM sustains it.

posts.title: OH SLEEP
posts.writeup: Soft and searching, 'OH SLEEP' finds BEAM at his most contemplative. The production is minimal, a slow-building arrangement that trusts the vocal entirely. He's working through something real and not packaging it for effect, which makes the difference.

posts.title: 2ND SAMUEL (feat. Papa San)
posts.writeup: BEAM pulls in Jamaican reggae legend Papa San for '2ND SAMUEL' and the pairing is more natural than it might read on paper. The track leans into its gospel-reggae roots without making a show of it, Papa San's weathered flow adding a generational weight that BEAM's vocals build off of. Spiritual without being preachy.

posts.title: SUNDOWN (feat. Justin Bieber)
posts.writeup: Bieber shows up on 'SUNDOWN' and, credit where it's due, he doesn't overpower the record. BEAM's production keeps things humble and intentional, with a slow groove that serves the song rather than the feature. The two voices share something tonally and it shows.

posts.title: HURT PEOPLE (feat. Zacari)
posts.writeup: Zacari and BEAM trade lines on 'HURT PEOPLE' over a production that keeps a low simmer throughout. Zacari is one of the more criminally underused voices in R&B right now, and BEAM gives him space to actually do something with the canvas. The track is honest about damage without wallowing in it.

posts.title: WORKING
posts.writeup: 'WORKING' is BEAM stripping back to the essential. Minimal production, deliberate pacing, a vocal that doesn't reach for anything it hasn't earned. This is the kind of track that sounds better coming out of a single small speaker than a full system.

posts.title: 30mgs (feat. Vory)
posts.writeup: Vory's voice is one of the most immediately recognizable in R&B right now, and '30mgs' uses that to full effect. The track is heavy without being loud, BEAM building a bed of sound that matches the subject matter without dressing it up. One of the more emotionally direct moments on the record.

posts.title: Beach Boy
posts.writeup: New Zealand's BENEE delivers 'Beach Boy' with the kind of sideways charm that's made her one of the more singular voices in pop over the last few years. The production is sun-bleached and slightly strange, guitar textures that feel familiar but not quite right, which is exactly the vibe she's going for. Catchy in the ways that actually last.

posts.title: Doja
posts.writeup: $NOT and A$AP Rocky make 'Doja' feel effortless in the way only happens when neither artist is trying to prove a point. The beat is carefree and sun-warmed, a simple loop that just works. Rocky's verse is sharp, $NOT holds his own, and the whole thing sounds like a summer window you didn't realize was closing.

posts.title: Next To Me
posts.writeup: Jim-E Stack is one of the more interesting producers working in the space between electronic and R&B, and 'Next To Me' with Lucky Daye shows exactly why. The track is meticulous without feeling clinical, each sound placed with intent. Daye's vocal does what it always does, turns a good production into something you keep coming back to.

posts.title: Drivin
posts.writeup: Willow Avalon builds 'Drivin' around a lazy summer groove that feels like exactly what the title promises. The guitar work is warm and unhurried, the vocals floaty without losing their footing. It's uncomplicated music that doesn't need to be anything else.

posts.title: Try
posts.writeup: Nigerian-British artist Obongjayar brings a rawness to 'Try' that most pop-adjacent music has been trained out of. The production has an abrasive, percussive edge that sits underneath his vocal in a way that creates real friction, intentional friction. He's one of the more singular artists we've had our ear on, and this track is a reminder why.

posts.title: Cigarettes
posts.writeup: Post-2021, every Juice WRLD release carries extra weight, and 'Cigarettes' earns its place in the catalog. The track is built around a wiry guitar riff and the kind of melodic hook he could construct in his sleep. What's left is the feeling that there was more, which there always was.

posts.title: Spiritual
posts.writeup: Pink Sweat$ strips 'Spiritual' down to nearly nothing and lets the song hold itself up. Acoustic guitar, voice, and a writing sensibility that trusts the listener. The Philly singer has been building a catalog of slow-burn R&B that ages well, and this is one of its quieter, better moments.

posts.title: Waiting on You (feat. Sabrina Claudio)
posts.writeup: Pink Sweat$ and Sabrina Claudio are two artists who share a commitment to restraint, and 'Waiting on You' leans into that overlap fully. The production is warm and unhurried, guitar and keys giving both vocals room to sit. Neither oversells it. That's the move.

posts.title: No Fuss
posts.writeup: OG Parker is one of the architects behind a generation of Atlanta hits and 'No Fuss' with PARTYNEXTDOOR shows the other side of that skill, a track that breathes, that doesn't rush. PND's vocals are characteristically effortless, floating over a production that's designed specifically for him. Easy and right.

posts.title: NW3
posts.writeup: Bakar locks in somewhere between melancholy and momentum on NW3, named for the London postcode where he came up. The production sits loose and warm, guitars ringing out over a shuffling groove that feels more Laurel Canyon than North West London. He's always been good at writing about place without making it a postcard, and this one earns that reputation. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Push It (feat. Young Thug)
posts.writeup: NLE Choppa and Young Thug find a comfortable lane together on Push It, leaning into a slick, mid-tempo trap bounce that lets both artists breathe. Thug's verse is characteristically elastic, bending over the beat in ways that shouldn't work but do. Choppa holds his own and then some. A well-executed collab that doesn't overreach.

posts.title: Snow
posts.writeup: Benny Bellson brings a quiet, stripped-back folk sensibility to Snow, letting the acoustic guitar and his voice do most of the heavy lifting. There's a restraint here that feels intentional, the kind of song that doesn't announce itself but stays with you. Worth a few quiet listens.

posts.title: Mott St
posts.writeup: Celia Babini names this one after a street in Manhattan's Chinatown neighborhood, and the track carries that downtown energy without leaning on it too hard. Her voice sits relaxed over a breezy production that pulls from indie pop and a bit of jazz-adjacent looseness. The writing is specific in the way good city songs are. We've been watching this one.

posts.title: Living & Surfing
posts.writeup: BAD WITH PHONES delivers something genuinely sunny on Living & Surfing without tipping into parody. The guitars are bright, the energy is casual, and the whole thing moves like a Saturday with nowhere to be. If warm-weather indie pop is your thing, this is right up your alley.

posts.title: dollar signs
posts.writeup: Nemahsis is an Algonquin rapper out of Fredericton, New Brunswick, and dollar signs carries the weight of that context without making it the whole story. The production runs sparse and deliberate, giving his words room to land. He's not chasing radio and the track is better for it.

posts.title: tellmewhatuwant
posts.writeup: aldn works in that soft, late-night bedroom pop space and tellmewhatuwant is a solid entry point. The production is light and hazy, his vocals tucked into the mix in a way that feels more textural than front-and-center. Short, effective, and easy to loop.

posts.title: Nimbus
posts.writeup: Brahny builds Nimbus around a lush, orchestral-adjacent production that gives his vocals a lot of room to unfold. There's a cinematic quality here, something between a film score moment and a full R&B cut. He's got range and this track shows it without showing off.

posts.title: putdatfonedown
posts.writeup: Namou keeps it lean on putdatfonedown, a quick, percussive rap track that doesn't overstay its welcome. The beat is punchy, the flow is locked in, and the message is exactly what it says. Sometimes simple is the right move.

posts.title: Come My Way (feat. Krayzie Bone)
posts.writeup: Saba pulling in Krayzie Bone is a cross-generational move that actually makes sense. Come My Way rides a soulful, mid-tempo groove that feels like it belongs to both of them, Saba's measured Chicago lyricism sitting comfortably next to Krayzie's unmistakable cadence. The production ties it together without forcing it. One of the more interesting collabs we've heard lately.

posts.title: thank you song
posts.writeup: FKA twigs strips things down on thank you song, leaning into a fragile, piano-and-voice intimacy that cuts differently than the more produced material on CAPRISONGS. It's a direct expression of gratitude that earns its sentiment because she doesn't over-explain it. Brief and quietly devastating.

posts.title: jealousy (feat. rema)
posts.writeup: Rema brings the Afrobeats warmth and twigs brings the cool, and jealousy finds a genuinely interesting middle ground between them. The production runs light and hypnotic, their voices playing off each other in ways that feel effortless. A pairing that shouldn't work on paper but clearly does.

posts.title: lightbeamers
posts.writeup: lightbeamers hits harder than most of what twigs has put out recently. The beat is dense and physical, the vocals layered and processed in her signature way, and the energy builds without losing control. This is the experimental side of her catalog that keeps us coming back.

posts.title: P power (feat. Drake)
posts.writeup: Gunna and Drake are built for each other and P power makes that obvious from the jump. The production is luxurious and unhurried, both artists in full cruise-control mode over a beat that doesn't need to try hard. Gunna holds his ground next to Drake, which not everyone can say.

posts.title: Rapture in Blue
posts.writeup: Cautious Clay continues to operate in his own lane on Rapture in Blue, blending jazz-informed production with R&B song structure in a way that feels earned rather than studied. The track is warm and melancholic, his voice carrying just enough tension to keep it interesting. He's one of the more consistent artists working right now.

posts.title: DYE
posts.writeup: Brevin Kim is still building her catalog and DYE is a good reason to keep watching. The production floats somewhere between indie pop and R&B, and her voice has a clarity to it that cuts through without effort. Quiet confidence on record.

posts.title: idk that bitch (feat. G Herbo)
posts.writeup: Gunna and G Herbo don't seem like an obvious pairing but idk that bitch makes it work. Herbo's street directness sits interestingly next to Gunna's melodic trap ease, and the beat keeps both of them grounded. A collab that delivers more than the sum of its parts.

posts.title: how you did that (feat. Kodak Black)
posts.writeup: how you did that is one of the harder-leaning tracks on DS4EVER, Kodak Black's presence pushing Gunna into a slightly rawer register than usual. The production is still polished but there's an edge here. Kodak's verse lands.

posts.title: Is There Someone Else?
posts.writeup: The Weeknd goes full retro-pop on Is There Someone Else?, the production sitting squarely in '80s synth territory without feeling like a costume. The emotional core is classic Abel, that specific kind of ache dressed up in bright production. Dawn FM is one of his better sequenced records and this is a key piece of it.

posts.title: Here We Go… Again (feat. Tyler, the Creator)
posts.writeup: Tyler, the Creator showing up on a Weeknd track is the kind of left-field move that pays off. Here We Go Again is one of the more playful moments on Dawn FM, Tyler's verse bringing a looseness that contrasts well against Abel's controlled cool. The production bridges both of their worlds in a way that feels genuinely collaborative.

posts.title: Sacrifice
posts.writeup: Sacrifice is pure disco-influenced pop executed at a high level. The production lifts from the right places without being derivative, and Abel's vocal performance is locked in from start to finish. It was one of the most streamed tracks from Dawn FM for good reason.

posts.title: south to west
posts.writeup: south to west is a slower, more atmospheric Gunna cut, the production building space around his vocals rather than pushing the tempo. It's the kind of track that rewards headphones and patience. A good showcase of how much he's grown as a melodic rapper.

posts.title: private island
posts.writeup: private island leans fully into the luxury rap lane Gunna has made his own. The production is polished and unhurried, the writing matching the vibe without apology. Not his most complex work but it doesn't need to be.

posts.title: mop (feat. Young Thug)
posts.writeup: Gunna and Young Thug together are always worth a listen and mop is no exception. The chemistry is easy, both artists passing energy back and forth over a slick, melodic trap beat. One of the tighter collabs from the DS4EVER era.

posts.title: pushin P (feat. Young Thug)
posts.writeup: pushin P became a cultural moment and the track still holds up outside of the meme. The production is clean and confident, Future and Thug's presence pushing Gunna to deliver one of his clearest hooks in years. The green hat energy is baked into every bar.

posts.title: Feelwitchu
posts.writeup: Tennyson operates somewhere between jazz-trained and bedroom-electronic, and 'Feelwitchu' sits right at that intersection. The Canadian duo layers warm Rhodes chords over stuttered percussion, everything breathing with that half-time swing they do better than anyone. It's intimate without being delicate. Keep an ear on these two.

posts.title: All Good
posts.writeup: Kiddo Dust keeps it close to the chest on 'All Good,' riding a minimal beat with a quiet confidence that doesn't need to announce itself. The production leaves plenty of space, which is where the vocal does its work. An understated one, worth sitting with.

posts.title: Wave Gods
posts.writeup: Nas, A$AP Rocky, and DJ Premier in the same room is exactly as loaded as it sounds. Premier's loop is vintage crate-dig, gritty and warm, and Nas sounds sharper than he has any right to after three decades. Rocky adds the lean without undermining the weight. This is for the heads.

posts.title: Heaven
posts.writeup: Tierra Whack continues to do whatever she wants, and 'Heaven' is proof she's operating in her own lane entirely. The production is airy and off-kilter, and her delivery bounces between playful and pointed without ever losing the thread. Philly's most unpredictable voice stays that way.

posts.title: The Best Part of Life
posts.writeup: SAINt JHN has always leaned into the tension between dark subject matter and melodic hooks, and 'The Best Part of Life' threads that needle cleanly. The track moves with a slow-burn urgency, the production atmospheric and cinematic. Brooklyn-via-Guyana with global ambition.

posts.title: By Any Means
posts.writeup: 6LACK's 'By Any Means' is the kind of slow R&B cut that gets better the quieter you play it. The vocal sits low in the mix, conversational and worn, over a sparse beat that knows when to stay out of the way. 'East Atlanta Love Letter' era energy, refined.

posts.title: Rent Free
posts.writeup: 'Rent Free' works the same late-night frequency 6LACK has made his own, all minimal production and emotional honesty. The phrasing is direct, no poetry for poetry's sake. Just the thought you can't shake, put to music.

posts.title: tears in the club (feat. the weeknd)
posts.writeup: FKA twigs pulled The Weeknd into her world on this one, not the other way around. 'Tears in the Club' is all sharp angles and processed vulnerability, the production cutting like glass over a low pulse. Her vocal control here is unreal. From 'M3LL155X' forward she's been untouchable.

posts.title: 25 million
posts.writeup: Roddy Ricch arrived at the top of 'Please Excuse Me' with a lot to prove on the follow-up, and '25 million' opens with that weight front and center. The Compton flex comes through in the cadence, the production warm and heavy. He's processing success the same way he processed the come-up.

posts.title: don’t i (feat. Gunna)
posts.writeup: Gunna and Roddy share a melodic pocket on 'don't i' that makes the collab feel natural rather than engineered. The beat drifts, both artists floating over it without fighting for space. Easy replay value, nothing wasted.

posts.title: murda one (feat. Fivio Foreign)
posts.writeup: Fivio Foreign brings Brooklyn drill energy into Roddy's West Coast melodic world, and the contrast works. The production is darker than most of the album, the 808s heavier. Fivio sounds like he's been sharpening his pen for exactly this kind of feature slot.

posts.title: crash the party
posts.writeup: Roddy keeps the energy high on 'crash the party,' the production punchy with that synthetic shimmer he runs through consistently on this record. The hook lands without having to try. It's one of the looser, more celebratory cuts on the project and earns it.

posts.title: hibachi (feat. Kodak Black & 21 Savage)
posts.writeup: Three of the most distinct voices in Southern rap on one track. Kodak's cadence is immediately recognizable, 21 brings the ice-cold delivery, and Roddy holds the melodic center. The production is minimal enough to let all three breathe. Heavy.

posts.title: rollercoastin
posts.writeup: 'Rollercoastin' is Roddy at his most melodic, the production airy and slightly hypnotic. He's not trying to do too much here. The emotion carries the track and the simplicity is the point. A quiet standout from the project.

posts.title: all good (feat. Future)
posts.writeup: Future and Roddy Ricch vibing over a slow, atmospheric beat is exactly as effortless as it sounds. Both artists work best when the production gives them room, and this one does. Future's signature auto-tune sits against Roddy's natural rasp without either crowding the other.

posts.title: Adorn - Version 4
posts.writeup: Isaia Huron's take on Miguel's 'Adorn' strips the original to its bones and rebuilds it with a rawer, less polished warmth. His voice has a genuine ache to it, and the sparse arrangement keeps every imperfection intact. The kind of cover that makes you hear the original differently.

posts.title: Make a Mall
posts.writeup: Preme gets PARTYNEXTDOOR on the hook and the result is the kind of Toronto-tinged R&B that made OVO's run feel untouchable. The beat is brooding and minimal, PND's falsetto doing exactly what it always does. Worth the listen.

posts.title: Good Thanks
posts.writeup: Australian rapper Allday has been quietly building a catalog that sounds nothing like where he's from. 'Good Thanks' is breezy and self-aware, the production sitting somewhere between bedroom pop and hip-hop. Light, but not throwaway.

posts.title: Sky Song
posts.writeup: 'Sky Song' floats on ambient guitar and a vocal that barely touches the ground. Cayo Coco is working in the space between indie folk and dream pop, and the execution here is gentle without being thin. Right up your alley if you're drawn to the quieter corners of the catalog.

posts.title: sipping on tea
posts.writeup: Sfven keeps the vibe low and warm on 'sipping on tea,' the production built around a simple loop that loops in the best way. The vocal delivery is casual and unhurried. It's a mood more than a statement, but it's a good one.

posts.title: In My Head
posts.writeup: ROYBOY works the melodic rap space with a sensitivity that doesn't feel forced. 'In My Head' rides a clean, mid-tempo beat with enough emotional weight to stick past the first play. An artist worth watching as the catalog builds.

posts.title: AMERICAN HORROR STORY
posts.writeup: EARTHGANG brings Atlanta weirdness to 'AMERICAN HORROR STORY,' the production lurching and unpredictable, the energy theatrical. Olu and WowGr8 have never been easy to pin down and this one doubles down on that. From Mirrorland forward they've been one of the more interesting acts in their city.

posts.title: Eyes Open (feat. Lil Baby & Young Thug)
posts.artist: PnB Rock, Young Thug, Lil Baby
posts.writeup: PnB Rock, Lil Baby, and Young Thug stacking hooks is almost unfair. 'Eyes Open' lives in that melodic trap pocket all three artists own, the production layered and smooth. The late Philly artist was on a run in this period and this track holds up.

posts.title: One in a Million
posts.writeup: Isaia Huron's 'One in a Million' pulls from the classic soul lineage without feeling derivative. The production is warm and restrained, and his vocal sits in a register that's immediately comfortable. We've been watching him closely. He's the real thing.

posts.title: Lavender Haze
posts.writeup: Montgomery's 'Lavender Haze' is all shimmer and restraint, the production gauzy and melodic in a way that sits somewhere between indie pop and dream R&B. The vocal is soft but present. A pleasant discovery from the deeper end of the playlist.

posts.title: Can I?
posts.writeup: Lance Redeker keeps it airy and understated on "Can I?" — soft acoustic guitar, a voice that floats just above a whisper. The production is minimal to the point of transparent, letting the emotion carry the weight. It's the kind of track you put on when the room needs to breathe. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: SEQUOIA
posts.writeup: Dylan Kidd's "SEQUOIA" builds like a slow exhale, layering gauzy synths over a beat that never rushes. The production has that Pacific Northwest patience to it — wide open, unhurried, letting each note settle. His voice sits deep in the mix and it works. A name worth remembering.

posts.title: Mess
posts.writeup: "Mess" moves on a bed of pitched-down keys and a trap-leaning rhythm that keeps things from getting too soft. Deyaz leans into the chaos of the lyric without overselling it, which is exactly right. The production choices are smart for someone operating this quietly. Watch this space.

posts.title: Lose My Cool (feat. NLE Choppa)
posts.writeup: 070 Shake navigates between fever-dream production and a surprisingly grounded NLE Choppa verse on "Lose My Cool." The beat floats and stutters in that signature Shake way, all warped textures and emotional pressure. NLE brings a different energy that shouldn't work, but does. These two occupy different corners of the room and it makes the track interesting.

posts.title: LIFE IS CHANGING
posts.writeup: DESTIN CONRAD has been building something real for a minute, and "LIFE IS CHANGING" is one of his better moments. Silky R&B production with a modern edge, the kind of track that sounds expensive without being showy. His voice is in a comfortable pocket here. LA's watching this one closely.

posts.title: MISSED CALLS
posts.writeup: "MISSED CALLS" works in the space between melodic rap and straight R&B without committing to either, which gives it room to breathe. Khi Infinite's delivery is controlled, the production clean and unhurried. The concept is familiar but the execution earns it. Worth a spin.

posts.title: Build Me a Way - Single Version
posts.writeup: Bakar is one of those artists who sounds like he's making music for a slightly better version of the world. "Build Me a Way" is all warm guitar tones and open-road energy, his voice carrying that raw sincerity that's hard to manufacture. The South London soul-rock thing fits him like a second skin.
