We've been watching Bakar for a while and he keeps delivering.

posts.title: Spacing Out
posts.writeup: "Spacing Out" does exactly what the title promises, drifting through cloud-rap territory on a beat that feels like it's slowly dissolving. Sylo's delivery is hazy and intentional, the vocal production matching the mood without overdoing it. Good music for the late hours.

posts.title: Nine Stories
posts.writeup: Hazel English has always understood the balance between warmth and ache, and "Nine Stories" sits in that sweet spot. Jangly indie-pop guitars, a melody you catch on first listen, vocals that feel both close and distant at once. It's bittersweet in all the right ways. Right up your alley if you're a fan of Alvvays or early Japanese Breakfast.

posts.title: Lamb Cake
posts.writeup: Keith Cameron and fijitrip build a hazy, late-night mood on "Lamb Cake" that works on instinct more than architecture. The beat is low and warm, the energy deliberate. It's the kind of track that reveals more with each listen rather than front-loading everything.

posts.title: Hydroplane
posts.writeup: "Hydroplane" drifts through a smoky, low-lit space on nothing but atmosphere and restraint. The Daniel Novello collaboration brings something textural to Cameron's sound, adding depth without cluttering. Not a lot of artists can make quiet feel this full.

posts.title: Feel Better Soon
posts.writeup: Keith Cameron keeps the streak alive on "Feel Better Soon" — same hazy palette as the rest of the Hydroplane project, but with a slightly more direct emotional center. The production is intimate, the intent clear. Three cuts from this EP made the list for good reason.

posts.title: Siamese
posts.writeup: "Siamese" is dreamy in the way that actually sticks, built on soft synths and a vocal performance that pulls double duty as melody and texture. Brahny writes like someone who's spent time with the music they love and figured out something personal to say. The track earns its runtime without overstaying it.

posts.title: Sell My Soul
posts.writeup: Olive Amun comes in with an R&B edge that has some weight to it on "Sell My Soul." The production leans dark, the vocal delivery controlled and intentional. There's a restraint here that suggests someone with more in the tank. Keep an ear on where this goes.

posts.title: Destroy Myself Just For You
posts.writeup: Montell Fish operates in his own quiet corner of the internet and "Destroy Myself Just For You" is another reminder of why people follow him there. Sparse production, a voice that sounds like it's confessing more than performing, raw and unguarded. The lo-fi quality isn't a limitation, it's the point.

posts.title: Hate To Hate You
posts.writeup: Amanda Cy wraps contradiction in a hook that goes down easy on "Hate To Hate You." The production from the Ugh! EP is bright and airy, her voice sitting right up front with a confidence that feels earned. Pop writing with enough specificity to give it some grip.

posts.title: Jonesin'
posts.writeup: "Jonesin'" has that restless indie energy that keeps bouncing between anxious and joyful, never quite settling. Worry Club builds tension and release into the structure without making it feel mechanical. The guitar work is sharp, the songwriting tighter than the lo-fi aesthetic suggests. A band worth following.

posts.title: I Find God
posts.writeup: Doublethink makes music that operates at the intersection of alternative hip-hop and something harder to name. "I Find God" has a searching quality to it, the production pushing and pulling under a lyric that takes the subject seriously. Ambitious for a relatively early release.

posts.title: dread ahead
posts.writeup: "dread ahead" is a slow-burning thing, built on unease and a beat that never fully commits to resolution. Jack Slade and Sorry make good use of the tension, letting the track sit in discomfort rather than resolve it cheaply. The collaboration is tight and focused.

posts.title: The Dress
posts.writeup: Dijon's "The Dress" arrives from the Absolutely album and it's one of the standout moments on a project full of them. Lush, intimate production that nods to classic soul without mimicking it, his voice sitting right at the center of everything. He writes with a clarity about feeling that most artists spend years chasing. Keep a close ear on Dijon.

posts.title: To Be Loved
posts.writeup: "To Be Loved" is the emotional anchor of 30 and one of Adele's finest vocal performances on record. The arrangement strips back to piano and orchestration, leaving nowhere to hide, and she doesn't need it. A reminder that when she commits to a moment, there's nobody better at this.

posts.title: Champagne Shots
posts.writeup: Sainté has a UK sound that travels well and "Champagne Shots" is a good example of why. The production on Local MVP is smooth and assured, built for replay without feeling disposable. His vocal tone has a natural warmth that makes even the celebratory moments feel grounded.

posts.title: Tweet Bird (Never Broke Again feat. YoungBoy Never Broke Again, Rojay MLP, Meechy Baby, P Yungin, Rjae)
posts.artist: Never Broke Again, YoungBoy Never Broke Again, Rojay MLP, Meechy Baby, P Yungin, RJAE
posts.writeup: YoungBoy's crew assembles for "Tweet Bird" and the result is a posse cut that actually holds together across six voices. The beat gives everyone room and nobody overstays their welcome, which is harder to pull off than it sounds. YB's presence looms even when he's not leading, and that's just how it goes with this camp.

posts.title: Rounds
posts.writeup: "Rounds" from No Refunds is Whookilledkenny doing what he does best: hard production, sharp bars, a hook that lands clean. The energy doesn't let up, the pacing is tight. This is the lane he's built and he moves through it with confidence.

posts.title: Rolling Stone
posts.writeup: Oliver Malcolm writes like someone with something to prove and "Rolling Stone" is a good case for paying attention. The guitar-forward production has warmth without being nostalgic, his voice a little rough at the edges in the best way. UK indie-pop with real backbone. We've had our eye on Malcolm for a while.

posts.title: Wisdom Tooth
posts.writeup: Wallice has a knack for writing songs that sound casual and cut deep at the same time. 'Wisdom Tooth' leans into that comfort zone sonically, indie guitar textures sitting under a melody that bends toward self-examination. The LA-based songwriter's delivery stays unhurried, almost conversational, which makes the ache land harder. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: Dizzy (feat. Thomas Headon and Alfie Templeman)
posts.artist: chloe moriondo, Thomas Headon, Alfie Templeman
posts.writeup: Three of the more interesting young voices in guitar-pop sharing one track, and it holds up. chloe moriondo, Thomas Headon, and Alfie Templeman trade verses over production that's fizzy without being frantic. 'Dizzy' earns its title through momentum rather than chaos, all jangling strings and sugar-rush hooks that feel genuinely collaborative rather than stitched together.

posts.title: Summer Rain (feat. Jazmine Sullivan)
posts.writeup: Leon Bridges and Jazmine Sullivan is one of those pairings that should've existed years ago. 'Summer Rain' is unhurried gospel-soul, both voices doing exactly what they do best: Bridges warm and understated, Sullivan with more power in one phrase than most singers manage in a full song. The production gives them room. They use every inch of it.

posts.title: Black Illuminati (feat. Jadakiss)
posts.writeup: Freddie Gibbs rapping over a track that sounds like it was recorded in a dim backroom, paranoid and heavy, inviting Jadakiss to match that energy. Both MCs are in full command here, trading verses with the kind of no-frills menace that's rare outside of peak-era Def Jam. 'Black Illuminati' doesn't oversell anything. It doesn't need to.

posts.title: Crystal Ball
posts.writeup: Lila Drew writes pop with a specificity that most writers twice her age haven't found yet. 'Crystal Ball' runs on restrained production that prioritizes her vocal, clear-eyed and steady, delivering lyrics about uncertainty without any of the usual melodrama. She's one to watch closely.

posts.title: Unfamiliar Faces
posts.writeup: Chloe Parche works in a space between bedroom pop and something more atmospheric, and 'Unfamiliar Faces' sits right at that threshold. The production is sparse enough that every sound registers, and her vocal has a hushed intimacy that pulls you in without effort. An early signal from an artist still finding her ceiling.

posts.title: LONDON
posts.writeup: Tariq Disu makes London feel like a character, not just a location. The track carries the weight of distance and memory, production that breathes slowly, his vocal sitting on top of it with clarity and restraint. There's an emotional intelligence to this one that goes beyond the geography.

posts.title: Viagra
posts.writeup: Celia Babini gets right to the point. The production on 'Viagra' is sardonic and propulsive, the kind of alt-pop that winks without blinking, and her delivery treats the subject with the exact right amount of seriousness: not much. Sharp and self-aware, which is harder to pull off than it looks.

posts.title: lowlife - with kenny beats
posts.writeup: Kenny Beats building a track around not dvr's energy is a natural fit. 'lowlife' is claustrophobic in the best way, drums that hit hard and immediate, not dvr's voice sitting in the low end of its register. The collaboration doesn't feel like a feature so much as two people sharing the same wavelength from the jump.

posts.title: stupid
posts.writeup: The other not dvr cut in the batch, and it earns its own space. 'stupid' strips things back further, rawer production letting the emotional weight carry more of the load. If 'lowlife' is the hook, this is the bruise.

posts.title: Track Star
posts.writeup: Willy P and Peter Kuli on a track that sounds built for the road: punchy, wide-open, the kind of pop-adjacent record that works in headphones and out of a speaker on a sunny day equally well. 'Track Star' has momentum baked into its DNA, and it doesn't let up.

posts.title: HOW RUDE
posts.writeup: Aries is working in a space where pop-punk DNA gets filtered through a more contemporary production lens, and 'HOW RUDE' gets that balance right. There's genuine attitude here without posturing, guitars that feel earned rather than retrofitted, and a hook that sticks on the first pass.

posts.title: Keep My Spirit Alive pt 2
posts.writeup: From the Donda sessions, this one hit differently when it leaked: raw in construction but sharp in intention. The sample flips and layered vocals are vintage Kanye at the compositional level, the spiritual anxiety running through it as present as anything on the album proper. It's a fragment that feels complete.

posts.title: Life Of The Party (with André 3000)
posts.writeup: Andre 3000 doesn't show up often, so when he does, you pay attention. 'Life Of The Party' is Kanye at his most restrained, making space for Andre to deliver a verse that's quietly one of his best in years, oblique and searching and alive. The production lets it breathe. We've played this one more than once.

posts.title: You Up?
posts.writeup: Johan Lenox works at the intersection of classical training and contemporary production, and 'You Up?' with Ant Clemons shows that range without making a spectacle of it. Strings that feel composed rather than decorated, vocal performance that's warm and precise. The whole thing has a late-night quality that rewards attention.

posts.title: Doctor, My Eyes
posts.writeup: Khamari taking on the Jackson Browne standard and not flinching. His version strips some of the original's buoyancy and leans into the disillusionment instead, his voice carrying the weight of the lyric with enough grit to make it his own. The arrangement is spare and deliberate. It works.

posts.title: then i met her
posts.writeup: EKKSTACY builds in a lane where emo-pop and lo-fi production share the same walls, and 'then i met her' is him at his most melodically direct. The distortion is baked in but controlled, the sentiment is earnest without being overwrought. Short and to the point.

posts.title: NAVIG8
posts.writeup: Downtown Kayoto is building a sound that leans into electronic textures without losing the organic core. 'NAVIG8' moves like something designed to keep you moving, rhythm-forward and locked in, with enough melodic detail to hold your attention between the drops. Worth following.

posts.title: I Deserve (w/ NOS.)
posts.writeup: Smino doesn't miss often, and 'I Deserve' with NOS. is another reminder why. His cadence alone is worth the listen, floating over production that blends jazz-adjacent chords with a contemporary low end. NOS. holds their own in the collaborative space. Warm and lived-in.

posts.title: i met the devil at 4 years old
posts.writeup: The title tells you where this is going emotionally, and flowerkid doesn't pull back from it. The production is understated, letting the narrative carry the weight, which takes confidence in the writing. It pays off. An artist processing something real in real time.

posts.title: i been up to things i don't think i should be.
posts.writeup: The title is a thesis statement, and kid apollo delivers on it with production that sounds like late-night second-guessing. Guitar tones that blur at the edges, vocal that's conversational and specific. The self-awareness in the writing is what separates this from the crowd.

posts.title: I Want U
posts.writeup: No Rome has been one of the more consistent names in bedroom-adjacent guitar pop, and 'I Want U' is him comfortable in his zone: clean tone guitars, a melody that sounds effortless, production that's shiny without being slick. Right up your alley if you have any love for that early Beabadoobee or Phum Viphurit pocket.

posts.title: Where Do You Go
posts.writeup: Day Wave's Jackson Phillips has been quietly refining this sound for years. 'Where Do You Go' is shoegaze-tinted indie pop done with precision, layers of guitar sitting under a vocal that's distant in that way that somehow makes things feel closer. Consistent and quietly excellent.

posts.title: Crashed My Bike
posts.writeup: The title is disarming, and so is the song. Keith Cameron writes with a specificity that roots the emotional stuff in something physical and recognizable, and 'Crashed My Bike' follows that instinct well. Melodic, light on its feet, the kind of track that earns a second play before the first one ends.

posts.title: New Shapes (feat. Christine and the Queens and Caroline Polachek)
posts.artist: Charli xcx, Christine and the Queens, Caroline Polachek
posts.writeup: Three of the most adventurous women in pop on one track, and none of them play it safe. 'New Shapes' is a study in restraint meeting ambition: the production has space, the vocal trade-offs feel genuinely considered, and Caroline Polachek's contribution in particular is one of the best verses she put on a feature in this era. A document of a moment in pop that was genuinely exciting.

posts.title: MAFIA
posts.writeup: MAFIA landed as the B-side to ESCAPE PLAN and it was the better half. Where ESCAPE PLAN leaned into atmosphere, this one has a harder bounce, 808s compressed tight with a percussive tag that hits like a snare flush. Travis is at cruise control here but that's still a higher ceiling than most. Fits exactly where he was at the tail end of 2021.

posts.title: Jumpin (feat. Polo G)
posts.writeup: NLE Choppa and Polo G share an energy that makes sense on paper and even more in practice here. The beat is minimal Memphis-adjacent with hi-hat rolls and a low-end that leaves room for both rappers to occupy their own space. Polo's verse is precise as usual, Choppa holds his own with more control than he typically showed at this stage. Solid collab.

posts.title: No Love (with SZA)
posts.writeup: "Still Over It" is one of the most cohesive R&B projects of that era and "No Love" is one of its anchors. Summer Walker and SZA both operate in that raw, unguarded vocal register and here they feed off each other rather than compete. The production is understated, just enough bed to support two voices that don't need much else. SZA's presence elevates the whole thing another notch.

posts.title: Talk Down
posts.writeup: Dijon has an old-soul ache in his voice that makes almost everything he touches feel lived-in. "Talk Down" pulls from classic soul structure but the production is too restrained to feel nostalgic, it just feels honest. The guitar work here is subtle but it's doing a lot. One of the stronger cuts off "Absolutely."

posts.title: Annie
posts.writeup: "Annie" is Dijon leaning fully into warmth without losing any of the weight. The arrangement is spare and intimate, vocal sat close in the mix so you catch every inflection. It doesn't try to be a big song. It earns its feeling in smaller ways, which is exactly the point. We've been watching Dijon for a while now and "Absolutely" delivered.

posts.title: I Dare You
posts.writeup: Swansea Skag comes out swinging on "I Dare You" with a raw indie rock energy that doesn't waste any runway. The guitar tones are dirty in exactly the right way, rhythm section locked tight, vocals sitting just slightly above controlled. Low profile right now but the bones are there. Worth flagging early.

posts.title: ‎telephone ‎wires
posts.writeup: "telephone wires" is a quiet, introspective indie-folk track from maxime. that earns its lowercase aesthetic. The production is minimal, acoustic guitar and a voice that's searching for something specific. There's a real intimacy to it, like catching a rough demo that doesn't need any polish. Right up your alley if you like your discoveries small and genuine.

posts.title: Process
posts.writeup: Cabu teams up with Milan Ring and Ta-ku for something that sits comfortably in that melodic electronic space all three have carved out. "Process" is smooth in the way that rewards repeat listens rather than first impressions. The production layering is careful without being overworked, and Milan Ring's vocal contribution ties the whole thing together. Good company to be in.

posts.title: someone else
posts.writeup: gcmayn keeps it understated on "someone else," a bedroom-pop track built more on texture than event. The guitar is fingerpicked and dry, the production doesn't crowd the space, and the vocal performance sits right in the pocket of whatever feeling the song is trying to name. Early catalog, low profile, but the songwriting instinct is real.

posts.title: CANDY
posts.writeup: Che Ecru brings a confident R&B energy to "CANDY" from the "Til Death (Deluxe)" project. The groove is sticky and deliberate, built for replay, with a vocal delivery that knows exactly where it wants to land. Ecru has a smoothness that doesn't feel borrowed from anyone specific. Worth the watch.

posts.title: Demon High
posts.writeup: "Demon High" caught Uzi in a transitional moment, bridging the Eternal Atake era toward whatever came next. The production is characteristically alien, synth work pitched and bent in ways that match his vocal cadence better than most beats he'd been on that year. More melodic than aggressive, which suited him here.

posts.title: Polaroid
posts.writeup: Leo Bhanji writes with a specificity that distinguishes him from the indie-pop crowd. "Polaroid" is built on clean guitar and a vocal performance that doesn't oversell anything. The "Arm's Length" project introduced him as someone with a real handle on restraint, and this track is a good entry point. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Window Up
posts.writeup: "Window Up" is the harder-edged companion to Bhanji's quieter material on "Arm's Length," a little more direct in both production and lyrical approach. The rhythm here is more assertive than his usual mode, and it suits him. Both tracks from this project made the list for a reason.

posts.title: not a summer song
posts.writeup: Namou leans into the title with a track that actually delivers on the anti-summer premise, something muted and restrained that doesn't perform any particular season. The songwriting is understated and the production stays out of its own way. Debut-era material that signals something worth following.

posts.title: mason jar
posts.writeup: BEL keeps "mason jar" small and specific, which is exactly why it works. Acoustic-driven with a folk-adjacent warmth, the track resists any temptation to scale up. The production around it is minimal enough to feel almost private. Good example of knowing what a song needs and not adding more.

posts.title: wanna be
posts.writeup: "wanna be" is the other side of BEL's double single release and it leans slightly harder into an R&B register while keeping the same lo-fi warmth. The vocal sits differently here, a little more searching, a little more raw. Two tracks out and already showing range.

posts.title: Apollo
posts.writeup: Brahny's "Apollo" moves in that cinematic indie-soul space where the production is doing as much heavy lifting as the vocal. The track builds slowly with orchestral texture creeping in at the edges, and the payoff is earned rather than forced. Swedish artist, worth the cross-continental attention.

posts.title: Charmander
posts.writeup: Aminé dropped "Charmander" as a standalone single and it lands as a confident, playful flex that doesn't take itself too seriously. The beat is light-footed with a snap to it, and Aminé's cadence is in full command mode. He's always been more interesting than his mainstream moments suggest, and this is him doing what he does well.

posts.title: Sungazing - Bonus
posts.writeup: BLESSED is a Vancouver-based artist with a sound rooted in soul and jazz-adjacent production, and "Sungazing" from the LOCKDOWN TAPES project is a good introduction. The track breathes, built on warm tones and a measured vocal approach. Bonus tag notwithstanding, this one pulls its weight.

posts.title: Rodeo Clown
posts.writeup: Third Dijon track in this batch and the "Absolutely" LP earns that representation. "Rodeo Clown" is wry and self-aware without being arch, the kind of song that reveals more on the fourth or fifth listen. His production keeps the same woody, intimate quality throughout the record and this is no different.

Chloe Lilac captures a very specific feeling with "19," the anxious clarity of being just on the edge of something. The production is sparse and the songwriting is more precise than it has any right to be for someone documenting that particular age. Brooklyn artist who's been refining this voice for a while. The progress shows.

posts.title: WORTHLESS
posts.writeup: Maxo Kream is one of the most underrated storytellers in Houston rap and "WORTHLESS" from "WEIGHT OF THE WORLD" is him at his most reflective. The beat is somber and deliberate, giving him room to move through the emotional weight of the record without rushing anything. This project hit harder than most gave it credit for at the time.

posts.title: Distance
posts.writeup: Dritic and Lani Rose trade vocals on "Distance," a bedroom-produced R&B track that earns its intimacy. The production is soft-edged and the chemistry between the two voices carries more of the track than any single element. Low profile for now but the execution is clean.

posts.title: Do It Over
posts.writeup: WizTheMc brings a measured, introspective energy to "Do It Over," a self-produced track that sits somewhere between confessional hip-hop and indie R&B. The beat is lo-fi without leaning on that as an aesthetic crutch, and the lyricism has a honesty to it that doesn't announce itself. Early but promising.

posts.title: Call U Tomorrow
posts.writeup: Montell Fish writes gospel-infected bedroom soul like someone praying into a vocoder. "Call U Tomorrow" floats on a sparse acoustic loop with his voice stretched thin over the top, intimate and unhurried. The London singer-songwriter has been building a devoted following with this sound for years now, and this track is a good entry point. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Street You Live On
posts.writeup: Off her debut album Juno, Remi Wolf turns a simple hook into something that burrows into your head and stays. The production is peak retro-funk, all squelchy bass and clipped horns, but her vocal delivery keeps it from feeling like pastiche. She's got the kind of pop instinct that makes complicated feel effortless. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Easy On Me
posts.writeup: Adele doesn't need context. "Easy On Me" is a piano ballad that strips everything down to voice and memory, and her voice does the rest. The production is minimal by design, built to stay out of the way. Six years between albums, and the first move was to go quieter. It worked.

posts.title: Day Before (with Mac Miller)
posts.writeup: Punk's opening stretch is where Young Thug made his most melodic case, and "Day Before" with Mac Miller is one of the standouts. Mac's verse came from the vault and fits seamlessly, his cadence matching Thug's elastic phrasing in a way that feels almost eerie given the circumstances. The piano loop underneath is gorgeous. Worth the whole album.

posts.title: Hate The Game
posts.writeup: Thug working in a more contemplative mode here, his flow stretched across a mid-tempo beat that gives him room to sit inside the melody. "Hate The Game" doesn't chase a moment, it lingers. The hook is one of the more memorable ones on Punk.

posts.title: Love You More (with Nate Ruess, Gunna & Jeff Bhasker)
posts.artist: Young Thug, Gunna, Jeff Bhasker, Nate Ruess
posts.writeup: The Nate Ruess feature on Punk was a left-field swing that mostly landed. Thug and the fun. frontman orbit each other over a lush, Bhasker-produced backdrop that reads closer to pop than trap. Gunna holds down his corner without overpowering the track's emotional register. An interesting experiment in genre hospitality.

posts.title: Icy Hot (with Doja Cat)
posts.writeup: Doja Cat and Young Thug are both shape-shifters, and when they share a track the result is predictably slippery. "Icy Hot" bounces off a skittering 808 pattern with both artists playing cool and detached, their deliveries more texture than narrative. It doesn't overstay its welcome.

posts.title: Fifth Day Dead
posts.writeup: One of the more atmospheric cuts on Punk. "Fifth Day Dead" is slow-burning and slightly ominous, Thug stretching his vowels over a beat that keeps more space than usual. The restraint is what makes it memorable.

posts.title: Bubbly (with Drake & Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: The three biggest names in Atlanta-adjacent rap, 2021 edition, all showing up at their most relaxed. "Bubbly" is an easy-going banger that doesn't particularly try to be historic but ends up feeling like a document of a specific moment in the culture. The Travis hook is earworm material.

posts.title: Insure My Wrist (with Gunna)
posts.writeup: Thug and Gunna are so locked into each other's frequencies at this point that their collaborations feel less like features and more like conversations. "Insure My Wrist" is a straight flex record with some of the most fluid tag-team rapping on the album. Quick and to the point.

posts.title: Livin It Up (with Post Malone & A$AP Rocky)
posts.artist: Young Thug, A$AP Rocky, Post Malone
posts.writeup: Post Malone and A$AP Rocky both find comfortable spaces on "Livin It Up," a track that's looser and more celebratory than most of Punk. Rocky's verse in particular has some of his best cadence work in a while. Thug holds the whole thing together.

posts.title: Peepin Out The Window (with Future & Bslime)
posts.writeup: Future and Thug have been running parallel careers for over a decade, so their chemistry here is almost automatic. Bslime handles the hook with surprising confidence for his age. The beat is eerie and spare, giving everyone room to operate.

posts.title: Contagious
posts.writeup: A solo cut from Thug at his most melodically elastic. "Contagious" is the kind of track where the vocal performance is the instrument, every syllable landing just left of where you'd expect. Short, precise, no filler.

posts.title: Recognize Real (with Gunna)
posts.writeup: Gunna and Thug back at it on one of Punk's more grounded tracks. "Recognize Real" has a slight nostalgic pull in the production, the kind of beat that sounds like late nights in a studio. Both artists are comfortable, which translates directly to the listening experience.

posts.title: Stupid/Asking
posts.writeup: The two-part structure of "Stupid/Asking" is one of Punk's more deliberate moves. The first half is frantic and dense, the second strips back into something close to a lullaby. Thug at his most mercurial, and that's saying something.

posts.title: Stressed (with J. Cole & T-Shyne)
posts.writeup: J. Cole brings measured, metronomic energy to a track that could easily tip into chaos, and the contrast works. T-Shyne is a natural fit for the Young Stoner Life sound. "Stressed" is one of the album's more emotionally direct moments, the lyrics reaching past the usual flex territory.

posts.title: Die Slow (with Strick)
posts.writeup: Strick has been in Young Thug's orbit for years and this collaboration sounds earned rather than obligatory. "Die Slow" has a brooding low-end and Thug sounds genuinely invested in the storytelling. One of the album's deeper cuts worth returning to.

posts.title: Summer's Gone
posts.writeup: Casper Sage sits in that quiet melancholy space where indie folk meets lo-fi pop, his vocals hushed and close-mic'd over minimal production. "Summer's Gone" does exactly what the title suggests. One to sit with.

posts.title: GREENER KNOTS
posts.writeup: Maxo Kream raps like someone who's seen both sides and is still processing. "GREENER KNOTS" is dense and slow-moving in the best way, his cadence deliberate over a beat that gives every bar room to land. The Houston rapper has always trusted his ear for production, and this single is no exception.

posts.title: ARTIST
posts.writeup: SAIAH is an LA-based rapper and producer making music that lives somewhere between conscious rap and experimental beat work. "ARTIST" is self-referential without being navel-gazing, the production layered and deliberate. He's building a catalog worth paying attention to.

posts.title: Girls In Purgatory
posts.writeup: Julia Wolf writes pop with the emotional accuracy of someone who's been taking notes on their own bad decisions. "Girls In Purgatory" hits the sweet spot between anthemic and raw, her vocal performance carrying most of the weight. The hook is hard to shake.

posts.title: Swangin’ On Westheimer
posts.writeup: Don Toliver at his most Houston-proud. "Swangin' On Westheimer" is a lowrider anthem with melody to spare, his Auto-Tuned croon riding the beat like he was born on it. Life of a DON had a lot of these easy wins, and this one translates especially well outside of context.

posts.title: Double Standards
posts.writeup: Toliver moves into a slightly more introspective lane on "Double Standards," his voice soft and searching over a hazy beat. It's one of the more understated moments on the album, which is part of what makes it stand out in a tracklist full of bangers.

posts.title: Way Bigger
posts.writeup: "Way Bigger" is exactly the kind of Don Toliver track that made him one of Cactus Jack's most valuable players. The hook is effortless, the production airy and polished, his delivery threading the needle between rap and R&B. Easy to understand why it became one of the album's marquee singles.

posts.title: Lost Angel Nights
posts.writeup: James Blake making late-night music for the genuinely lonely. "Lost Angel Nights" is from his 2021 LP Friends That Break Your Heart and it captures him in a contemplative, open-hearted mode, piano and voice doing most of the heavy lifting. The production is restrained in a way that trusts the song completely.

posts.title: Coming Back (feat. SZA)
posts.writeup: James Blake and SZA share a rare chemistry here, both operating in that hollow, aching space where soul music and electronic minimalism overlap. Blake's production is signature: sparse piano, treated bass tones, vocals that feel like they're barely holding together. SZA brings the warmth his records often hold at arm's length. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Flocky Flocky (feat. Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: Don Toliver and Travis Scott are essentially working the same sonic territory, so when they link up it just amplifies. The production is woozy and warped, trap architecture dressed in psychedelic textures. Toliver's melodic runs sit perfectly in the pocket Scott carved years ago. If you've been sleeping on Don since Heaven or Hell, now's the time.

posts.title: Cortisol
posts.writeup: Samuel Petra writes about anxiety the way it actually feels: circular, unresolved, physically present. 'Cortisol' pairs that subject matter with indie-folk production that's deliberately understated, letting the tension sit in the negative space. There's a restraint here that most artists in this lane abandon too quickly. Watch this name.

posts.title: PTSD
posts.writeup: Headie One has been one of the most honest voices in UK drill since day one, and 'PTSD' doesn't deviate from that. The production is dark and cinematic, built for the weight of what he's saying. He doesn't dramatize the trauma, he just lays it flat, which hits harder. North London's finest, no argument.

posts.title: Tsunami
posts.writeup: Suzuki Saint deals in slow-build atmospherics, and 'Tsunami' is a good example of the patience he brings to a track. The production swells without ever breaking, which is a harder trick than it sounds. His vocals are cool and measured against it. Sydney-based and very much worth a spot on your radar.

posts.title: jogging shoes (the rerun)
posts.artist: Lola Scott, Evan Klar, Abby Bella May
posts.writeup: Lola Scott, Evan Klar, and Abby Bella May turn something as mundane as running shoes into a meditation on grief and memory. The 'rerun' framing adds texture, like they're revisiting something already lived. Soft production, emotionally precise writing. Australian indie pop with real craft behind it.

posts.title: boundary issues
posts.writeup: girlhouse writes pop-rock with a self-awareness that keeps it from feeling formulaic. 'boundary issues' is hooky and direct, guitars up front, lyrics that land without over-explaining. There's a 2000s alt influence in the sonics but the perspective is fully current. Worth a spot in the rotation.

posts.title: feelings
posts.writeup: clide operates somewhere between bedroom pop and proper singer-songwriter territory, and 'feelings' doesn't force a choice between the two. The production is warm and slightly hazy, guitar and voice doing most of the work. It's the kind of track that sounds better the third time than the first. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Hot (feat. Moneybagg Yo)
posts.writeup: Meek Mill and Moneybagg Yo are both working with the same energy here, aggressive and lean, no wasted bars. The beat carries momentum without doing too much. This is rap that works because the execution is clean, not because of any conceptual ambition. Does exactly what it's supposed to.

posts.title: Sharing Locations (feat. Lil Baby & Lil Durk)
posts.artist: Meek Mill, Lil Durk, Lil Baby
posts.writeup: Three of rap's hardest workers in 2021 sharing a track is exactly what it sounds like. Lil Baby and Lil Durk both show up with purpose, and Meek holds his own throughout. The production is polished trap, built for movement. A machine of a record.

posts.title: Feelin' Like Tunechi
posts.writeup: Wayne doing Wayne things, which means quotables and a flow that still sounds like nobody else in the game. Rich The Kid provides the right energy alongside him without overreaching. The beat is flashy in the right ways. It's been a long run, and he's still got it.

posts.title: Want It All (feat. Polo G)
posts.writeup: Burna Boy's Afrofusion sound is as confident as ever here, and Polo G slots in cleanly with a verse that has real weight. The production bridges both worlds without forcing it. We've been watching Burna ascend since 'African Giant' and this is another step up in reach. Right up your alley if you're already on him.

posts.title: Flawless
posts.writeup: Reign brings a cool, unfussy R&B energy to 'Flawless' that doesn't lean on production tricks to carry the room. The writing is direct and the delivery matches. There's a maturity in how the track breathes, nothing rushed. One to file under artists to track.

posts.title: ANSWERS
posts.writeup: Khi Infinite works in a reflective, lo-fi adjacent rap space where the beats are patient and the bars do the real lifting. 'ANSWERS' reads like someone working through something in real time, honest without being performative. The production matches the mood without overselling it. An artist with something to say.

posts.title: Sentimental
posts.writeup: GRAACE has a voice that pulls attention without demanding it, which is a hard thing to train and harder to fake. 'Sentimental' leans into that quality, pairing her vocals with production that's glossy but not cold. The emotional range is real. Australian pop done with actual artistry.

posts.title: Far Cry
posts.writeup: Wet's Kelly Zutrau writes longing the way it actually lives in the body, slow and persistent. 'Far Cry' is dream pop with sharp edges, guitars shimmer but the lyrics cut. Their catalog has always rewarded close listening, and this track is no exception. One of the more underrated acts of the decade.

posts.title: long sleeves - t-shirt mix
posts.writeup: rakey's 'long sleeves' exists in that delicate space between indie pop and something rawer, production that sounds intentionally fragile. The t-shirt mix is stripped back in a way that makes the emotional content harder to ignore. Simple and effective. An artist who understands restraint.

posts.title: Materialistic Being
posts.writeup: Tj Loft interrogates ambition and consumption without being preachy about it, which is harder than it sounds. The production is warm and unhurried, giving the lyrics room to land. There's a thoughtfulness here that separates him from the pack. Worth a closer look.

posts.title: Do You Still Dream?
posts.writeup: Julius Black asks the question directly in the title and then spends the track earning it. The production is sparse and melodic, built to hold introspection rather than distract from it. His vocal delivery has a sincerity that's easy to trust. New artist with real potential, keep an ear on.

posts.title: SW3
posts.writeup: Oscar Welsh names the track after a Chelsea postcode and delivers something that actually feels rooted in a place and time. The production is warm acoustic pop with a British melancholy running through it. His ear for melody is sharp. File under: artists who will matter more in a year.

posts.title: Glass Ceiling
posts.writeup: Matilda Mann has been delivering understated, emotionally precise indie pop for a few years and 'Glass Ceiling' might be the fullest realization of that so far. The piano-led production doesn't overcrowd her vocal, which remains the best thing in the room. Lyrically she's operating well above her years. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: BRANDY
posts.writeup: Young & Sick brings a nostalgic warmth to 'BRANDY' that doesn't feel cheap or borrowed. The production sits between classic R&B and something more contemporary, and the execution is clean throughout. His ear for texture is one of the better ones in the indie space. Worth the dig.

posts.title: Hometown Famous
posts.writeup: 99 Neighbors are a Burlington, Vermont rap collective doing something genuinely unusual, genre-fluid and communal in a way that doesn't feel manufactured. 'Hometown Famous' captures that energy well, a track about earned local credibility that has the sound to back it up. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Easy Going
posts.writeup: Kacy Hill's voice has always been the main event, and 'Easy Going' gives it the right setting: clean production, open space, nothing crowding her range. The song is deceptively simple, the kind of thing that sounds effortless because the craft is invisible. We've had our eye on her since GOOD Music, and she keeps delivering.

posts.title: Bad Days
posts.writeup: YaYa writes about struggle with a directness that doesn't tip into wallowing, which keeps 'Bad Days' listenable even when the subject matter is heavy. The production is warm enough to carry the emotion without becoming saccharine. A track for the kind of day the title describes.

posts.title: lens
posts.writeup: LORA's "lens" is the kind of track that earns its runtime in the first eight bars. Warm, close-mic'd vocals sit over understated piano and production that knows when to hold back. The London-based singer has a way of making restraint feel like presence. Keep an ear on her.

posts.title: Avalanche
posts.writeup: reggie and Smino make "Avalanche" feel effortless, two distinct voices finding a pocket and staying there. Smino's contribution pushes the track into soulful neo-R&B territory without losing reggie's conversational cool. The production breathes without calling attention to itself. Solid.

posts.title: it only gets worse, i promise
posts.writeup: EKKSTACY leans into the emo-rap lane without apology on this one. Distorted guitars and washed-out production frame vocals that are all raw feeling, minimal filter. It lands closest to the SoundCloud-era lineage but with sharper self-awareness. Not for everyone, but it's not trying to be.

posts.title: too easy
posts.writeup: Gunna and Future on the same track is a statement before a note plays. "too easy" coasts on Atlanta melodic trap fundamentals, both artists in full cruise mode over a Metro-adjacent beat. Nothing here surprises, but sometimes comfort is the point. Feast your ears.

posts.title: No Where
posts.writeup: YoungBoy operates in his own gravity on "No Where," the production spacious enough to let his cadence breathe. There's a restless energy underneath the melodic hooks that keeps it from settling too far into comfort. One of the more focused cuts in a prolific stretch.

posts.title: For Tonight
posts.writeup: GIVEON's bass-heavy baritone is a full instrument, and "For Tonight" is built around letting it dominate. The production stays skeletal — minimal percussion, soft keys, plenty of room. His Atlantic Records debut era had this restraint dialed in from jump. A standout.

posts.title: What A Waste
posts.writeup: GRACEY lands somewhere between late-night pop and alt-R&B on "What A Waste," her voice carrying the kind of earned weariness that most writers reach for and miss. Production from the UK camp that knows when to strip back. She's been building quietly; this one rewards the attention.

posts.title: No Doubt
posts.writeup: RIZ LA VIE has been putting in work on the West Coast indie side for a minute, and "No Doubt" shows why we've been watching. Warm guitar tones, easy pocket groove, vocals that sit in the mix like they belong there. The kind of track that soundtracks drives you don't want to end.

posts.title: Fire
posts.writeup: Glasgow's Joesef writes about feeling with a directness that doesn't need to be loud. "Fire" is all warm R&B production and a vocal performance that earns every bar. He's drawing comparisons to the likes of Arlo Parks and rightly so, but the voice is entirely his own.

posts.title: Jersey City
posts.writeup: Golden Vessel pairs his signature soft-synth production with rei so la on "Jersey City" and the result is exactly what you'd hope. Hazy, intimate electronic pop with a vocal performance that drifts in the best way. The Australian producer has a consistent aesthetic and this track sits comfortably in it.

posts.title: Something To Hold
posts.writeup: The Shadowboxers bring their blue-eyed soul credentials to "Something To Hold," all layered harmonies and slick production that nods at classic pop without feeling nostalgic. They've toured with Justin Timberlake for a reason. This is the kind of music that makes you check your own pulse.

posts.title: White Skies
posts.writeup: Tanisako keeps things minimal and moody on "White Skies," placing a fragile vocal over production that knows how to use space. There's a kind of Nordic stillness to the arrangement that makes it sit differently than most indie-pop. Worth a few listens.

posts.title: Lemonhaze
posts.writeup: PawPaw Rod has carved out a corner of rap that feels personal without being precious. "Lemonhaze" is relaxed, a little hazy, with the kind of flow that sounds easy until you try to replicate it. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Flipside
posts.writeup: New Zealand's LEISURE have always been right up your alley if your alley runs through soft funk and slow-burn R&B. "Flipside" is warm production with a groove that doesn't rush. The collective sound is dialed in after years of work, and this cut reflects that polish.

posts.title: Fragrance
posts.writeup: "Fragrance" is quiet and careful in all the right ways, Augustine building atmosphere before asking for your attention. Singer-songwriter territory that leans closer to ambient folk than anything with a hook, but it earns the listen through feel alone.

posts.title: Plain Jane
posts.writeup: Amanda Cy's "Plain Jane" rides a low-key groove with a vocal that is too comfortable to be anything but confident. The production leaves just enough room for the personality to come through. She's working in the alt-R&B lane with a clarity of vision that stands out in a crowded field.

posts.title: Spilled My Coffee (feat. Allday) - HONNE Rework
posts.writeup: HONNE's rework of "Spilled My Coffee" turns the original into exactly the kind of track the duo does best, dreamy late-night production with Allday's verses sitting easy in the new arrangement. It's a remix that improves the source material without overwriting it. HONNE doing HONNE things.

posts.title: Many Times
posts.writeup: Dijon's Stranger in the Alps EP has been on repeat, and "Many Times" is a good reason why. Sparse, aching folk-pop with a voice that sounds like it knows something the rest of us are still figuring out. Production stays out of the way and lets the songwriting carry weight. Right up your alley.

posts.title: amphetamine
posts.writeup: boylife writes songs that feel like they were recorded at 2 AM in the truest sense. "amphetamine" is raw and close, the production built around a vocal that doesn't try to be anything other than honest. He's a songwriter worth following.

posts.title: Deadweight
posts.writeup: Olive Amun takes the weight of the title literally and builds a track around it. Layered vocals, restrained production, and an emotional center that holds without needing to announce itself. Early work worth tracking.

posts.title: We Outside
posts.writeup: Duckwrth brings the energy the title promises. "We Outside" is a festival-ready banger rooted in funk and hip-hop maximalism, his LA sensibility cutting through every layer. If you've slept on him, this is the track that wakes you up.

posts.title: idk why the light's on
posts.writeup: Akurei is working in the space between bedroom pop and alt-R&B and "idk why the light's on" shows the range. Soft synth textures, introspective lyrics, and a vocal approach that feels genuine rather than calculated. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Without You
posts.writeup: Chasing Daze brings clean indie-pop craft to "Without You," a track that knows its lane and executes it well. Bright production, a hook that works, and a vocal performance that sells the emotion without overselling it. Solid entry from a duo worth watching.

posts.title: I'm Naive
posts.writeup: "I'm Naive" leans into the vulnerability of the title, Chasing Daze leaning on understated production and a vocal that carries more feeling than the arrangement demands. The restraint is the point. Two tracks in and we're already paying attention.

posts.title: lost souls
posts.writeup: Baby Keem's The Melodic Blue is one of the defining rap records of this era, and "lost souls" captures why. Produced in the Keem-Lamar orbit, it lives in the tension between introspection and bravado. The cadence switches are deliberate, the emotion is not a trick. Necessary listening.

posts.title: issues
posts.writeup: Baby Keem keeps the pressure on "issues," a tight, paranoid cut from The melodic blue. The beat sits low and menacing, with Keem's cadence toggling between melodic and razor-sharp in a way that still sounds like nothing else in his lane. He's working through something real here, and it shows. PGLang had a vision for this kid from day one.

posts.title: We're All Falling Apart
posts.writeup: OCTAVIO the Dweeb writes anthems for the quietly overwhelmed. "We're All Falling Apart" dresses collective anxiety in warm indie-pop production, with guitar tones that feel lived-in and a vocal delivery that avoids melodrama entirely. He's based out of LA, and this has the kind of understated craft that tends to travel. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Funky
posts.writeup: Deb Never makes melancholy sound effortless, and "Funky" is the best kind of contradiction: it's heavier than the title suggests. The production leans into a hazy, lo-fi warmth that she's made her signature, with distorted low-end and a hook that stays in your head longer than it has any right to. She's been building quietly since 2019, and tracks like this are why.

posts.title: scapegoats
posts.writeup: "scapegoats" is one of the more emotionally direct moments on The melodic blue. Keem strips back the bravado and sits with something closer to grief, the production spacious and slightly disorienting. It doesn't overstay its welcome, which is the point. A lot of rappers his age wouldn't trust the silence like this.

posts.title: trademark usa
posts.writeup: Pure flex, pure fun. "trademark usa" is Keem at his most unhinged in the best possible way, the flow scrambling all over a beat that sounds like it was built for someone to wreck. Kendrick's fingerprints are all over the production aesthetic at PGLang, but this one is Keem's show top to bottom. Replay value is high.

posts.title: vent
posts.writeup: The melodic blue opener does exactly what it promises. Keem arrives anxious and wired, cycling through flows on "vent" like he's still figuring out what he wants to say, and somehow that restlessness is the whole statement. The production is sparse, letting his voice carry the weight. It's a strong introduction to what the project becomes.

posts.title: range brothers (with Kendrick Lamar)
posts.writeup: This is the one. "range brothers" is the moment The melodic blue announced itself as something bigger than a debut, Kendrick showing up not to overshadow but to match. The beat is industrial and thumping, both of them swapping aggressive, unpredictable flows that feel genuinely competitive. This kind of chemistry doesn't happen on command.

posts.title: Karma Called
posts.writeup: Downtown Kayoto moves in a space that blends R&B atmosphere with hip-hop structure, and "Karma Called" leans into that tension. The production is moody and deliberate, built for late listening. Not much press behind him yet, which makes the track hit differently when you stumble across it cold.

posts.title: Photograph
posts.writeup: Samuel Petra writes with a specificity that most bedroom pop artists miss. "Photograph" is clean and intimate, the kind of track that sounds best through headphones, with production that knows when to get out of the way. The vocal performance carries real weight. We've been watching this one develop for a while.

posts.title: Knife Talk (with 21 Savage ft. Project Pat)
posts.writeup: Drake, 21 Savage, and Project Pat on the same track should not work as cleanly as it does. "Knife Talk" runs cold from the jump, Certified Lover Boy's most stripped-back and threatening moment. The Memphis interpolation of Pat's "Don't Save Her" gives it a lineage that both 21 and Drake use wisely. Short, direct, no wasted moves.

posts.title: No Friends In The Industry
posts.writeup: Drake at his most territorial. "No Friends In The Industry" is a solo statement buried in the Certified Lover Boy rollout, direct and deliberately understated in its production. He's not trying to make a banger here; he's making a point. Whether you buy the grievance or not, the track holds its ground.

posts.title: Pipe Down
posts.writeup: One of the more melodically confident moments on Certified Lover Boy. "Pipe Down" is a slow-burn R&B cut with production that gives his voice room to move, built around a low-key groove that rewards patience. It lands differently in context of the album, but it also stands alone without trouble.

posts.title: N 2 Deep
posts.writeup: Drake and Future have done this before, and "N 2 Deep" reminds you why that combination works. Two people deeply comfortable being themselves on record, the production split between atmospheric and menacing, each artist staying in their lane while the track locks in. No surprises, no complaints.

posts.title: TSU
posts.writeup: "TSU" hits like an afterthought that becomes a standout. Drake sounds unguarded on this one, working through something personal over a production that's more minimal than most of CLB. It doesn't announce itself. That's part of why it sticks.

posts.title: Fair Trade (with Travis Scott)
posts.writeup: Travis Scott and Drake found a pocket here that neither of them occupies very often. "Fair Trade" is cinematic without being overwrought, the production swelling in all the right places while both artists stay focused. Scott's contribution in particular lands harder in context than his earlier loosies from that period. Worth revisiting.

posts.title: Love All (with JAY-Z)
posts.writeup: Two generational figures at very different career points, both fully aware of it. "Love All" doesn't try to be more than it is: a patient, luxury-rap statement with JAY-Z arriving fully formed and Drake holding court. The beat breathes. The conversation between them is the actual content.

posts.title: In The Bible (with Lil Durk & Giveon)
posts.writeup: Lil Durk and GIVEON make this one more interesting than the title suggests. Three artists with very different tonal registers finding a shared space on "In The Bible," the gospel-tinged production doing the heavy lifting underneath. GIVEON's presence adds something the rest of CLB is missing.

posts.title: Girls Want Girls (with Lil Baby)
posts.writeup: "Girls Want Girls" is a left turn that mostly works, Drake and Lil Baby landing in R&B-adjacent territory over production that's warmer than either of them usually reaches for. The hook is built for repetition and delivers. Context inside CLB helps, but the track holds on its own terms.

posts.title: Sea Demon
posts.writeup: Leo Bhanji deals in slow-building emotional weight, and "Sea Demon" earns its runtime. The production is atmospheric without being ambient, something shifting underneath the surface the whole way through. Limited press on this one, which makes it worth sharing while the moment is still early.

posts.title: Day By Day
posts.writeup: Willy P keeps it grounded on "Day By Day," a straightforward reflection on the grind that gains weight from its lack of decoration. The production is clean and unassuming, letting the message carry without a lot of sonic dressing. Sometimes that's the move.

posts.title: Clementine
posts.writeup: Kelly Zutrau has always written in a register that sounds both personal and detached, and "Clementine" is Wet at their most refined. The production is cool and minimal, her vocal performance doing the emotional work without overselling it. This came after a few years of quiet, and it reminded us immediately why we'd been waiting.

posts.title: Heaven and Hell
posts.writeup: The Donda opener is a statement of intent, and it works. "Heaven and Hell" runs under two minutes but does more with orchestral production and a vocal sample than most artists manage in five. It's a reintroduction, not a song, and as an opening move it's almost perfectly executed.

posts.title: lush
posts.writeup: boylife makes music that sounds like it's dissolving before it finishes. "lush" is built around guitar and voice, the production staying skeletal while everything emotional lives in the negative space. San Francisco-based, deeply singular, and one of the more interesting quiet signals we've tracked in the indie-folk adjacent world.

posts.title: No Child Left Behind
posts.writeup: Donda's closing track works differently in context than it does alone, but "No Child Left Behind" holds up either way. Ye builds it around a gospel sample that's transformed almost beyond recognition, the whole thing feeling more like a hymn than a rap song. Whether you read it as redemption or spectacle, it lands.

posts.title: Pure Souls
posts.writeup: "Pure Souls" is the spiritual center of Donda, a meditation dressed in choral production with Roddy Ricch arriving late and making the most of his feature. Ye's cadence here is one of his most reflective in years, less the provocateur and more the devotional. The beat breathes like it's praying.

posts.title: Lord I Need You
posts.writeup: A Donda closer that strips everything back to organ, choir, and Kanye at his most vulnerable. The production barely breathes, letting the weight of the confession carry. It sits in the tradition of "Ultralight Beam" but reaches for something more private. Keep an ear on moments like these from an artist who rarely asks for anything.

posts.title: New Again
posts.writeup: Travis Scott's hook floats through a gospel-soaked instrumental that rides the line between hymn and hedonism. The beat pulses low and warm, giving both voices room to stretch. Kanye leans into the spiritual contradiction he's been chasing since "Jesus Walks." It shouldn't work as cleanly as it does.

posts.title: Jesus Lord
posts.writeup: At nearly nine minutes, this is Donda's most unguarded statement. Kanye builds the track around Larry Hoover Jr.'s voice, letting the testimony breathe before the production swells into something cathedral-sized. The gospel architecture is sturdy but the emotion underneath it is raw. One of the album's most quietly devastating moments.

posts.title: Moon
posts.writeup: Don Toliver and Kid Cudi orbit a beat so delicate it almost disappears. The track runs just over two minutes and doesn't need more time than that. Distant synths, soft falsetto, a pocket of space that feels intentional. Donda's most understated moment and somehow one of its best.

posts.title: Remote Control
posts.writeup: The Young Thug assist lands over a skittering, percussive groove that keeps shifting underneath you. Kanye's bars are loose but the beat has genuine forward momentum. It feels like an outtake that earned its spot through pure energy. Not the deepest cut on the album, but it moves.

A Sunday Service hallucination built entirely from vocals. No drums, no keys, just stacked harmonies and a choir pushing upward. It's the kind of track that makes you forget Kanye is a rapper. The restraint here is the whole point.

posts.title: Believe What I Say
posts.writeup: Built around a chopped Lauryn Hill sample that's been stretched into something elastic and warm. The production is looser than most of Donda, which makes it feel like a window into an earlier Kanye. He sounds comfortable here in a way he rarely does across the album's more ambitious stretches. We've had this one on repeat.

posts.title: Junya
posts.writeup: Playboi Carti arrives and the temperature drops ten degrees. The industrial percussion hit is sharp and unpolished, which is exactly what the moment needs. Kanye goes off-script, Carti barely needs to try, and the track lands harder than almost anything else on the record. Pure momentum.

posts.title: Jonah
posts.writeup: Vory and Lil Durk help Kanye build something that feels genuinely wounded. The production is spare enough that every melodic choice registers. It's a midtempo confessional that doesn't reach for resolution, which gives it staying power. Chicago's weight is all over this one.

posts.title: Praise God
posts.writeup: Travis Scott and Baby Keem both show up, but the beat belongs to no one in particular. It heaves and churns beneath layers of distortion and sampled vocal textures. Kanye threads through it all with his most confident verse on the album. The final minute builds into something genuinely grand.

posts.title: Hurricane
posts.writeup: The Weeknd and Lil Baby on one track, both dialed in. The production is cinematic without being overwrought, all strings and controlled space. Kanye's verse is some of his most direct writing in years. Donda's clearest bid for a single and rightfully so.

posts.title: Off The Grid
posts.writeup: Fivio Foreign and Playboi Carti show up and immediately make their case. The drill-inflected beat hits with real pressure, and Fivio's verse is one of the best guest spots on the entire project. Kanye's own bars are sharp enough to keep pace. It goes as hard as anything he's touched in a decade.

posts.title: Jail
posts.writeup: Jay-Z's verse is the conversation everyone had, but the production underneath it earns its own attention. The guitar riff is blunt and heavy, giving the whole thing a rock weight that reads as intentional. Kanye sets the table and Jay delivers. Worth remembering how few people can still do that to each other.

posts.title: Mood (feat. BNXN)
posts.writeup: BNXN, then known as Benson Daniel, and Wizkid slide into a groove that moves like water. The Afrobeats architecture is tight but the feeling is completely loose. We've been following Wizkid since Ojuelegba, and the chemistry he builds with emerging Lagos talent keeps holding up. Right up your alley if you move to rhythm first.

posts.title: Still In Love
posts.writeup: Jaden plays guitar and it actually lands. The production is lo-fi and intimate, a far cry from the bigger swings on his earlier records. His voice sits low in the mix in a way that feels deliberate, not accidental. Growing into something real here.

posts.title: Falling In Love
posts.writeup: Julia Wolf trades the bedroom-pop of her early work for something warmer and more textured. The guitar tone is clean but the emotional register is murkier in the best sense. Her voice stays in a pocket throughout, never overselling the feeling. Worth watching.

posts.title: family ties (with Kendrick Lamar)
posts.writeup: Kendrick shows up mid-track and the thing reshapes itself around him. Baby Keem holds his own on the first half with a delivery that's genuinely strange and gripping. The beat switch is abrupt and perfect. One of the year's most talked-about collaborations and it earned it.

posts.title: Safe Side
posts.writeup: Shakota builds tension slowly, layering atmosphere before anything melodic arrives. The production has a cinematic patience that's rare for an independent release at this level. Keep an ear on where this one goes.

posts.title: Residing in the Sky
posts.writeup: Abby Sage's production floats between folk and something more expansive. Her voice sits at the center without any ornamentation, which is the right call. The arrangement knows when to step back. A quiet one worth sitting with.

posts.title: who you callin' cold hearted?
posts.writeup: Jonah Zed works in a space between indie-pop and something more emotionally direct. The guitar work is unhurried and the production doesn't oversell the hook. It holds together on repetition, which is the real test.

posts.title: Summer
posts.writeup: Jaden in full singer-songwriter mode, and it suits him better than expected. The track is warm and slightly hazy, built for exactly the season it's named after. Less ambitious than his broader catalog work but more honest for it.

posts.title: The Mission
posts.writeup: The London-via-Jamaica artist comes through with a track that's all forward motion and controlled heat. The guitar tone is rugged and the production has a texture that draws from post-punk without being obvious about it. Bakar has been one to watch for a couple years, and this keeps the case open.

posts.title: 9 Times Outta 10
posts.writeup: Gunna floats across a plush, trap-adjacent beat that doesn't demand much from him and that's fine. The production is comfortable and well-made, and his melodic instincts fill the space without friction. Reliable rather than revelatory, which in Atlanta trap is its own kind of craft.

posts.title: Tick Tock
posts.writeup: Thug in cruise control over a slick, mid-tempo beat that moves easily. The hook sticks, the production breathes, and there's no wasted motion in it. He's been making this kind of track sound effortless for years, and the formula still holds.

posts.title: Memories
posts.writeup: Daniel Daley and Ty Dolla Sign share space over a production that OVO's Night Owl Sound keeps immaculate. The harmonies are the whole story here, stacked and warm without becoming saccharine. dvsn has been quietly building one of the most consistent catalogs in contemporary R&B. This one fits right in.

posts.title: Lose Control
posts.writeup: Francis and the Lights has always moved at his own pace, and "Lose Control" is no different. The production is minimal but precise: soft synth swells, a locked-in kick, and his falsetto doing most of the heavy lifting. It's intimate without feeling small, the kind of track that sounds best through headphones at 2am. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Full Size Render
posts.writeup: j ember operates in that space where ambient and indie-pop blur, and "Full Size Render" is a clean case study. Textures layer up slowly over a deliberate beat, his voice staying understated throughout. The production choice to let silence breathe between phrases is what makes it land. Quietly impressive work.

posts.title: Lord of the Flies
posts.writeup: Myles Cameron and Downtown Kayoto bring a brooding energy here that feels earned rather than manufactured. The track builds on a slow-burn soul foundation, Cameron's voice cutting through with more weight than most. Reference points land somewhere between Frank Ocean's introspective mode and a harder-edged R&B sensibility. Worth your full attention.

posts.title: Sun, Moon, and Stars
posts.writeup: gcmayn works in shimmering bedroom pop territory, and "Sun, Moon, and Stars" is a solid entry. The production keeps things light, textured with gentle reverb and a hook that doesn't overstay its welcome. There's a clarity to the songwriting that hints at something more polished ahead. We've got our eye on this one.

posts.title: See Through
posts.writeup: Masati pulls from a toolkit of warm synths and deliberate pacing, and "See Through" feels like the kind of track that takes a few listens to fully open up. The vocal delivery is restrained in a way that builds trust, never reaching for emotion it hasn't earned. Production sits comfortably in the pocket throughout. Right up your alley if you like your pop left of center.

posts.title: Woah
posts.writeup: TOBi has been building something real over the last few years, and "Woah" with Jazz Cartier is the kind of track that shows why. The energy is high but controlled, the production hard-hitting without sacrificing the melodic thread. Both artists bring a confident ease that makes the collaboration feel natural rather than forced. Toronto knows what it's doing.

posts.title: All Of The Time
posts.writeup: Jungle continues to be one of the best pure-funk acts working today. "All Of The Time" locks into a groove almost immediately and doesn't let go, with the band's signature layered vocals riding a bass line that earns its place. It's not reinventing the wheel, but when the wheel rolls this smooth, you don't need to.

posts.title: Summer Wine
posts.writeup: Augustine brings a wiry, atmospheric indie sound to "Summer Wine," building around a fingerpicked guitar figure that anchors the whole thing. The production choices are smart, adding texture without cluttering the core of the song. His voice has a brittle quality that suits the mood. A good one for when the season changes.

posts.title: Outside
posts.writeup: Weston Estate tends to lean into guitar-driven indie pop with a slightly nostalgic edge, and "Outside" fits that mold well. The song has an easy forward momentum, nothing flashy, just solid instincts around arrangement and melody. It's the kind of track that earns repeat plays without demanding them. Keep an ear on this artist.

posts.title: KIDS ON MOLLY
posts.writeup: Aries leans full into a hyper-specific early-2000s pop-punk aesthetic here, and the commitment makes it work. "KIDS ON MOLLY" is punchy, loud, and self-aware, treating the nostalgia as raw material rather than a costume. The production has enough modern sheen to keep it from feeling like a costume. If you grew up on that era of radio, this one hits right.

posts.title: Limousine
posts.writeup: Junior Varsity moves through "Limousine" with a kind of measured tension, indie rock arrangements built around a melody that knows when to hold back. There's a maturity to the songwriting that suggests they've been doing the work quietly for a while. It doesn't overreach, which is exactly why it works.

posts.title: Invisible Fence
posts.writeup: binki operates in a space where jangly indie-pop meets low-key introspection, and "Invisible Fence" captures that balance. The guitar work is the highlight, a little warped and sun-bleached, sitting under a vocal that sounds like it was recorded in one take for good reason. Lo-fi in texture but sharply written.

posts.title: Dad
posts.writeup: "Dad" is a spare, acoustic-forward track from COLETON RUBIN that earns its emotional weight through restraint rather than reach. The production steps back entirely, letting the lyrical content carry the full load. It's the kind of song that works precisely because it doesn't try too hard. Genuinely moving.

posts.title: Big Enough
posts.writeup: Keni Titus has a natural ease as a vocalist, and "Big Enough" showcases it over a warm, unhurried production. The groove never rushes itself, giving the song room to breathe and the hook space to settle. Soul-adjacent but not stuck in any one era. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Nobody More
posts.writeup: Samuel Petra's "Nobody More" runs on a gentle but persistent piano loop with production that opens up slowly through the runtime. His voice carries a searching quality that fits the material without ever tipping into melodrama. It's a patient track from a writer who seems to understand that timing matters. We've been watching.

posts.title: Sweet & Spice
posts.writeup: Deb Never and Jim-E Stack are a natural pairing, and "Sweet & Spice" demonstrates why. Stack's production here is cool and slightly off-kilter, giving Deb Never's vocals a bed that matches her edge-of-detachment delivery. The track's best moments come when it pulls back and lets the negative space do work. Confident and a little uneasy in the best way.

posts.title: Do What You Gotta Do
posts.writeup: Che Ecru works in a neo-soul adjacent zone with just enough grit to keep things from feeling too smooth. "Do What You Gotta Do" has a deliberate groove, the production stripped down enough to put the vocal front and center. There's real craft in the arrangement even when it sounds effortless. Right up your alley.

posts.title: Before We Knew
posts.writeup: Day Wave has been a reliable source of hazy, guitar-layered indie pop for years, and "Before We Knew" is another strong entry. The reverb-soaked production is lush without being muddy, and the hook arrives with that bittersweet clarity that Jackson Phillips does better than almost anyone. It's nostalgic without being precious about it.

posts.title: Fallen/DGYLA
posts.writeup: Isaia Huron pairs two distinct emotional modes in one track and makes the transition feel inevitable. The first half of "Fallen/DGYLA" is bruised and tender, the second lifts with a kind of guarded optimism, production shifting to match each beat. The songwriting is cohesive enough to carry the experiment cleanly. Keep an ear on Huron.

posts.title: signs
posts.writeup: OSKAR O. keeps "signs" deliberately sparse, a minimal production framework that puts the focus on the melodic line and the space between notes. It's bedroom pop that knows when to stop adding and just let the song exist. The restraint is the statement. A solid find.

posts.title: waiting on nothing
posts.writeup: joan have built a reputation for a specific kind of earnest, hook-forward indie pop, and "waiting on nothing" is a clean example of what they do best. The production is warm and accessible, the melody working harder than it looks. There's nothing experimental here, just good songwriting executed well.

posts.title: Ride
posts.writeup: Fossa Beats brings a smooth, sample-influenced production style to "Ride" with Olive Amun, building something that sits comfortably in the neo-soul and lo-fi R&B overlap. Amun's vocal contribution is the standout, effortless and with a natural sense of phrasing. The track moves at exactly the right pace. Feast your ears.

posts.title: Larabar
posts.writeup: Wet continues the slow-burning, hazy indie-pop sound they've cultivated since the early records. "Larabar" is guitar-forward and slightly dreamy, Kelly Zutrau's vocal floating over a production that resists any urgency. The effect is calming and slightly melancholic without leaning too hard into either. A good late-night track.

posts.title: Still Got Time
posts.writeup: dexter in the newsagent writes with a UK sensibility and a feel for melody that translates cleanly across the board. "Still Got Time" is bright and driven, guitars doing the structural work while the vocal hooks catch quickly. It's the kind of indie-pop track that earns its place in a heavy rotation without asking for it.

posts.title: BUBBLY
posts.writeup: Hadji Gaviota brings a playful, high-energy approach to "BUBBLY," the production light on its feet and the vocal delivery matching that mood. It's fun without being thin, with enough production detail to reward a second listen. A solid palette cleanser in any playlist. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: OYSTER
posts.writeup: Hadji Gaviota leans into a drowsy, textured indie-soul pocket on "OYSTER," his voice floating above layered guitar and a beat that barely disturbs the surface. The LA-based artist has a knack for writing around feelings rather than at them, and it pays off here. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: boy.
posts.writeup: "boy." arrives wrapped in soft electric guitar and hyde's conversational baritone, the production staying sparse enough to let every word land. The Portland singer-songwriter works in the same quiet-ache tradition as Novo Amor and Mt. Wolf, but with a confessional directness that's his own. A small song that sticks.

posts.title: Talk Words
posts.writeup: Bickle keeps the palette tight on "Talk Words" — fingerpicked acoustic, a low hum of reverb, and a vocal that sounds like it was tracked in one take on purpose. The restraint is the whole point. This is folk-adjacent indie that trusts the listener to meet it halfway.

posts.title: Cheater (feat. Tom The Mail Man)
posts.writeup: SAIAH and Tom The Mail Man bring an easy chemistry to "Cheater," riding a mid-tempo R&B groove that never oversells itself. The production sits warm and low, giving both vocalists room to trade without crowding each other. Smooth without being slick.

posts.title: Someone to Hold You
posts.writeup: Jelani Aryeh has been one of the more interesting young singer-songwriters to watch, and "Someone to Hold You" is a good reminder why. The track breathes over acoustic guitar and brushed percussion, his falsetto carrying a weight that the arrangement quietly supports. San Diego via Tokyo, and it sounds like it.

posts.title: Love Dies At Dawn
posts.writeup: "Love Dies At Dawn" finds Aryeh pulling from a slightly more cinematic place, the strings subtle but felt, the melody landing with the kind of inevitability that takes real craft. It pairs well with "Someone to Hold You" but earns its keep on its own. We've been watching him since the Berklee years and the growth is real.

posts.title: From the Garden (feat. Lil Uzi Vert)
posts.writeup: Isaiah Rashad tapped Lil Uzi Vert for one of the more unexpected pairings on "The House Is Burning," and it works. Rashad's slurred, elastic flow wraps around the beat while Uzi floats in and out like a fever dream cameo. The TDE veteran sounds fully alive here, two years removed from his extended absence.

posts.title: THIB
posts.writeup: "THIB" is Rashad at his most unguarded, the production from Kenny Segal skeletal and humid, the bars circling personal mythology with a mix of swagger and honesty you rarely get anymore. The Chattanooga reference points are all over it. A standout from an album full of them.

posts.title: 9-3 Freestyle
posts.writeup: Rashad drops the formal structures and just raps on "9-3 Freestyle," the loose West Coast groove giving him plenty of room to stretch out. It's the kind of track that rewards repeat listens because there's always a bar you missed the first time. No features, no tricks, just the work.

posts.title: True Story (feat. Jay Rock & Jay Worthy)
posts.artist: Isaiah Rashad, Jay Rock, Jay Worthy
posts.writeup: Jay Rock and Jay Worthy slot into "True Story" like they've been riding that loop their whole careers. It's a West Coast deep cut, deliberate and confident, the three MCs trading bars without any of them rushing. Rashad built the anchor; these two know exactly what to do with it.

posts.title: Score (feat. SZA & 6LACK)
posts.writeup: SZA and 6LACK on the same track was always going to be something. "Score" earns those features, the silky TDE production giving all three vocalists space to coexist without competing. Rashad threads the needle between bravado and vulnerability and somehow makes it feel natural.

posts.title: Claymore (feat. Smino)
posts.writeup: Smino and Rashad share a frequency that's hard to manufacture, and "Claymore" is what happens when two artists genuinely locked in find the same pocket. The groove is deep, the wordplay sharp, and the track never announces itself as a highlight. It just is one.

posts.title: Lay Wit Ya (feat. Duke Deuce)
posts.writeup: Duke Deuce brings Memphis club energy crashing into Rashad's lane on "Lay Wit Ya," and somehow it holds together. The beat pivots without warning and both artists land every transition. One of the wilder swings on the album and probably the most fun.

posts.title: RIP Young
posts.writeup: Rashad gets reflective on "RIP Young," the track sitting heavier than most of the album's back half. The production from Sounwave is measured and atmospheric, creating space for a tribute that doesn't overreach. Direct and earned.

posts.title: Darkseid
posts.writeup: "Darkseid" closes Rashad's chapter here on an ominous, cinematic note, the bass sitting thick under a vocal that sounds like a challenge and a eulogy at once. Brief but complete. He's playing with DC mythology in the title but the track operates on its own terms.

posts.title: Got Me Like
posts.writeup: Chicago's Amaria recruits Mick Jenkins for "Got Me Like," and the pairing makes sense immediately. Her voice is butter-smooth over the minimal R&B production, and Jenkins' verse adds texture without taking over. A quiet flex from an artist with real range.

posts.title: Don't Wait Forever
