posts.writeup: Terry Presume sits in the quieter end of alternative R&B, "Don't Wait Forever" moving at an unhurried pace with vocals that feel lived-in rather than performed. The simplicity is intentional. Worth the time.

posts.title: Hello World
posts.writeup: "Hello World" introduces Downtown Kayoto with a hazy, lo-fi indie-pop sound that lands somewhere between bedroom recording and something more considered. The rough edges are part of the appeal. Early days but the instincts are right.

posts.title: Rosanna
posts.writeup: Benny Bellson brings a warm, sun-bleached indie-folk feel to "Rosanna," the acoustic fingerpicking and gentle harmonies painting a summer afternoon without being precious about it. Comparisons to early Lord Huron aren't a stretch. We'll be back for more.

posts.title: Go Again
posts.writeup: RIZ LA VIE works a melodic hip-hop space with confidence on "Go Again," the track bouncing on a clean, minimal production with hooks that stick on the first pass. The LA-based artist has a tonal clarity that sets him apart from the crowded field. Right up your alley if you run with Cordae or Ari Lennox playlists.

posts.title: Verdansk
posts.writeup: UK rapper Dave drops into "Verdansk" with the same precision and density that made "Psychodrama" one of the decade's more discussed records. The Call of Duty reference anchors a track about escapism and survival that works on more than one level. Sharp writing, expectedly.

posts.title: I DON'T MIND
posts.writeup: Colby Lafayette keeps "I DON'T MIND" smooth and unhurried, his voice carrying a maturity that sits comfortable over the R&B-leaning production. The Atlanta artist understands restraint. Every element earns its place.

posts.title: HOLD MY NAME
posts.writeup: "HOLD MY NAME" finds Lafayette dialing up the emotion without dialing up the volume, the production swelling just enough to support a vocal performance that doesn't need the help. He's building a small catalog worth following.

posts.title: EVERYTIME
posts.writeup: The third Colby Lafayette cut in the batch and "EVERYTIME" is the most direct of them, a clean R&B track with a tight melody and a confidence that comes from knowing exactly what you're doing. No filler.

posts.title: Understand
posts.writeup: Zach Zoya is a Montreal-based artist with a sound that sits between alt-R&B and pop, and "Understand" is a polished piece of evidence for why we've kept him in rotation. The production glows, the hook lands clean, and he delivers it all with a lightness that hides the craft. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: cheap flowers
posts.writeup: Lexi Jayde writes from the gut, and "cheap flowers" is no different. It's a breakup song that skips the theatrics: sparse acoustic production, her voice sitting dry and close in the mix, nothing to hide behind. The lyric lands because it's specific, not sweeping. Nashville by way of Gen Z bedroom pop, and she's got the craft to back it up.

posts.title: Composite One
posts.writeup: Melbourne's Thrupence built his name stitching jazz harmony into electronic frameworks, and "Composite One" with Josh Hooke and Jack Vanzet is that instinct fully realized. Layered woodwinds, brushed percussion, and a low-end that rolls rather than kicks. It moves like a film score that decided it wanted to be a song instead.

posts.title: METTA WORLD PEACE
posts.writeup: Francis Farewell Starlite has always operated outside the grid, and "METTA WORLD PEACE" keeps that streak intact. Pure falsetto over minimal synth production, the kind of track that sounds like nothing else in his peers' catalogs. He named it after an athlete who changed his own name as an act of peace. That's the whole vibe.

posts.title: THE EDGE
posts.writeup: COTIS is a Tokyo-based rapper who moves between English and Japanese without breaking stride, and "THE EDGE" is a showcase of that fluency. Hard-knocking percussion, trap-influenced production with a Japanese ear for texture. The bilingual approach isn't a gimmick here, it sharpens the delivery.

posts.title: Weightless
posts.writeup: Montreal's Chiiild makes R&B that breathes. "Weightless" floats on layered falsetto and a production style that owes as much to ambient music as it does to soul. It's from his debut album Hope for Sale, where he spent two years building a sound that feels lived-in from the first listen. Keep an ear on him.

posts.title: Wat U Sed (feat. Doechii & Kal Banx)
posts.writeup: Isaiah Rashad resurfaced after years away, and "Wat U Sed" with Doechii and Kal Banx is the kind of collab that rewards patience. Rashad's cadence is as slippery as ever, Doechii brings teeth, and Kal Banx rounds it out with a more melodic lean. TDE-era low-fi warmth with something newer sitting underneath.

posts.title: Resting B*tch Face: Part 2
posts.writeup: Julia Wolf has been carving out space in the alt-pop lane for a few years now, and "Resting B*tch Face: Part 2" leans into her sharpest instincts. It's self-aware without being smug, the production keeps enough grit to avoid feeling over-produced. She's building a catalog worth tracing back.

posts.title: INDUSTRY BABY (feat. Jack Harlow)
posts.writeup: "INDUSTRY BABY" arrived like a statement. Kanye West and Take A Daytrip on the boards, Jack Harlow holding his own in the feature slot, and Lil Nas X operating at full confidence after a year of proving everyone wrong. Brass-heavy, anthemic, and built for the moment it documented.

posts.title: Survivor's Guilt
posts.writeup: Dave's "Survivor's Guilt" is ten-plus minutes of a British rapper at full compositional stretch. He's processing grief, class, and Black British identity over production that shifts under the weight of what he's saying. There's no chorus because there's no resolution. One of the defining UK rap tracks of 2021.

posts.title: Both Sides Of A Smile (feat. James Blake)
posts.writeup: Dave and James Blake is a pairing that makes complete sense once you hear it. Blake's piano and vocal production give Dave's lyricism a chamber-music frame, the kind of emotional space his writing needs to land properly. "Both Sides Of A Smile" is the album's most intimate track and probably its most lasting.

posts.title: Lazarus (feat. Boj)
posts.writeup: Boj has been a quiet force in the UK and Nigeria for years, and linking up with Dave on "Lazarus" is a natural fit. Afrobeats rhythm patterns meet Dave's South London flow, the track moving with a looseness that contrasts sharply with the heavier material on the album. It's the exhale between the harder listens.

posts.title: System (feat. WizKid)
posts.writeup: Dave and WizKid on the same track is a UK-Nigeria summit worth paying attention to. "System" rides a slow-burning Afropop groove, both artists at a lower temperature than their usual registers. It functions almost as a cooldown, but the political undertow in Dave's writing never fully disappears.

posts.title: We're All Alone
posts.writeup: Dave closes with a gut-punch. "We're All Alone" is piano-led and plainspoken, the production pulled back to almost nothing so his voice carries the full weight. It's the kind of closer that recontextualizes everything before it. The album title becomes a thesis statement by the time it ends.

posts.title: BAD NEWS
posts.writeup: The Kid LAROI was nineteen and already sounding like a veteran on the "F*CK LOVE 3" sessions. "BAD NEWS" leans into the emo-pop-rap pocket he helped define, raw vocal delivery over a production that hits harder than it should at that tempo. Australia produced something genuinely different with him.

posts.title: STILL CHOSE YOU (feat. Mustard)
posts.writeup: Mustard's production credit on "STILL CHOSE YOU" brings a West Coast low-end that grounds LAROI's more melodic instincts. The push-pull between the beat's weight and his lighter vocal register is where the track lives. It's the most radio-ready moment on the project without feeling like a concession.

posts.title: SAME ENERGY
posts.writeup: "SAME ENERGY" is LAROI at his most unguarded. No featured artist to deflect to, just his voice and a production that leaves him exposed in the best way. The writing here is less polished and more honest for it, which is what separated him from the pack at this stage of his career.

posts.title: NOT SOBER (feat. Polo G & Stunna Gambino)
posts.artist: The Kid LAROI, Polo G, Stunna Gambino
posts.writeup: Polo G and LAROI orbit similar emotional territory, so "NOT SOBER" is a logical crossover. Both bring the same confessional honesty to melodic rap, Stunna Gambino filling out the back end. It's a deep cut on the project that rewards the full-album listen.

posts.title: OVER YOU
posts.writeup: "OVER YOU" is LAROI doing what he does at its most distilled: short, sharp, emotionally direct. The production is almost skeletal, which puts all the pressure on the vocal, and he holds it. Easy to overlook at four minutes and some change, but it sticks.

posts.title: New Normal
posts.writeup: Khalid's voice carries inherent warmth, and "New Normal" leans into that fully. It's a mid-tempo R&B track that doesn't reach for drama, just settles into a groove and trusts the writing to do the work. El Paso by way of everywhere, and still one of the most consistent voices of his generation.

posts.title: Summer '19
posts.writeup: Myles Cameron has an ear for nostalgia that doesn't tip into sentimentality. "Summer '19" with Moise captures a specific feeling without overselling it, the production staying light and the vocal performance staying honest. Worth tracking if you haven't already.

posts.title: Act Like
posts.writeup: Cal in Red is operating in the quieter end of indie-pop, and "Act Like" is a solid example of why that space still has room for new voices. Understated production, a vocal that doesn't overreach, and writing that trusts the listener. Not everything needs to announce itself.

posts.title: Peace of Mind
posts.writeup: Nick Murphy returned to the Chet Faker name for Hotel Surrender, and "Peace of Mind" is the album's thesis in song form. Heavy bass, filtered vocals, and a production approach that sounds like 2021 filtering 1989 through a very particular set of memories. He sounds more settled than he did under his own name, which is the point.

posts.title: Fire
posts.writeup: Tanisako is a Japanese artist making R&B that sounds more at home in London or LA than Tokyo, which is a compliment to the craft. "Fire" is clean and controlled, the production sitting in a neo-soul pocket without leaning on the reference too hard. Worth remembering the name.

posts.title: Vanilla & Light
posts.writeup: Tommy Newport has been making hazy bedroom pop with a surprisingly sharp melodic sense for a while now, and "Vanilla & Light" captures that balance. It's warm, lo-fi without being precious about it, and the hook lands on the first listen. Exactly what the title suggests.

posts.title: dog eat dog
posts.writeup: aldn and renforshort both work in the emotionally-direct indie-pop lane, so "dog eat dog" off the greenhouse project is a comfortable fit. The production stays sparse, both vocalists trading off without overshadowing each other. It's a quick four minutes that earns repeated plays.

posts.title: Hands Tied
posts.writeup: HARRY WAS HERE sits somewhere between shoegaze and bedroom pop, and "Hands Tied" leans all the way into that tension. The production is sparse but deliberate, letting the vocal carry weight without much to hide behind. There is a restraint here that reads as confidence. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: All in My Head
posts.writeup: Kevin George and Johan Lenox have been building something quietly for a while, and "All in My Head" is the clearest proof yet. The arrangement is cool and elastic, RnB bones with enough breathing room to feel effortless. George's voice does not push for attention but earns it every time. Right up your alley if you are sleeping on these two.

posts.title: Popeye
posts.writeup: Peach Tree Rascals keep threading the needle between rap and indie pop without ever sounding like they are trying to. "Popeye" with AG Club has that loose, off-the-cuff energy the Bay Area collective does better than almost anyone right now. The hook sits low and easy in the mix. Worth your time.

posts.title: People Watching
posts.writeup: Conan Gray writes the kind of songs that feel embarrassingly specific and completely universal at the same time. "People Watching" is one of his sharpest, a mid-tempo piano-driven track about projecting romance onto strangers that hits harder the more you sit with the lyrics. The production is clean and uncluttered, which is exactly right for a song this emotionally direct.

posts.title: Winter's Coming
posts.writeup: Lucca Dohr writes with a Nordic restraint that makes every detail land. "Winter's Coming" is built on layered acoustic textures and a vocal that sounds like it was recorded in one quiet, unhurried sitting. The song does not announce itself. It just settles in.

posts.title: Origami
posts.writeup: Ani Brava is a Barcelona-based singer-songwriter working in a kind of sparse folk-pop mode that does not waste a note. "Origami" folds emotional complexity into a compact, beautifully recorded track. The guitar work is especially worth your attention. We have been watching her.

posts.title: Off The Drugs
posts.writeup: TOBi has been one of the more interesting voices in Toronto's rap-adjacent scene for years, and "Off The Drugs" with Mick Jenkins is him at his most direct. The production has a lived-in, late-night quality, warm samples and a pace that never rushes. Jenkins adds exactly the right texture. This one sticks.

posts.title: Can't Be Love
posts.writeup: "Can't Be Love" is a compact piece of electronic RnB that Boon builds around a deceptively simple premise. The synth work is tactile and the vocal sits right in the pocket, never overselling the emotion. Short, precise, and more affecting than it has any right to be.

posts.title: Anything
posts.writeup: Ben Chandler is working in the tradition of confessional singer-songwriting but the production on "Anything" keeps it from feeling familiar. There is a dusty, worn quality to the mix that matches the emotional weight of the lyric. He is not trying to impress anyone, and that is what makes it worth listening to.

posts.title: Versos de Placer
posts.writeup: maye is a Chilean artist making Spanish-language pop that feels genuinely global without losing its roots. "Versos de Placer" is warm, melodically confident, and built around a vocal performance that does not need translation to land. We have been wanting to share this one for a while.

posts.title: Cry in Church
posts.writeup: IDK's USEE4YOURSELF project was one of the more ambitious rap albums of 2021, and "Cry in Church" with Sevyn Streeter captures what made it work. The contrast between her silky vocal and IDK's clipped delivery is the whole tension of the song. The production holds space for both without forcing anything.

posts.title: Dogs Don't Lie
posts.writeup: IDK flips the script on this one, leaning into a cleaner melodic mode that sits closer to RnB than anything he has done before. "Dogs Don't Lie" is a short, focused piece that shows range without making a big deal of it. The hook is genuinely good.

posts.title: Red
posts.artist: IDK, Westside Gunn, MF DOOM, J A Y E L E C T R O N I C A
posts.writeup: Four names on this track and none of them are wasted. IDK, Westside Gunn, MF DOOM, and Jay Electronica each bring a distinct weight to "Red," and the instrumental is exactly sparse enough to let all of it breathe. DOOM's verse alone is worth the price of admission. A standout from a standout project.

posts.title: Talk 2 Me
posts.writeup: Montell Fish is building a sound that is almost impossible to pin down, gospel-touched bedroom RnB that feels both intimate and enormous. "Talk 2 Me" is one of his most direct songs, a simple request carried by a vocal that sounds like it is barely holding together. That fragility is the point.

posts.title: World On Fire (with Kevin George)
posts.writeup: Johan Lenox has a gift for orchestral pop that never tips into excess, and "World On Fire" with Kevin George is a clean example of that restraint working in his favor. The arrangement swells without overwhelming the vocal, which George handles with his usual ease. These two together make sense.

posts.title: Zebra
posts.writeup: COLETON RUBIN writes with a directness that cuts through fast. "Zebra" is a guitar-forward indie pop track with a hook that earns its place and a vocal delivery that does not oversell. There is a specificity to the songwriting here that suggests someone who knows exactly what they are doing.

posts.title: STAY (with Justin Bieber)
posts.writeup: By the time "STAY" landed it was already everywhere, but the reason it worked is worth noting. The Kid LAROI found a production pocket that splits the difference between emo-pop and radio-ready pop-punk, and Bieber's feature is one of his best in years. The chorus is relentless in the best way.

posts.title: WHAT WOULD YOU DO? (feat. Pink Sweat$)
posts.writeup: HONNE have always been good at writing songs that feel warm even when the subject matter is not, and "WHAT WOULD YOU DO?" with Pink Sweat$ leans into that warmth fully. The production is smooth without being slick, and the two vocalists find a register that feels genuinely effortless. This one is for late evenings.

posts.title: I Like Me
posts.writeup: The name is a mouthful but the music is worth it. dexter in the newsagent is a UK-based artist making jangly, emotionally honest indie pop with a self-awareness that keeps it from curdling into irony. "I Like Me" is a three-minute lesson in saying something sincere without flinching.

posts.title: Honeymoon Die
posts.writeup: Keith Cameron operates in a sparse, low-key mode that rewards patience. "Honeymoon Die" is a dark-edged folk track with a title that earns its strangeness, the production stripped back enough to let the lyric sit uncomfortably in the room. We are watching closely.

posts.title: Galipette
posts.writeup: Lolo Zouai splits her time between French and English and her sound between LA gloss and Parisian cool, and "Galipette" is one of her most confident moments yet. The production is sleek and the switch between languages feels completely natural. She has been one to watch for a while now.

posts.title: Drive
posts.writeup: BEL makes RnB that sounds expensive without trying to. "Drive" is built on a slow-rolling groove and a vocal that knows when to pull back, which is most of the time. The restraint is the point. This one rewards repeat plays.

posts.title: Bait Em In (feat. Lil Keed)
posts.artist: Unfoonk, Young Stoner Life, Lil Keed
posts.writeup: Unfoonk is Young Thug's brother and carries some of that same elastic, pitch-wandering energy into "Bait Em In" with Lil Keed. The production is hard and minimal, lots of space around the 808s, and both rappers fill it comfortably. Young Stoner Life keeps delivering.

posts.title: PradadaBang
posts.writeup: IDK and Young Thug is a collaboration that makes complete sense on paper and delivers on the record. "PradadaBang" is a flex track that earns its confidence, built on a polished beat that gives Thug room to do what only he does. IDK holds his own throughout.

posts.title: Weather (feat. Spill Tab)
posts.writeup: Junior Varsity and Spill Tab link up for something that sounds like the best possible version of a song you would discover on shuffle at 2 AM. "Weather" is hazy indie pop with a vocal dynamic between the two that feels genuinely conversational. Short and just right.

posts.title: Wasting Away
posts.writeup: Abby Sage opens cold, her voice barely above a whisper before the track blooms around her. Wasting Away sits in that quiet ache where indie-folk and bedroom pop overlap, all fingerpicked warmth and restrained percussion. She keeps the arrangement sparse so every word lands. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Crazy
posts.writeup: Domenic Haynes works a classic soul formula here, but the execution is clean enough that it doesn't feel borrowed. Stacked harmonies, a groove that locks in early and never lets go, and a vocal that knows when to push and when to pull back. Straightforward and effective.

posts.title: I Believed It (feat. Mac Miller)
posts.writeup: This one hits different with context. dvsn and Ty Dolla $ign build a slow-burn R&B frame that Mac Miller floats through effortlessly, his verse relaxed and reflective in that way he had toward the end. The production is lush without being crowded, giving everyone room. A genuinely moving piece of archive material.

posts.title: FIT PIC
posts.writeup: Hadji Gaviota keeps it lo-fi and unforced, FIT PIC riding a lazy afternoon energy that doesn't overstay its welcome. The production is minimal, the vibe is the point. Worth a spin if you like your rap unbothered.

posts.title: Wasting Time ( feat. Drake & The Neptunes )
posts.writeup: The Neptunes touch is all over this one from the jump, that signature snap and space baked into every bar. Faiyaz and Drake trade in romantic detachment over a bed of clean guitar and soft percussion. It's polished to a high shine without losing the loose feeling that makes it work. Classic Neptunes architecture updated for now.

posts.title: Sunlight
posts.writeup: Your Grandparents pull from classic soul and lo-fi R&B, Sunlight landing somewhere between a warm memory and a lazy Sunday. The vocal chemistry is natural, the production unhurried. This is music that doesn't demand your attention but rewards it.

posts.title: The Idea of You
posts.writeup: Grady brings an anthemic energy to The Idea of You, the guitar-driven production landing in that space where indie rock and radio alt overlap without apology. lovelytheband's presence adds texture and the hook is genuinely built to stick. Right up your alley if you've been sleeping on both.

posts.title: FALLOUT
posts.writeup: FALLOUT builds slowly, Finn Harry layering atmosphere before the drop earns itself. The production has a cinematic quality, processed vocals sitting in reverb-heavy space while the track takes its time. Patient and confident.

posts.title: DUMMY DUDE
posts.writeup: Colby Lafayette comes in relaxed, DUMMY DUDE carrying the easy confidence of someone who knows exactly what they're doing. The production splits the difference between funk and contemporary rap, a wiry guitar loop anchoring the whole thing. Worth noting.

posts.title: WILSHIRE
posts.writeup: WILSHIRE is the emotional cornerstone of Call Me If You Get Lost's side B, a 10-minute suite that unfolds like a short film. Tyler traces a relationship from spark to collapse over lush orchestration and jazz-inflected production, his pen as sharp as it's ever been. It's the kind of track that reminds you he's operating on a different level.

posts.title: Nights2Long
posts.writeup: Ayelle writes with real emotional precision, Nights2Long moving through longing without leaning on cliche. The production is clean and contemporary, a floating R&B backdrop that keeps the focus on the vocal. Straightforward in the best sense.

posts.title: Truffles
posts.writeup: Mick Jenkins keeps things cerebral, Truffles operating at the intersection of jazz rap and conscious hip-hop that he's owned for years. The production is understated and earthy, his flow unhurried but deliberate. Chicago's been sitting on one of the sharpest lyricists of his generation for a while now.

posts.title: SWEET / I THOUGHT YOU WANTED TO DANCE (feat. Brent Faiyaz & Fana Hues)
posts.artist: Tyler, The Creator, Brent Faiyaz, Fana Hues
posts.writeup: A genuine suite moment on CALL ME IF YOU GET LOST, running through two movements that show two sides of Tyler's range. The first half is woozy and romantic with Brent Faiyaz doing what he does, and then it pivots into a sprawling orchestral dance number with Fana Hues. Ambitious and it pulls it off.

posts.title: WUSYANAME (feat. Youngboy Never Broke Again & Ty Dolla $ign)
posts.artist: Tyler, The Creator, YoungBoy Never Broke Again, Ty Dolla $ign
posts.writeup: WUSYANAME is one of the breezier moments on the album, Tyler in full charm mode over a bouncy, sample-flipped groove. YoungBoy and Ty Dolla $ign both feel at home here in ways that could have gone sideways. The energy is summer-block-cookout and it earns it.

posts.title: HOT WIND BLOWS (feat. Lil Wayne)
posts.writeup: Putting Lil Wayne on a Pharrell-adjacent, orchestral rap track sounds like a thesis statement, and Tyler delivers. HOT WIND BLOWS has a grandeur to it that Wayne matches verse for verse, the two of them flexing in a setting that would have seemed impossible a decade ago. A highlight.

posts.title: Make U Go
posts.writeup: Duckwrth leans into his funk-forward pocket here, Make U Go built on a groove that moves from the first bar. The production is bright and kinetic, all synth textures and tight percussion, and his vocal sits right in the mix. The LA artist has been quietly stacking wins for years.

posts.title: Walk
posts.writeup: Griff writes pop with real economy, Walk delivering its emotional payload in under three minutes without a wasted note. The production is clean and airy, letting the vocal do the work. British pop that earns its placement every time it shows up in a playlist.

posts.title: Whoa
posts.writeup: Cautious Clay has always split his time between jazz sensibility and pop accessibility, and Whoa is one of his cleaner fusions. The guitar work is present without being showy, and the vocal carries an ease that sounds effortless even when the emotion underneath it isn't. A steady hand.

posts.title: Options (feat. JID)
posts.writeup: Options pairs two of the sharper lyricists in pop and rap right now and lets them play. JID's verse is exactly what you want from him, quick and cutting, while Doja handles the hook with her usual casual precision. Planet Her-era confidence in full effect.

posts.title: Been Like This
posts.writeup: Been Like This is Doja at her most relaxed, a low-key groove that doesn't need to prove anything. The production breathes, the vocal floats, and the whole thing lands without announcement. Underrated cut from that run.

posts.title: hey! - Spotify Singles
posts.writeup: Unusual Demont has a voice that carries old soul instincts into a modern R&B frame, and this Spotify Singles session strips the production back enough to let it show. Hey is warm, unhurried, and well-delivered. A good entry point if you haven't tracked him down yet.

posts.title: BYE
posts.writeup: BYE finds Jaden in his singer-songwriter mode, the production spare and the emotion close to the surface. There's a looseness to the performance that feels deliberate, like he recorded it before second-guessing himself. That quality makes it stick.

posts.title: dancing in the kitchen
posts.writeup: LANY have a formula and they know it works, dancing in the kitchen leaning into the nostalgic synth-pop warmth that made them. Paul Klein's vocal delivery is unhurried and sincere, the production never overshooting the emotion. We've been watching this band for years and the consistency is real.

posts.title: JUGGERNAUT (feat. Lil Uzi Vert & Pharrell Williams)
posts.artist: Tyler, The Creator, Lil Uzi Vert, Pharrell Williams
posts.writeup: JUGGERNAUT is the album opener and it sets the tone hard. Pharrell's production is alien and percussive, and both Tyler and Uzi come in locked. The three-way collaboration shouldn't feel this cohesive given how different their lanes are, but it works on every level. A mission statement.

posts.title: You Right
posts.writeup: The Weeknd collab landed exactly where it was supposed to, You Right is effortless and a little hazy, built for night drives. The production is warm and minimal, both vocalists playing it understated to maximum effect. One of the better pop collaborations of that year.

posts.title: Wanderlust
posts.writeup: Zekey keeps it warm and unhurried. The production breathes in this soft pocket of bedroom R&B, lightly strummed chords cushioning a vocal that sounds like it's floating somewhere between a text you shouldn't send and one you already did. Melodic, understated, and sitting right in that sweet spot we keep coming back to.

posts.title: Bentayga Dust
posts.writeup: Skiifall moves at his own pace and it suits him. Bentayga Dust has that glazed-over midnight energy, 808s sitting low under a delivery that's part brag, part confession. The Canadian artist has been building quietly and this one sounds like a checkpoint, not a peak.

posts.title: Shot On You
posts.writeup: Quarry locks into a groove that doesn't let go. Shot On You rides a clean, stripped-back production with room for the vocal to stretch, and that space is exactly what makes it land. There's a confidence here that feels earned, not performed.

posts.title: beg
posts.writeup: LOONY keeps the alt-pop palette minimal and cuts straight through. Beg runs on a restless tension between the production's clean structure and a vocal that sounds like it's holding something back. She's got real control over the quiet moments, and those are the ones that stick.

posts.title: He Gets Me So High
posts.writeup: He Gets Me So High is beabadoobee doing what she does best: making guitar-forward indie-pop feel genuinely felt instead of formula. There's a classic shimmer to the production, traces of early-2000s bedroom rock, but her instincts keep it from ever going nostalgic for its own sake. Honest and immediate.

posts.title: District Love
posts.writeup: Shakota brings a measured, introspective energy to District Love. The production sits in a moody R&B pocket, low-lit and unhurried, letting the vocal carry the weight. Worth a listen if you've been keeping an ear on the quieter corners of independent soul.

posts.title: Obsessed
posts.writeup: LAUREL has been one of the most underrated names in UK pop for a while now, and Obsessed is a reminder of why. The production is clean with an edge, synth layers pushing against a vocal that knows exactly how much pressure to apply. Propulsive and direct.

posts.title: The Song With Feelings
posts.writeup: fijitrip works in a space that's equal parts ambient and melodic, and The Song With Feelings lands squarely in that zone. There's a textured softness to the production, plucked tones and gentle atmospherics framing a vocal that sounds quietly overwhelmed. Good emotional weight without overstating it.

posts.title: Pretend To Be Cool
posts.writeup: Pretend To Be Cool carries that classic fijitrip feeling of being too aware of your own awkwardness to pretend otherwise. The production floats on airy chords and a rhythm that never rushes, leaving room for the kind of self-conscious honesty that makes indie-pop worth listening to. Clean and wistful.

posts.title: I Love You (I Don't Want You To Know)
posts.writeup: One of fijitrip's more quietly devastating ones. I Love You (I Don't Want You To Know) does the thing that the best indie-pop does: it takes a messy, complicated feeling and makes it sound simple on purpose. Light production, heavy subtext, and a vocal that earns every second.

posts.title: The End (World On Fire)
posts.writeup: fijitrip closes out with a track that earns its dramatic title. The End (World On Fire) leans into a slightly bigger sonic frame, the production adding a sense of scale without abandoning the intimate quality that makes their work connect. We've been watching this project closely and this one lands.

posts.title: ANSWERS
posts.writeup: BERWYN doesn't flinch. Answers is raw in the way that actually costs something, his voice sitting unadorned over sparse production that makes every line feel like a direct statement. The South London artist has been putting out some of the most emotionally honest music around and this one belongs near the top of that list.

posts.title: MONEY COMES AND MONEY GOES
posts.writeup: Money Comes And Money Goes takes a subject that trips up a lot of artists and handles it with clarity. BERWYN's delivery is matter-of-fact in a way that reads as wisdom more than boast, and the production stays out of his way while still giving the track structure. Economical and real.

posts.title: Headshots (4r da Locals)
posts.writeup: Isaiah Rashad keeps the TDE catalog intact after years of silence, and Headshots 4r da Locals is exactly the kind of return you hope for. The Chattanooga MC's flow is as elastic as ever, trading between pockets in a way that sounds effortless but clearly isn't. Production is muted and warm, letting him run.

posts.title: How We Coming
posts.writeup: Culture III Migos in full stride. How We Coming runs the trap melodics they helped define, but the execution is sharp enough that it still lands. Offset's verse in particular feels locked in, and the production has that rolling, pressurized energy they've always done well.

posts.title: Patience (feat. Jorja Smith)
posts.writeup: Wesley Joseph brings a kind of understated grace to everything he touches, and Jorja Smith is a natural fit for that frequency. Patience is exactly what the title suggests: unhurried, layered, a slow-building thing that reveals more with each listen. The UK neo-soul scene doesn't get much better than this.

posts.title: On Your Side
posts.writeup: Wet have always operated in a particular emotional frequency, gauzy and bruised, and On Your Side keeps them squarely there. Kelly Zutrau's vocal sits over production that shimmers without ever fully resolving, which is kind of the point. If you've been following them since the early records, this one feels like home.

posts.title: Every Night
posts.writeup: Koko Love lands in that pocket of melodic R&B where the production does the heavy lifting and the vocal takes its time. Every Night has a patient, after-midnight quality to it, nothing rushed, everything settled into place. A name worth noting.

posts.title: Let's Leave This Place
posts.writeup: Tj Loft makes indie-pop that feels lived in rather than assembled. Let's Leave This Place rides a gentle forward momentum, production that's light without being weightless, and a vocal that sounds like someone making a real decision. Good instincts and a clean execution.

posts.title: Young & Dangerous
posts.writeup: Tanisako moves through Young & Dangerous with a cool confidence that suits the track's subject. The production keeps a low center of gravity, giving the vocal room to work, and the result is something that feels self-assured without trying too hard. One to watch.

posts.title: Antisocial (feat. Juice WRLD)
posts.writeup: Antisocial pairs two artists who both understood how to weaponize melodic delivery, and the combination works. Juice WRLD brings his signature emotional directness while Migos provide the trap architecture, and the track holds its own in both their catalogs. A good window into where both were at in this era.

posts.title: Modern Day
posts.writeup: Modern Day is Culture III as a mission statement. The production is clean and stacked, and the trio sounds focused in a way that the album's better moments sustain throughout. Offset leads with a verse that's among the project's strongest.

posts.title: Malibu (feat. Polo G)
posts.writeup: Polo G brings a different emotional register to the Culture III framework, and it opens the sound up in interesting ways. Malibu has a wistful, aspirational quality that the lighter production underscores, and both acts play to their strengths. A standout from the record.

posts.title: Type Shit (feat. Cardi B)
posts.writeup: Cardi B and Migos in the same room is familiar territory at this point, but Type Shit still delivers. The energy is high and mutual, production driving hard under two acts who know exactly how to match it. Uncomplicated and effective.

posts.title: be my guest
posts.writeup: Internet Girl works in that hazy, lo-fi adjacent space where the production is as much mood as music. Be My Guest has a slow, inviting warmth to it, chord progressions that feel slightly sun-blurred and a vocal that doesn't oversell anything. If you're already on this frequency, you know where this lands.

posts.title: Can't Read
posts.writeup: Lil Berete sounds unbothered on "Can't Read," riding a sparse trap instrumental with the kind of ease that comes from knowing your city is watching. The Toronto rapper's cadence is slippery and confident, shifting registers without losing the thread. 6ixbuzz amplifies the regional energy. This one's for the streets.

posts.title: war outside
posts.writeup: Kamal. opens with piano keys and a weight that earns your attention before the first verse lands. The production stays minimal, letting the emotional tension breathe rather than masking it. War outside, peace inside, the whole track operates on that friction. Keep an ear on this one.

posts.title: Ferris Wheel
posts.writeup: Jerry Folk teams up with Alvah and FENGSEL on something delicate and precise, built on layered acoustics and a vocal that never oversells the moment. The Norwegian producer has always understood restraint, and "Ferris Wheel" is a reminder of why that approach works. Quiet and patient in all the right ways.

posts.title: Black Hearted
posts.writeup: Polo G brings the same street-gospel tension he's built his catalog on, melancholy 808s underneath bars that feel more like testimony than rap. "Black Hearted" pulls from his Hall of Fame era when he was still sharpening his storytelling rather than coasting on it. The grief is specific and it hits.

posts.title: Just For Me (with SZA) - Space Jam: A New Legacy
posts.writeup: SAINt JHN and SZA make this soundtrack cut feel personal rather than corporate, which is no small feat given the Space Jam branding attached. SZA's voice does what it always does, threading warmth through the production and pulling focus. The chemistry between the two is easy and unhurried.

posts.title: Blouse
posts.writeup: Clairo dials back the bedroom-pop warmth of Immunity and arrives somewhere colder and more literary. "Blouse" moves slowly with piano and sparse percussion, the melody circling a discomfort that never gets overstated. It's a study in saying difficult things quietly. One of her best songs.

posts.title: Thot Shit
posts.writeup: Megan Thee Stallion turns in a percussion-forward Houston romp that doesn't bother with a slow build. The flow is blunt and precise, the subject matter unapologetic, and the energy is stadium-ready from bar one. Produced to hit through a phone speaker or a car trunk.

posts.title: Name
posts.writeup: "Name" is soft-spoken indie folk with enough production texture to keep it from feeling sparse. Boon builds the track around a simple premise and doesn't wander from it, which is the discipline a lot of artists in this lane miss. Clean and honest.

posts.title: Isn't It Romantic?
posts.writeup: BEL takes the classic Rodgers and Hart standard and strips it to near-nothing, just voice and a cushion of ambient production. The result is something wistful without being precious, a cover that feels like it belongs to the song rather than borrowing it. Understated and well-executed.

posts.title: SUPER8
posts.writeup: CANCEL CULTURE builds "SUPER8" around a dusty cinematic loop that feels straight out of a mid-90s crate dig, and Mick Jenkins arrives like he's been waiting for a beat this unhurried. Meltycanon adds production detail underneath Jenkins' measured cadence. The collaboration works because no one overplays their hand.

posts.title: good riddance, a new time
posts.writeup: Laica opens her EP on a slow exhale, piano-led and intimate without being precious. The title earns the feeling and the production stays out of the way. A careful introduction.

"20" captures that specific unease of early adulthood better than most, where the stakes feel enormous and undefined at the same time. Laica's vocal delivery is unhurried, the production kept to piano and breath. The simplicity is the point.

posts.title: helping hand
posts.writeup: Laica pulls something tender out of a straightforward arrangement, guitar and voice holding the whole thing together. The honesty in the writing is what makes it stick. No embellishment needed.

posts.title: losing feelings
posts.writeup: The production on "losing feelings" has a gentle drift to it, and Laica's vocal sits right at the center without competing with the space around it. The song understands distance better than it understands resolution, and that's the right call. Quiet and earned.

posts.title: i'm so fine at being lonely
posts.writeup: The title track closes the loop on what Laica's been building across the EP: a sound rooted in piano, restraint, and the kind of lyrics that don't try too hard. We've been watching this corner of indie folk get crowded, but Laica holds her own space.

posts.title: Dramamine
posts.writeup: Wallice comes out of Los Angeles with a dry wit and a guitar-forward sound that lands somewhere between Phoebe Bridgers and early Julien Baker, but with less ache and more eye-roll. "Dramamine" is sharply written, the melody as efficient as the lyricism. She's one to watch.

posts.title: Ode To Soulja Girl
posts.writeup: Payday and AG Club share a wavelength that's part lo-fi rap, part performance art, and "Ode To Soulja Girl" leans into the absurdity with full commitment. The production is deliberately cheap-sounding in a way that takes skill to pull off. Irreverent and self-aware.

posts.title: Eventually
posts.writeup: Chiiild operates in that slow-burn R&B pocket where nothing arrives on time and that's entirely the point. "Eventually" floats on a production bed of warm synths and a vocal that suggests patience has its own kind of urgency. Montreal-raised, with a sound that travels.

posts.title: Glowin Up
posts.writeup: TyFontaine's Ascension project hit with a sound that blended melodic trap and alternative rap before either lane had fully defined itself. "Glowin Up" captures the upward momentum of that era, production bright and deliberate, the flow sitting right in the pocket.

posts.title: Lost Cause
posts.writeup: Billie Eilish shot this visual as one long house party, and the song matches the mood: loose, slightly petty, and uninterested in being anything else. The production is minimal and bass-forward, her voice doing the actual heavy lifting. Clean and effective.

posts.title: Threats
posts.writeup: Olive Amun writes with a precision that makes the emotional weight land without a lot of buildup. "Threats" keeps the production sparse and lets the tension sit in the words. Understated in the way that sticks.

posts.title: Damage
posts.writeup: The production on "Damage" breathes more than it pushes, which gives Olive Amun room to let the lyrics carry the weight they're supposed to. Short and unhurried, which is the right call.

posts.title: Again
posts.writeup: "Again" closes the Don't Leave Yet EP with a sense of repetition that's structural as much as emotional. Olive Amun's writing stays honest without reaching for profundity, which is harder than it sounds. A strong close.

posts.title: Beeline
posts.writeup: whiterosemoxie and Tom The Mail Man link up on something propulsive and slightly chaotic, production punchy enough to feel urgent without losing the melodic core. The tag-team energy is real. Keep an ear on whiterosemoxie.

posts.title: LIKE IT FAST
posts.writeup: Colby Lafayette keeps things short and confident on "LIKE IT FAST," the production clipped and driving, the vocal direct. No wasted space. Does what it needs to do and gets out.

posts.title: Don't Forget About Me, Demos
posts.writeup: Less than a year before this post, Dominic Fike released five demos from a jail cell in South Florida. Between then and this release, a bidding war broke out, a multi-million dollar deal was signed, and the music industry collectively held its breath. The final versions of those demos arrived here, and the music justified every bit of the hype. Fike arrived fully formed.

posts.title: Sexual
posts.writeup:
NEIKED are a Swedish production duo who have been quietly building a reputation for radio-ready pop with genuine edge. "Sexual" is their breakthrough moment — a track built around a sample of Montell Jordan's "This Is How We Do It" that somehow feels completely fresh and completely inevitable at the same time.

Yung Baby Tate's feature is perfectly placed, her voice cutting right through the production at exactly the right moment. The whole thing is immaculate.

This is a hit. One of those tracks that sounds like it was always going to exist.

posts.title: Ocean Eyes
posts.writeup:
Time and time again, one track is all it takes for a career to be set. For Billie Eilish, that track was "Ocean Eyes." The 14-year-old from Los Angeles recorded it with her brother Finneas for a dance class, posted it to SoundCloud without much expectation, and watched the internet lose its mind.

There's a weightlessness to Billie's voice that is genuinely extraordinary — a clarity and emotional control that most singers spend decades trying to develop. "Ocean Eyes" is a perfect pop song built around almost nothing: her voice, some soft production, and a melody that stays with you for days.

She's 14. We'll be talking about Billie Eilish for a long time.

posts.title: An Introduction
posts.writeup:
Memphis-based rapper healy makes the kind of music that sounds like it was recorded in a room with the lights off and the windows open in the middle of summer. His debut tracks are built around an "urban surf" aesthetic — guitar-driven, melodic, with a vocal approach that drifts between rapping and singing with effortless ease.

What immediately stands out about healy is how fully-formed his sound is for someone releasing their first music. The production is lush and layered, the songwriting is emotionally intelligent, and his voice carries a natural charisma that's impossible to manufacture.

Memphis has been producing quietly innovative music for years. healy feels like the next chapter of that story.

posts.title: An Introduction
posts.writeup:
Chelsea Cutler is an 18-year-old from Westport, Connecticut who has been quietly building a following on SoundCloud through a series of stunning cover versions. Her covers of artists like Hozier and Fleetwood Mac have earned her tens of thousands of plays, but it's her original songwriting that really signals where she's headed.

Her debut original material showcases a voice and a songwriting sensibility that feels genuinely distinct — a New England introspection wrapped in bedroom pop production. There's an emotional clarity to her writing that belies her age, a willingness to sit with uncomfortable feelings and make something beautiful out of them.

This is just the beginning for Chelsea Cutler. Keep this one close.

posts.title: Eems to Slide
posts.writeup:
We assume brother/sister combo Tom and Larua Misch didn't fight each other growing up because it looks like they decided to make beautiful music together instead. Much can't be the said for the rest of us and our siblings, but that's ok because Tom and Laura are here to compensate for everyone else.

To be honest we are not very familiar with this duo but what we can tell you is that Tom is the singer/guitar/producer & Laura is the saxaphone player. Judging from their individual Soundcloud pages, this is the first time they have released something together on the same song publically. Lets just hope that happens again because this track is beautiful. Sure the ending of the song gets a bit repetitive, but that still doesn't take away from how amazing the beginning of this song is.

Jazz, soul, contemporary and even hip-hop come into play here, so hit play and vibe out to this one.

posts.title: Room 93
posts.writeup:
Halsey is a 20-year-old from New Jersey who has been building a devoted following on Tumblr and SoundCloud for the better part of a year. Her debut EP Room 93 is the kind of debut that makes you feel like you're witnessing the beginning of something important.

There's a theatricality to Halsey's music that sets her apart — a willingness to go big emotionally that most artists take years to develop. Her voice is distinctive, her songwriting is sharp, and she has an instinct for melody that feels genuinely rare.

Room 93 is four songs. Every one of them is worth your time.

posts.title: Walk Away
posts.writeup:
There's something different going on with LANY. The LA-based trio dropped "Walk Away" earlier this year and we've been sitting with it ever since, trying to figure out why it won't leave us alone.

It's the production — clean, spacious, impossibly polished for a band this early in their career. And Paul's voice sits right in the pocket of it, effortless and warm. We've seem them compared to a lot of things but nothing quite fits. They just sound like LANY.

We have a feeling we'll be saying that a lot over the next few years.

posts.title: Birds of Paradise
posts.writeup: Toronto-based creative collective IXXI powers another incredible project from their gifted singer/songwriter, Daniel Caesar. Birds of Paradise is a two-sided EP complete with two tracks and one bonus track titled "Scream", an endearing cover of James Blake that first caught our attention a few weeks back. Both Side A ("Medulla Oblongata") and Side B ("Birds of Paradise") showcase the brilliant voice of Caesar atop minimal guitars and piano. The calming sincerity of each chord decelerates any heartbeat. Both records are produced by Jordan Evans, a multi-platinum, Grammy and Juno nominated producer, alongside Toronto's Matthew Burnett, whose resume includes co-production for Drake, Nas, Kelly Rowland, and Childish Gambino. This was day one for Daniel Caesar. Most people found out years later.

posts.title: Scream (James Blake Cover)
posts.writeup:
Daniel Caesar is a 19-year-old from Toronto who makes soul music that sounds like it was recorded in a cathedral at 3 AM. His debut EP is a quiet, devastating piece of work that rewards patience — there's nothing here that announces itself, everything earns its place.

His voice is the kind that makes you stop what you're doing. Rich, unhurried, and completely in control. The production frames it perfectly — spare, spacious, letting the emotion do the work.

Remember this name. Daniel Caesar is going to matter.

posts.title: Monday EP
posts.writeup:
Although at times we get overwhelmed with all the new artists popping up, there's also a part of us that absolutely loves it. Not only does it keep us on our toes and motivated, but it also never allows us to stop loving the constant search for new music.

Causing us to get a tad bit excited today is Holly Lapsley (Lapsley), the 17-year-old singer from Liverpool who builds her beautiful sounds using mainly downtempo production that centers around influences of house, garage & bass. The calm production she interacts with allows her unique timbre to shine through, giving us an overwhelming sense of warmth. Her music is much more than just a digital recording, though; each song is a piece of art evoking visualizations of something magical and unknown.

Her Monday EP came out just over a month ago, but as you will see on her SoundCloud page, this is not her first batch of music. However, to start, we suggest you run through the EP below (+ the remix & loosies), and from there we guarantee you will be hooked.

posts.title: Can't Afford It All (Kygo Remix)
posts.writeup:
We've been paying close attention to the Norwegian producer Kygo for a few months now, and each new release just confirms what we already suspected — this kid is going to be massive.

His remix of Jakubi's "Can't Afford It All" is the track that really put him on our radar. There's a warmth and emotional depth to his production that's rare in electronic music — he builds tracks that feel like memories, hazy and beautiful and just out of reach. The remix takes Jakubi's already strong original and transforms it into something genuinely transcendent.

Kygo is 21 years old and based in Bergen, Norway. He's been releasing music for less than a year. Pay attention.

posts.title: Stolen Dance
posts.writeup:
Milky Chance are a duo from Kassel, Germany who have built something genuinely remarkable with "Stolen Dance." The track is a slow-burning piece of indie folk built around a warm acoustic guitar loop and Clemens Rehbein's distinctive drawl — unhurried, deeply melodic, and utterly impossible to shake.

The song has been spreading quietly through blogs and playlists for the past few weeks, and it's easy to understand why. There's a timelessness to it that most artists spend careers chasing. It sounds like it could have been recorded anytime in the last 40 years.

Milky Chance are going to be a very big deal. Mark it down.

posts.title: So High
posts.writeup:
Doja Cat is a 17-year-old from Los Angeles who sounds like she's been making music her entire life. "So High" is built around an Evil Needle beat — that hazy, Macy Gray-esque vocal delivery floating over production that feels like a Sunday morning you don't want to end.

She's been releasing music quietly on SoundCloud, building a small but devoted following. The confidence in her voice is striking for someone her age — no hesitation, no over-explaining, just pure feeling.

Doja Cat is one to watch. This is only the beginning.

posts.title: Don't Need U
posts.writeup:
One listen to "Don't Need U" is all you're going to need to immediately fall in love with Astronomyy. Based in Worcestershire, UK, this unknown for now, singer/instrumentalist is about to become a major factor all on one song.

Through his drenched in deep-space guitar chords, trap-esque drums and picture-perfect vocals, Astronomyy impresses immensely on "Don't Need U".

Hit up his social networks and get familiar with this guy asap, because big things are ahead if he keeps this up.

posts.title: The Love Club EP
posts.writeup:
We've been running this blog for six years, which means we've had our fair share of exciting discoveries. Very few moments though have us as excited as we are right now about Lorde, a 16-year-old from Auckland, New Zealand who has been quietly building an incredible catalogue of music on SoundCloud for the past year. Her debut EP, The Love Club, which was released last month, is a stunning piece of work that defies her age.

There's a classic quality to Lorde's music that draws comparisons to the likes Santigold and Lykke Li, yet her sound is wholly her own — a dark, brooding pop aesthetic built around minimal production and a voice that sounds like it's been crawling and scratching its way out of her for years. The Love Club is one of those records that rewards repeated listens, revealing new layers each time through.

The Love Club EP is available for free download on her SoundCloud page.

posts.title: Let Her Go
posts.writeup: From deep grooves to soaring melodies, the German producer, Peer Kusiv, really takes you for a spin with his deep electro-house remix of The Passenger song "Let Her Go".  Make sure you let this one play out until the sample drops in because you won't regret it.  Hear the greatness below.

posts.title: Walking on a Dream (Robotaki Unfinished Remix)
posts.writeup: Bet you didn't think you were gonna hear any new remixes of this tune, eh? Me either, and despite how undeniably catchy the original tune is, I don't think anyone was really yearning for any more remixes of it either. But this is too good to ignore, and it's not strictly new either. This remix has apparently been sitting unfinished in Robotaki's vault for years, not deemed ready by his extremely high standards to be heard by the public yet. For whatever reason Robotaki never ended up finishing it so he just recently decided to expose his "work in progress" remix of Empire of the Sun's Walking on a Dream on his Soundcloud for streaming. Sounds pretty damn polished to me.

posts.title: Running to the Sea" ft. Susanne Sundfør
posts.writeup: In Norway today there was a record amount of people who went into cardiac arrest.  Investigators say that 100% of the patients were all listening to this song when it happened.  Apparently they all say it was the fault of Susanne Sundfør's voice.  While we may never know the truth behind that report, we can however get some empirical evidence for ourselves by pressing play below.  Because you only live once right?  "Running to the Sea" is Röyksopp's brand new song that was just released today, grab it HERE.

posts.title: Let Us Move On ft. Kendrick Lamar
posts.writeup: While we all most likely remember Dido for her collaboration with Eminem on "Stan", it seems we may have another reason to put her back in our into minds due to this very engaging new single, "Let Us Move On" that features a great verse from Kendrick. Look for her new album to drop in March.

posts.title: Smalltalk" (Four Tet Remix)
posts.writeup: The Four Tet Remix of "Smalltalk" wastes no time in getting your attention by placing an emphatic emphasis on the vocals of singer, Laura Bettinson, right off the bat.   By switching to a sparse lead up instead of the original that was full of afrobeat-electronica, Four Tet, gives "Smalltalk" a completely new angle. Your attention may waver as this song goes along but at least it was worth the listen.

posts.title: So Bad (Synkro Dub Mix)
posts.writeup: Robots Don't Sleep is a collaboration between Robot Koch, a renowned progressive space   beatmaker by trade, and John LaMonica, an American singer/songwriter. The result is an out of this world sound that is self-described by them as "Like Motown With Lasers". Synkro and Mr Koch sample John's croonings to perfection by creating a beautiful futuristic soundscape that you could dance, make love, or relax to. BBC described it perfectly saying “Sounds like artificial intelligence discovering religion. Essential listening!” So good.

posts.title: Out Of The Fold (Silent Rider Remix)
posts.writeup: Silent Rider brilliantly escapades into the ether of all things amazing with this drool worthy remix of the Vaticans track,  "Out Of The Fold".  Armed with pitched down vocal samples, dreamy guitar chords & percolating percussion; this track is very mesmerizing so beware of actually drooling on yourself.  Hit up the Soundcloud logo below to grab this one for free.

posts.title: The Bait (Michael Creange & WEKEED remix)
posts.writeup: There are not enough remixes of Electric Guest out there, so the French producers Michael Creange & WEKEED have decided to do something about that.  Check out their re-work of "The Bait" below.

posts.title: Happiness
posts.writeup: Connected was easily one of the best hip-hop albums of the last decade.  By 2004 we all knew about Phonte and Little Brother, but it was the collaboration of Phonte and the Dutch beatmaker, Nicolay on Connected that really blew everyone away.  This album is absolutely essential.   Hear some of the highlights below.

posts.title: Gold Dayzz" (Maribou State Remix)
posts.writeup: The London based production duo, Maribou State, have shaken up the Ultraísta track, "Gold Dayzz", with fuzzed out electronica interjected by dizzing elements of soul and outer space.  Hear it below.  Grab Ultraísta's debut album on itunes if you like what you hear.

posts.title: Lighters Up ft. Mavado and Popcaan
posts.writeup: When we first heard that Snoop Dogg was going under the new moniker "Snoop Lion", the internets were definitely snickering at that.  Then when the news broke that Major Lazer would be teaming up under the Snoop Lion moniker to provide the production, the snickering started to subside.  Today their new single, "Lighters Up", will stop the talk about the name change and start the talk of anticipation for the Snoop Lion debut, Reincarnated, that will drop on Dec 18th.  Hear "Lighters Up" below.

posts.title: Your Girl (Louie Louie Remix)
posts.writeup: The Paris based house label Healthymotto is home to the works of the talented producer, Louie Louie, who shows us what he's all about with this hypnotic remix of "Your Girl" from Brighton UK's Tourist.  Check it out below.

posts.title: Border Crosser (The Seshen Remix)
posts.writeup: Entering 2013 without some extensive remix work on one of 2012's breakout bands would just be unacceptable. So in keeping up with all things common sense, TRAILS AND WAYS, will be releasing a new remix EP in early 2013.  The first release from this EP is the remix from fellow town mate, The Seshen, who takes his own spin on "Border Crosser".  Click on the Soundcloud logo below to be taken to download this for free. All you need to do is become a fan of T&W and you're set.

posts.title: Send The Pain On
posts.writeup: It's evident that the rise of Chrome Sparks is going to be a quick and easy one if he keeps seeping out beautiful tracks like this one.  "Send The Pain" is the latest epic tale from the Ann Arbour native who clearly escapes through his whimsical music.  Listen up.

posts.title: Up Up And Away
posts.writeup: It's pretty clear that Esta is going for the slow build up on "Good News" but what you aren't aware of at the beginning is how eventually this one warps into a shocking hip-hop banger.   Toss this song in your parents dinner playlist and see what happens.  Esta is a San Diego based beatmaker who just released his free mixtape, Bananas!.  Grab it for FREE HERE.  Check out the highlight below.

posts.title: Protovision
posts.writeup: First the Parisian wonder wowed everyone with "Nightcall" and then proceeded with the stunning job he did on the Drive Soundtrack.  Now he is back to do it all over again with his new piece, "Protovision", that is lifted from his new EP, Outrun. "Protovision" rings out with devilish power chords and haunting synth pulses — this is no easy listening experience so don't expect to relax to this one. If you live in France, you can grab this one on itunes, if you don't live in France, then you're going to have to settle with the stream below.

posts.title: Modern Magic
posts.writeup: Keeping things normal is clearly not apart of the plan for the London based producer, Brassica, who sets a frantic grooving pace of electrifying sounds on, "Modern Magic".  This track is taken Brassica's new EP, Temple Fortune, that dropped last month.  Hear the electro madness below.  Purchase on itunes (link above)

posts.title: Gold
posts.writeup: For being nestled up in what looks like a very cold place in Northern England (Lancaster, UK), the two young producers known as Bondax have provided us with some very warm and melodic music.  "Gold" is their latest single that was just released 2 days ago.  Get familiar below.

posts.title: Hit Me
posts.writeup: After some years of back luck with the law, Mystikal is back, but not in the conventional Cash Money 'bling bling' kind of way — nope.  For this comeback, Mystikal is channeling his inner James Brown to come out of the gates with a brand new dedication track to the Soul Brother #1.  "Hit Me" goes from singing, to rambling and rapping; it's a very entertaining song that is going to give you more than a few smirks.

posts.title: So Good To Me
posts.writeup: There is no better way to end your night off than taking in some soothing sounds from the NYC based electronic musician, Chris Malinchak.  If this is your first listen into the realm of Chris Malinchak prepare to have your kundalini rustled because this stuff is incredible.  Expect to see more of him on HD moving forward.  S/O to Caleb for the heads up.

posts.title: Heartbreak ft. Chela
posts.writeup: While Clubfeet are currently dominating the airwaves in Australia it's only a matter of time before this song hits here and does the same.  "Heartbreak" busts through with pop laden hooks that float over great guitar tones & fluttering synths.  Grab the single on itunes if you dig.  Look our for their new album, ‘Heirs & Graces’, that will be released in Aus in Jan 18th.

posts.title: 10,000 (prod. by 14KT)
posts.artist: Jamall Bufford (Buff1 of Athletic Mic League)
posts.writeup: There is no rapper exaggeration going on here as Jamall Bufford keeps it hella honest with himself as he raps about his rough estimate of having 10,000 fans across the world. While this is incredibly modest, I think its probably more than that if you count all the AML fans.  Jamall Bufford is fka Buff1 who was one of lead rappers on the Ann Arbour based hip-hop conglomerate, Athletic Mic League.  AML has birthed the likes of Mayer Hawthorne (fka DJ Haircut) and 14KT.  "10,000" was just released yesterday and features the stellar production of the 2008 Red Bull Big Tune Beat Battle champion 14KT.  Download below.

posts.title: Cot Damn ft. Ab-Liva & Roscoe
posts.writeup: Escaping the early 2000's without having this song stuck in your head for at least a month was damn near impossible because during the Summer/Fall of 2002, this track was alll over the place.  Taking a re-visit to a classic track like this one is always a great feeling.  If you forgot about this one, re-up your nostalgia of 2002 and hop back into your memories of 10 years ago right now.  COT DAMN.

posts.title: Sweet Nothing ft. Florence Welch (diplo & Grandtheft mix)
posts.writeup: Diplo & Grandtheft join forces to double up the creativity by turning this already massive single into a trap anthem. This song premiered on the BBC radio a few weeks ago and has been floating around as a radio rip ever since.  However in light of Thanksgiving (in the US) Diplo let it loose for free earlier today.  Unfortunately the DL link has since gone down, so you will have to resort to streaming only.

posts.title: Oddisee w/ Olivier DaySoul & Animal Nation @ The Electric Owl (Nov 10th, 2012)
posts.artist: Oddisee w/ Olivier DaySoul & Animal Nation @ The Electric Owl (Nov 10th, 2012)

posts.title: CunninLynguists w/ Tonedeff & Sadistik @ Fortune Sound Club (Nov 7th, 2012)
posts.artist: CunninLynguists w/ Tonedeff & Sadistik @ Fortune Sound Club (Nov 7th, 2012)
posts.writeup: *pictures courtesy of Hip Hop Vancouver.

posts.title: Illstate Massive ft Akrobatik
posts.writeup: It's been a good month or 2 since our last throwback post so I apologize for not getting these up! It's been a crazy last few months booking and producing shows so time has been limited. Anyways lets get right back into the swing of things with this certified underground classic from the Chicago based hip-hop duo who struck gold with their 2003 hit "Illstate Massive" that featured Akrobatik.  The vocal chops on this beat are legendary.  Listen below.

posts.title: Brother Ali w/ Homeboy Sandman & The Reminders @ VENUE (Oct 19th, 2012)
posts.artist: Brother Ali w/ Homeboy Sandman & The Reminders @ VENUE (Oct 19th, 2012)
posts.writeup: *photos courtesy of Hip-Hop Vancouver.

posts.title: Your Drums, Your Love
posts.writeup: "Your Drums, Your Love" is the kind of song that can be remixed by a multiple amount of talented producers and never get old.  The latest remix of "Your Drums, Your Love" sees the Toronto-based producer deebs methodically slow this one down.  The beat drop at 1:28 is the highlight on this one. Check it out below.

posts.title: Utomlands" ft. Peter Morén
posts.writeup: Swedish translation please? It's really weird listening to a song that is not in your native tongue because the visual aspect that is created through words is lost. For us non Swedish speaking people it forces us to pin-point our attention on the musical aspect of the song and the 'sound' of the artists voices, rather than what they are saying. For this track we will like the beat and hearing Peter Morén of Peter Bjorn & John on the hook.  Zacke is a Swedish rapper on the Bad Taste Records label that is also home to Chords.  "Utomlands" is the first single from his new album that will be released soon.  Listen to the track below and if you are in Sweden grab the single on itunes.

posts.title: Help Me
posts.writeup: The Sweet Serenades just might have a surprise hit album on their hands with their sophomore album, HELP ME!.  This Swedish rough and rugged duo juxtapose their mean looking demeanour with their whimsical guitar tones, catchy songwriting and pop-sensibilities.  HELP ME! will hit digital outlets on Sept 12th. For now check out two great songs from this album below.

posts.title: Gasoline
posts.writeup: ALPINE is a six-piece indie-pop outfit from Melbourne that's set to make their mark on the music world with their debut album, A Is for Alpine, on August 10. The album has already been selected as Triple J's album of the week, so you know with that co-sign comes a good promise, and now with "Gasoline", the first single from the album that was actually released back in June, we have actual proof that it's going to be good. Listen to the track below.

posts.title: The Bar w/ Brothers From Another @ The Electric Owl (July 20th, 2012)
posts.artist: The Bar w/ Brothers From Another @ The Electric Owl (July 20th, 2012)
posts.writeup: *photos courtesy of Taylor Walsh

posts.title: Racing Stripes
posts.writeup: Aesop Rock's Rhymesayers debut is now available for purchase on itunes. If you are going to buy this record regardless please ignore this post and just go buy the album.  For the chance you are not familiar with Aesop Rock, check out my favorite track from the album, "Racing Stripes", below.  I think it's a great introduction for what you can expect to hear on Skelethon.

posts.title: Keep It For Your Own
posts.writeup: POP ETC (formerly The Morning Benders) have released "Keep It For Your Own" as the lead single from their upcoming debut album under their new moniker. The album is set to drop June 12 and, if the funky upbeat feel of this debut is a sign of things to come, should be one of the top releases of the summer. Check out the track below.

posts.title: Tessellate
posts.writeup: alt-J (∆) is a 3 piece group from Leeds, UK who get their band name from the key stroke of alt + J (yes, on your keyboard - but only on a Mac). This super secret key combination makes the ∆ symbol, so go ahead and try it for yourself.  This triangle symbol is also used in mathematical equations to represent change and it is from this meaning that the band attaches itself to.  Now that we have figured out what their name is all about, we can get down to the important thing here, the music!  So go ahead and check out the teasers below from their debut album, An Awesome Wave  will be released on May 28th. Make sure you give these ones a few listens over if you are unsure about them after one listen.  Pre-order HERE

posts.title: Starring Role
posts.writeup: "Starring Role" is taken from the forthcoming sophomore album from the singer-songwriter Marina and The Diamonds, Electra Heart. Look for the album to be released on April 30th. You can pre-order the album HERE. Take a listen to the strong track below and get lost within the conviction of her voice.

posts.title: Sol & Grynch w/ Elekwent Folk @ The Electric Owl (March 9th, 2012)
posts.artist: Sol & Grynch w/ Elekwent Folk @ The Electric Owl (March 9th, 2012)
posts.writeup: The #SOLDOUT Sol & Grynch with Elekwent Folk show was on March 9th, 2012 at The Electric Owl in Vancouver, BC.  Photo's courtesy of Taylor Walsh.  If you are wondering why there are no pictures of Elekwent Folk and solo Grynch, it's because our photographer was a bit late.  We ain't mad though!

posts.title: Ms. Meteor ft. Steve Knight
posts.writeup: Therapy at 3 is a free flowing musical expression from 2 incredibly talented artists (Eligh + Amplive) who display an innate ability to create music that will be impossible NOT to appreciate. AmpLive and Eligh have joined forces through a mixture of one part 'outer space' beats and one part vocals from an abyss of euphoria. Therapy at 3 is exactly what good hip-hop is. This album will be used as a medical remedy to fix all the disappointment and pollution on the right side of your brain with a refreshing blast of musical greatness. Expect to see Therapy at 3 on top 10 lists for 2011. Grab this album in itunes right now.

posts.title: New Age
posts.writeup: Marlon Roudette is the former vocalist from Mattafix and this is his solo debut without the production of his other Mattafix half (Preteesh Hirji). For fans of Mattafix the initial concern was how would the production differ, in the good or bad sense, from the amazing production that Preteesh brought to the table on Mattafix's first two albums. My initial reaction is that the production does not stay on par with Preteesh's, it does however hold its own and allows Marlon to continue to make good music. Matter Fixed comes across as a more 'grown' album with piano chord progressions that we find on the lead single "New Age" that sound like a film score from Legends Of The Fall. "The Loss" is another cool new sound for Marlon with that rustic reverb added to his vocals that reminds me of Edward Sharpe. That "New Age" song is the #1 single currently in Germany right now. So yea the guy is big in Germany. Purchase on itunes above.

posts.title: Blue Scholars w/ Bambu & Sol @ FiveSixty (Sept 14th, 2011)
posts.artist: Blue Scholars w/ Bambu & Sol @ FiveSixty (Sept 14th, 2011)

posts.title: Macklemore & Ryan Lewis w/ The Physics @ FiveSixty [Sept 10/11]
posts.artist: Macklemore & Ryan Lewis w/ The Physics @ FiveSixty [Sept 10/11]
posts.writeup: [portfolio_slideshow]

posts.title: Viices
posts.writeup: Made In Heights is the 'mythical filth pop' duo of DJ/Producer Sabzi of Blue Scholars and vocalist Kelsey Bulkin. They blew us away back in December when they released their debut EP Winter Pigeons (Songs To Raise Your Dead Spirits). APORIA- IN THESE §TREETS is the follow up EP that features 6 new songs + the instrumentals from each. Sabzi takes on a whole other persona when he produces for Made In Heights. It seems like he is having too much fun with these beats. The result is a roller coaster of percussion and synths that dips and dives between the seductive vocals of Kelsey. Listen to my favorite track below then proceed to purchase this for whatever amount you choose. Purchase this from Bandcamp.

posts.title: Smack (Headnodic Remix)
posts.writeup: Headnodic is the producer responsible for the production for the Crown City Rockers, The Mighty Underdogs, Megaphone + many more. Nodrospective is a free compilation of his work over the past few years. Check out some dope remixes from him below.

posts.title: Claimin Respect (Original)
posts.writeup: Today's throwback features a gem from 2004 off of the rare Boulevard Connection 12inch single. The Boulevard Connection was the three headed lyrical beast of Edo. G, Masta Ace and Common. This was the only time these 3 emcees ever got together. There were hopes of an album but this was all we got. Masta Ace's verse is so legendary its ridiculous. If you knew about this song back then, you probably know all the words to the verse because its THAT good. The original and remix are both produced by Contaxt with cuts from DJ Noize. To find this vinyl single you're going to have to do some digging because it's out of stock everywhere. For fans of Cunninlynguists, RJD2, Pack FM.

posts.title: You Make The Music
posts.writeup: Lightheaded members Othello and Theory Hazit have always been slept on and its tracks like "You Make The Music" that really prove them worthy of being known to more ears. This EP is a digital only release and features production from 14kt, Mayer Hawthorne and Hir-O. Purchase the EP above.

posts.title: The Future
posts.writeup: Emcee John Robinson and producer Robot Koch have joined forces to give us this EP that was released on March 4th.  "The Future" is a great example of what happens when these two get together.  You can grab this EP from itunes (above) or from Bandcamp HERE.

posts.title: And Stars, Ringed
posts.writeup: Seattle's Blue Sky Black Death are the very talented production duo who create a multifaceted organic melting pot of sounds that form into cinematic dream worlds.  BSBD's beautiful compositions emit a field of emotion that elevates your mind state to a point of pure elation. NOIR is an work of art that should be used with meditation.  Over repeat listens of NOIR you will still feel like you are hearing it for the first time.  It truly is remarkable and is an absolute must have album.  Please take a listen to some of the highlights from this album below.  My favorite track from this album was "Gold In, Gold Out" and you can stream that via vimeo HERE.  NOIR drops on April 26th but you can Pre-order NOIR HERE.

posts.title: Exile Vilify
posts.writeup: This new National song is from the new Portal 2 video game, they recorded the song specifically for the game. Luckily this song is not just featured on the game because we can buy it from itunes (use the link above to buy it). Peep the song below and relax to another wonderful song from The National. *that chord progression on the piano is beautiful*. For fans of Arcade Fire, Interpol, Joy Division.

posts.title: High For This
posts.writeup: The Weeknd is 20 year old singer-songwriter, Abel Tesfaye from Toronto, Canada who released this free mixtape on March 21st.  If it has been a while since you have last heard good rnb then its time to re-up yourself with some fresh new music from an artist who brings a progressive style to the genre.  The production on House of Ballons is a refreshing accompaniment to Abel’s vocals.  You will find a healthy diversity in the production with synth lines, melodic bass pads, dub-step bass lines, guitar riffs and heavy amounts of low end.Grab this for FREE HERE.

posts.title: Good Woman
posts.writeup: Finnish rapper Redrama has been an integral part of Scandinavian hip-hop ever since he bursted onto the scene in 2003 with his debut album, Everyday Soundtrack.  Along with Looptroop Rockers, Madcon and The Paperboys they created a circle of great hip-hop acts that made Scandinavia known for its hip-hop more than it's woman.  Make sure you check out the songs below and get acquainted with the Finnish rapper Redrama.  You can purchase his albums from the itunes link above.

posts.title: My Notes
posts.writeup: Slug and Ant have been gracing our ears for over a decade collectively as the indie hip-hop group Atmosphere and are now back again to pluck our heartstrings with some more good music for humans. Of course we can't leave out the new editions to the band since 2008 and that is Nate Collis on the guitar and Erick Anderson on the keys.  Since the release of their 2008 album, When Life Gives You Lemons, You Paint That Shit Gold, the sound of Atmosphere has gone the way of live instrumentation that gives them more than just a hip-hop vibe.  Atmosphere have always progressed in a good way and kept the music good.  So as with their always great live show the expectations are always there for their new studio albums.  We now embark on a the brand new full length that is being released today (April 12th). The Family Sign takes on subject matter from stories of family life, relationships, fatherhood, and the various emotions as a result of these things.  Slug has always been one of hip-hop's best story tellers and with his writing on, The Family Sign,  he just re-confirms that statement.  While there is no doubt that this album musically and lyrically is top notch, I just don't think it has much replay value.  This album definitely has a place for a great listen to match a mellow mood.  It's not the album you will be bumping in your car or have on steady replay but it will be that album you need to have in your collection.  Especially if you have been a fan of Atmosphere for years.  My favorite song on the album was actually the very last track and the sad thing it is the shortest!  Make sure to grab the album from the itunes link above!
